$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \cdot\text{OH} + \text{OH}^-$$

The hydroxyl radical (•OH) is a ferocious initiator of lipid peroxidation. By sequestering Fe²⁺ into a stable chelate ring, the catalyst is removed, and fewer radicals form. Hence, even though the chelator itself is a poor radical scavenger, it amplifies the effect of primary antioxidants (BHA, tocopherol) by **3 to 5-fold** — a phenomenon quantified by the **synergy factor (SF)**.

The chelate-ring formation for Fe²⁺–EDTA can be written:

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{EDTA}^{4-} \longrightarrow [\text{Fe(EDTA)}]^{2-} \quad K_f \approx 10^{14}$$

The very high **formation constant K<sub>f</sub>** indicates that the iron is effectively locked away and cannot catalyse oxidation. KTU examiners reward the mention of K<sub>f</sub> with extra marks.

---

### 📕 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1]`

**"The functional role of an additive depends on the molecular mechanism by which it interacts with the food matrix."**
**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **stabilization of an oil-in-water emulsion by lecithin**, highlighting the role of HLB. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A fruit juice at pH 4.0 is preserved with **potassium sorbate (pK<sub>a</sub> = 4.76)**. Calculate the percentage of sorbic acid in the active, undissociated form. Comment on whether sorbate will work effectively at this pH. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution:

**(a) Stabilization of O/W Emulsion by Lecithin — 7 marks:**

Lecithin is a **phospholipid** with a polar head (choline phosphate group) and two non-polar fatty-acid tails. When oil is dispersed in water under high-shear mixing (homogenizer), lecithin molecules rapidly **migrate to the oil-water interface**, with:
- the **hydrophilic head** remaining in the aqueous (water) phase,
- the **hydrophobic tails** embedded in the oil droplet.

This oriented monolayer:
1. **Lowers interfacial tension γ<sub>OW</sub>** from ~ 35 mN/m to < 10 mN/m, reducing the thermodynamic drive toward coalescence.
2. Forms a **steric + electrostatic barrier** around each droplet, preventing two droplets from merging.

**HLB relevance:** Lecithin has an HLB ≈ 4–7 (lipophilic). HLB below 10 → W/O emulsifier, but when combined with co-surfactants it is also used in O/W systems. The **Griffin equation** HLB = 20 × M<sub>h</sub>/M is a common follow-up question.

**Diagram (KTU board expects a sketch):**
```
  Water (continuous phase)
  ↓
  [ polar head ]  ← oil droplet
  [ hydrophobic tail ——————————— ]
  [ polar head ]  ← oil droplet
```

**(b) Calculation of Undissociated Sorbic Acid — 7 marks:**

Given: pH = 4.0, pK<sub>a</sub> = 4.76 (sorbic acid).

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$\alpha_{HA} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{\,\text{pH} - \text{pK}_a}}$$

$$\text{pH} - \text{pK}_a = 4.0 - 4.76 = -0.76$$

$$10^{-0.76} = 0.1738$$

$$\alpha_{HA} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1738} = \frac{1}{1.1738} = 0.8519$$

Therefore, the **undissociated sorbic acid fraction = 85.2 %**.

**Comment:** Because more than 50 % is in the active undissociated form, **potassium sorbate will be highly effective as a preservative in this juice**, efficiently inhibiting yeasts and moulds at the typical use level of 0.05–0.2 %. This is the reason FSSAI allows sorbate in fruit juices, squashes, and bakery products.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in this topic (Board Examiner, 12 years of valuation experience):**
> 1. **Writing only the name of an additive and not its mechanism** — partial credit only. Always state *what* the molecule does at the molecular level.
> 2. **Confusing INS number and E-number** — they are the SAME globally (e.g., E102 = INS 102). Mixing them up costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Using ADI without units** — ADI = "5" is wrong; ADI = "5 mg/kg bw/day" is correct.
> 4. **Hesitating on the Fenton reaction** — it is one of the most frequently tested equations. Memorize it.
> 5. **Forgetting pK<sub>a</sub> values** of common preservatives — Benzoic 4.21, Sorbic 4.76, Propionic 4.87.
> 6. **Confusing "stabilizer" with "emulsifier"** — both modify texture but through different mechanisms (viscosity vs interfacial activity).
> 7. **Skipping the regulatory body (FSSAI)** when listing permitted levels — examiners expect a regulatory reference.
> 8. **Neglecting to draw the diagram** in any 7-mark question worth ≥ 50 % of the answer — at least a labelled block diagram earns 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize before entering the exam hall):**

- ☐ **Definition of food additive** — FSSAI / Codex formula must be reproducible verbatim.
- ☐ **Three tests for approval** — Technological justification, Safety, Consumer acceptability.
- ☐ **ADI** = mg/kg body weight/day; **not** absolute dose.
- ☐ **INS = E-number**; globally unique per molecule.
- ☐ **12 functional classes** are examinable: preservatives, antioxidants, sequestrants, emulsifiers, stabilizers, leavening, anti-caking, colours, flavours, sweeteners, humectants, acids/buffers.
- ☐ **Preservatives work only as undissociated acid** — Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is the master formula.
- ☐ **pK<sub>a</sub> values to remember** — Benzoic 4.21, Sorbic 4.76, Propionic 4.87, Acetic 4.76.
- ☐ **HLB scale** — 0 (lipophilic) → 10 (hydrophilic); W/O < 10, O/W > 10.
- ☐ **Fenton reaction** — Fe²⁺ + H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> → Fe³⁺ + •OH + OH⁻ — basis of sequestrant use.
- ☐ **Primary vs secondary antioxidants** — primary = radical scavengers; secondary = chelators + O<sub>2</sub> scavengers.
- ☐ **Water activity** a<sub>w</sub> thresholds: bacteria 0.91, yeast 0.87, mould 0.80, xerophiles 0.60.
- ☐ **Synergy** of BHA + citric acid + EDTA is 3–5× — cite the synergy factor SF equation.
- ☐ **FSSAI** = mandatory in India; **Codex GSFA** = international reference; **JECFA** = ADI setter.
- ☐ **Diagram must accompany** any long answer on emulsion or chelation.
- ☐ **Examples must include INS number** for full marks (e.g., "lecithin E322", not just "lecithin").
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Deterioration - Deteriorative Factors and Their Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Food Deterioration - Deteriorative Factors and Their Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Deterioration (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> Food deterioration is defined as the **undesirable, irreversible change in the sensory, nutritional, physico-chemical, or microbiological quality** of a food product, rendering it unfit for human consumption or reducing its shelf-life. It is governed by the intrinsic properties of the food matrix and extrinsic environmental factors acting through biochemical, microbiological, and physical mechanisms.

In the framework of **Food Process Engineering**, deterioration is universally expressed as a function of four interacting pillars:

$$\text{Food Deterioration Rate} = f(\text{Food Matrix} \mid \text{Microorganisms} \mid \text{Environment} \mid \text{Time})$$

### 1.1.1 Classification of Food Deterioration (KTU Module 1 Taxonomy)

| Sl. No | Class of Deterioration | Primary Causative Agent | Canonical Example |
|:------:|:-----------------------|:-----------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | **Microbial Spoilage** | Bacteria, Yeasts, Molds | Putrefaction of fish, mold on bread |
| 2 | **Enzymatic Deterioration** | Native food enzymes (e.g., PPO, lipase) | Enzymatic browning of cut apple |
| 3 | **Chemical Deterioration** | Oxidation, non-enzymatic reactions | Rancidity in oils, Maillard browning |
| 4 | **Physical Deterioration** | Mechanical, thermal, freeze-burn | Wilting of salad, ice crystal damage |
| 5 | **Pest/Insect Damage** | Insects, rodents, mites | Weevil infestation in stored grains |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Keyword Trigger:** When answering in the university exam, always specify the **type** of deterioration before explaining the **cause**. Examiners in KTU strictly award the 1-mark for *classification* separately from the mechanism marks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Living Building" Analogy**
> Imagine a freshly built house (your food). The moment construction ends, three forces begin destroying it:
> 1. **Termites & Fungi** (microorganisms) — silently eating the wooden beams.
> 2. **Rain & Sunlight** (water activity and UV) — rusting the iron, bleaching the paint.
> 3. **The House's Own Plumbing** (intrinsic enzymes) — slowly leaking and damaging walls from inside.
> Food preservation is essentially *building a fortress* around the house: controlling humidity, sealing entry points, lowering temperature, and removing oxygen.

## 1.3 The Concept of Shelf-Life

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** Shelf-life is the period of time during which a food product remains safe and acceptable for consumption under defined storage conditions. It is mathematically the time required for a quality attribute (Q) to degrade from its initial value ($Q_0$) to an unacceptable end-point value ($Q_e$).

The fundamental shelf-life equation, foundational to Module 1, is:

$$t_s = \frac{\ln(Q_0 / Q_e)}{k}$$

Where:
- $t_s$ = Shelf-life (days, months)
- $Q_0$ = Initial quality index (e.g., vitamin content, color intensity, microbial count)
- $Q_e$ = End-point quality index (consumer rejection threshold)
- $k$ = Reaction rate constant (temperature-dependent)

## 1.4 The Concept of Water Activity ($a_w$) — The Single Most Critical Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Activity ($a_w$) Definition:**
> $$a_w = \frac{P}{P_0} = \frac{\text{Partial vapor pressure of water in food}}{\text{Vapor pressure of pure water at same temperature}}$$
> $a_w$ is the **available water** for microbial and chemical reactions. It is NOT total moisture content.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Think of a sponge. A *damp* sponge has high $a_w$ (water is free, can evaporate, microbes can use it). A *bone-dry* sponge has $a_w \approx 0$ (water is bound tightly, microbes cannot access it). Food preservation by drying, salting, or sugar addition works by *tying up* the free water, lowering $a_w$.

### 1.4.1 Critical Water Activity Thresholds for Microbial Growth

| Microbial Group | Minimum $a_w$ for Growth | Typical Foods |
|:----------------|:------------------------|:--------------|
| Most bacteria | **0.91** | Fresh meat, milk, bread |
| Most yeasts | **0.88** | Syrups, fruit juices |
| Most molds | **0.80** | Aged cheese, dry-cured meat |
| Halophilic bacteria | 0.75 | Salted fish |
| Xerophilic molds | 0.65 | Dried fruits, jams |
| Osmophilic yeasts | 0.60 | Honey, confectionery |
| No microbial growth | $< 0.60$ | Properly dried foods |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Microbial Deterioration — Mechanism

Microbial spoilage proceeds through the following logical chain:

1. **Contamination:** Microbes arrive on the food surface from air, soil, water, equipment, or handlers.
2. **Lag Phase:** Microbes adapt to the new substrate; no significant growth yet.
3. **Log Phase (Exponential Growth):** Microbes multiply geometrically as $N_t = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/d}$, where $d$ is the doubling time.
4. **Stationary Phase:** Nutrient depletion, waste accumulation; growth = death.
5. **Decline Phase:** Cell death dominates; enzymatic autolysis releases intracellular enzymes, accelerating spoilage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Microbial growth follows first-order kinetics in the log phase. The **doubling time ($d$)** is inversely related to specific growth rate $\mu$ as $d = \ln 2 / \mu$.

## 2.2 Chemical & Enzymatic Deterioration

### 2.2.1 Enzymatic Browning (Polyphenol Oxidase - PPO)

$$\text{Monophenol} \xrightarrow{\text{PPO}} \text{o-Diphenol} \xrightarrow{\text{PPO + O}_2} \text{o-Quinone} \rightarrow \text{Melanins (Brown Pigments)}$$

**Control:** Blanching (heat inactivates PPO), exclusion of O$_2$, use of **sulfiting agents** (SO$_2$), ascorbic acid, citric acid, or 4-hexylresorcinol.

### 2.2.2 Lipid Oxidation (Rancidity)

The classical autoxidation mechanism is the **free-radical chain reaction**:

$$\text{Initiation: } RH \xrightarrow{\text{heat/light/metal}} R\cdot + H\cdot$$
$$\text{Propagation: } R\cdot + O_2 \rightarrow ROO\cdot$$
$$ROO\cdot + RH \rightarrow ROOH + R\cdot$$
$$\text{Termination: } R\cdot + R\cdot \rightarrow RR$$
$$ROO\cdot + R\cdot \rightarrow ROOR$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering significance:** Lipid oxidation is the *primary* cause of quality loss in edible oils, fried snacks, nuts, and fat-rich meat products. The **Peroxide Value (PV)** and **Thiobarbituric Acid (TBA) value** are the standard industrial QC metrics.

### 2.2.3 Maillard Reaction (Non-enzymatic Browning)

$$\text{Reducing Sugar} + \text{Amino Acid/Protein} \rightarrow \text{Schiff Base} \rightarrow \text{Amadori Compound} \rightarrow \text{Melanoidins}$$

This reaction is **desirable** in baking, roasting, brewing; **undesirable** in dried milk, white sauces, and during storage.

## 2.3 Physical Deterioration

| Phenomenon | Mechanism | Control |
|:-----------|:----------|:--------|
| Moisture loss (shrinkage) | Vapor pressure gradient | High RH storage, MAP, edible coatings |
| Moisture gain (caking) | Hygroscopic pickup | Waterproof packaging, desiccants |
| Freezer burn | Sublimation of surface ice | Airtight packaging (vacuum, laminated film) |
| Crystallization (chocolate bloom) | Migration of cocoa butter | Tempering, storage at $18\text{ }^\circ\text{C}$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $a_w = P / P_0$ | Water activity definition | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\ln a_w = -M_1 \cdot v_1 / 55.51$ | Norrish/RAOUlt relationship for $a_w$ depression by solutes | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\ln(Q_0 / Q_e) = k \cdot t_s$ | Shelf-life (first-order quality loss) | days |
| 4 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius equation (temperature dependence) | s$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $N_t = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/d}$ | Microbial growth (geometric) | CFU/g |
| 6 | $\mu = (\ln N_t - \ln N_0) / t$ | Specific growth rate | h$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $D = T_{ref} \cdot \log(N_0 / N)$ | Decimal reduction time (sterilization) | min |
| 8 | $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$ | Sterilization value (thermal process) | min |
| 9 | $a_w(\text{product}) = a_w(\text{component}) \cdot X_i$ | Raoult's law for ideal mixtures | dimensionless |
| 10 | $PV = \frac{V_{Na_2S_2O_3} \cdot N \cdot 1000}{W}$ | Peroxide value (lipid oxidation) | meq O$_2$/kg |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL — Do not confuse** *water content* (%, g water/100 g food) with *water activity* ($a_w$, dimensionless). They are completely different physical quantities. Most KTU exam questions test this distinction.

## 2.5 Application in Food Engineering Industries

- **Dairy Industry:** Pasteurization (72 °C × 15 s) inactivates pathogens; refrigerated storage prevents psychrotrophic spoilage.
- **Bakery Industry:** Sorbic acid + reduced $a_w$ + MAP prevents mold spoilage of bread.
- **Meat Industry:** Vacuum packaging, nitrite curing, low-temperature chilling control both oxidation and *Clostridium* growth.
- **Oils & Fats:** Nitrogen blanketing, addition of TBHQ/BHA antioxidants, opaque packaging prevent photo-oxidation.
- **Fruits & Vegetables:** Cold chain, controlled atmosphere storage (CAS), and 1-MCP treatment delay ripening.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP MECHANISTIC DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Shelf-Life Using the Arrhenius Equation

This derivation is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU ESE for Module 1.

### 3.1.1 Starting Premise

The rate of most deteriorative reactions (oxidation, browning, microbial growth) follows **first-order kinetics**:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = -k \cdot Q$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Integration

$$\frac{dQ}{Q} = -k \, dt$$

Integrating between limits $Q_0$ (at $t=0$) and $Q_e$ (at $t = t_s$):

$$\int_{Q_0}^{Q_e} \frac{dQ}{Q} = -k \int_{0}^{t_s} dt$$

$$\ln Q_e - \ln Q_0 = -k \cdot t_s$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{Q_0}{Q_e}\right) = k \cdot t_s$$

Rearranging for shelf-life:

$$t_s = \frac{1}{k} \ln\left(\frac{Q_0}{Q_e}\right)$$

### 3.1.3 Introducing Temperature Dependence (Arrhenius)

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Therefore:

$$t_s = \frac{1}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}} \ln\left(\frac{Q_0}{Q_e}\right) = \frac{e^{E_a / RT}}{A} \ln\left(\frac{Q_0}{Q_e}\right)$$

### 3.1.4 The $Q_{10}$ Approximation (Industrial Shortcut)

For many food systems, a simple rule of thumb is used:

$$Q_{10} = \frac{k_{(T+10)}}{k_T} = \frac{t_{s,T}}{t_{s,(T+10)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** If $Q_{10} = 2$, the shelf-life **halves** for every 10 °C rise in temperature. A product lasting 100 days at 20 °C lasts only 50 days at 30 °C and 25 days at 40 °C.

## 3.2 Derivation of Water Activity Depression by Solutes (Raoult-Norrish Equation)

For an ideal solution with solute mole fraction $X_s$ and molecularly-bound water, the Raoult-Norrish equation is:

$$a_w = X_w = \exp\left(-K \cdot X_s^2\right)$$

Where $K$ is a constant ($\approx 6.7$ for sucrose). Step-by-step:

1. Start with Raoult's law: $a_w = P / P_0 = X_w$
2. Express $X_w$ in terms of $X_s$: $X_w = 1 - X_s$
3. For non-ideal behavior, modify using empirical constant $K$.
4. Final form: $a_w = \exp(-K \cdot X_s^2)$

**Application:** A jam with 65% soluble solids has $X_s \approx 0.65$, giving $a_w \approx 0.81$, which is below the mold-growth limit (0.80) — hence the microbiological stability of high-sugar jams.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Strategy vs. Deteriorative Factor

| Deteriorative Factor | Primary Control Strategy | Secondary Control | Limitation |
|:---------------------|:------------------------|:------------------|:-----------|
| **Microbes (bacteria)** | Thermal processing ($F_0$ value) | Refrigeration, acidulants | Heat-labile nutrients destroyed |
| **Microbes (mold)** | Reduced $a_w$ (< 0.70) | Sorbates, benzoates | Off-flavors at high doses |
| **Enzymatic browning** | Blanching (90 °C × 2 min) | SO$_2$, ascorbic acid | Residual sulfite health concerns |
| **Lipid oxidation** | Nitrogen flushing | BHA/BHT/TBHQ, opaque packaging | Antioxidant cost & regulation |
| **Maillard browning** | Low $a_w$ storage | Refrigeration | Cannot be reversed once formed |
| **Moisture loss** | Edible coatings, MAP | Humectants | Texture changes |
| **Insect infestation** | Hermetic storage, fumigation | Irradiation, cold | Insecticide residues |

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A packaged food product has an initial vitamin C content of 50 mg/100 g. The consumer rejection threshold is 20 mg/100 g. If the deterioration follows first-order kinetics with $k = 0.005$ day$^{-1}$ at 25 °C, calculate the shelf-life.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $Q_0 = 50$ mg/100 g
- $Q_e = 20$ mg/100 g
- $k = 0.005$ day$^{-1}$

Applying the shelf-life equation:

$$t_s = \frac{1}{k} \ln\left(\frac{Q_0}{Q_e}\right)$$

$$t_s = \frac{1}{0.005} \ln\left(\frac{50}{20}\right)$$

$$t_s = 200 \times \ln(2.5)$$

$$t_s = 200 \times 0.9163$$

$$\boxed{t_s \approx 183.3 \text{ days}}$$

**Temperature check using $Q_{10} = 2$:** If stored at 35 °C, $k_{35} = 2 \times k_{25} = 0.01$ day$^{-1}$, so $t_s \approx 91.6$ days.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Mermaid Diagram: The Four Pillars of Food Deterioration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FOOD DETERIORATION SYSTEM"] --> B["MICROBIAL SPOILAGE"]
    A --> C["CHEMICAL DETERIORATION"]
    A --> D["PHYSICAL DETERIORATION"]
    A --> E["BIOLOGICAL PESTS"]

    B --> B1["Bacteria: Putrefaction"]
    B --> B2["Yeasts: Fermentation Off-Flavor"]
    B --> B3["Molds: Visible Mycelium Toxins"]

    C --> C1["Enzymatic Browning PPO Pathway"]
    C --> C2["Lipid Oxidation Free Radical Chain"]
    C --> C3["Maillard Reaction Amino + Sugar"]
    C --> C4["Hydrolysis of Proteins and Lipids"]

    D --> D1["Moisture Loss Shrinkage"]
    D --> D2["Freezer Burn Ice Sublimation"]
    D --> D3["Texture Softening Cell Wall Breakdown"]
    D --> D4["Recrystallization Sugar and Fat Bloom"]

    E --> E1["Insects Weevils Beetles"]
    E --> E2["Rodents Rats Mice"]
    E --> E3["Mites Storage Mites"]

    B1 --> F1["CONTROL: Thermal Processing pH Reduction Refrigeration"]
    B2 --> F1
    B3 --> F1
    C1 --> F2["CONTROL: Blanching Oxygen Exclusion Ascorbic Acid"]
    C2 --> F3["CONTROL: Antioxidants Nitrogen Blanket Opaque Packaging"]
    C3 --> F4["CONTROL: Low aw Storage Refrigeration"]
    C4 --> F4
    D1 --> F5["CONTROL: Edible Coatings MAP Humectants"]
    D2 --> F5
    D3 --> F5
    D4 --> F5
    E1 --> F6["CONTROL: Hermetic Storage Irradiation Fumigation"]
    E2 --> F6
    E3 --> F6

    F1 --> OUT["PRESERVED SAFE FOOD"]
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT
    F4 --> OUT
    F5 --> OUT
    F6 --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: The Hurdle Concept (Leistner's Principle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTRINSIC["INTRINSIC HURDLES INSIDE FOOD"]
        A1["pH Low Acid"]
        A2["aw Low Water Activity"]
        A3["Redox Potential Eh Low Oxygen"]
        A4["Antimicrobials Natural"]
    end

    subgraph EXTRINSIC["EXTRINSIC HURDLES OUTSIDE FOOD"]
        B1["Temperature Cold or Hot"]
        B2["Atmosphere Vacuum or N2"]
        B3["Packaging Modified"]
        B4["Radiation UV or Gamma"]
    end

    subgraph MICROBE["TARGET MICROORGANISM"]
        C1["Bacterial Cell"]
        C2["Mold Spore"]
        C3["Yeast Cell"]
    end

    subgraph OUTCOME["OUTCOME"]
        D1["Multi Hurdle Synergy"]
        D2["Stable Safe Product"]
    end

    INTRINSIC --> MICROBE
    EXTRINSIC --> MICROBE
    MICROBE --> OUTCOME
    D1 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Selecting a Preservation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Identify Food Type and Spoilage Risk"] --> Q1{"Is pH below 4.5?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> ACID["Apply Acidic Preservation Fermentation Pickling"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Can Heat be Applied?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> HEAT["Thermal Processing Pasteurization Sterilization Canning"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Can Product be Dried?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> DRY["Dehydration Spray Drying Freeze Drying"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Is Refrigeration Feasible?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> COLD["Chill Chain at 0 to 4 C"]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{"Is High Pressure or Irradiation Allowed?"}
    Q5 -- Yes --> NON["Non Thermal Hurdle HPP Irradiation PEF"]
    Q5 -- No --> CHEM["Chemical Preservation Salt Sugar Nitrite Sorbate"]
    CHEM --> FINAL["Shelf Stable Product"]
    ACID --> FINAL
    HEAT --> FINAL
    DRY --> FINAL
    COLD --> FINAL
    NON --> FINAL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

### Q1. Differentiate between water content and water activity. Why is $a_w$ more important than moisture content in determining food stability? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Water Content | Water Activity ($a_w$) |
|:----------|:--------------|:----------------------|
| Definition | Total water in food (g/100 g) | Ratio of partial vapor pressure of water in food to that of pure water at same T |
| Symbol | % moisture or $M$ | $a_w$ |
| Range | 0 to 100% | 0 to 1.0 |
| Measures | Quantity of water | **Availability/energy** of water |
| Microbial control relevance | Indirect | **Direct** — limits growth precisely |

> **Conclusion:** Two foods may have the same moisture content (e.g., 20%) but very different $a_w$ values. Bound water in dried fruits (low $a_w$) is unavailable to microbes, while the same percentage of free water in fresh meat (high $a_w$) supports rapid spoilage. Hence, $a_w$ is the **true predictor** of microbial, enzymatic, and chemical stability.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

### Q2. List the major deteriorative changes that occur in food. Explain briefly the role of enzymes in food deterioration. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Major deteriorative changes:**
1. Microbial spoilage (bacterial, yeast, mold)
2. Enzymatic deterioration (browning, softening, off-flavors)
3. Chemical deterioration (oxidation, non-enzymatic browning)
4. Physical deterioration (moisture migration, texture loss)
5. Infestation by insects and pests

**Role of enzymes:**
- **PPO (Polyphenol Oxidase):** Causes browning in cut fruits and vegetables.
- **Lipase:** Hydrolyzes triglycerides to free fatty acids, causing hydrolytic rancidity.
- **Pectic enzymes (PME, PG):** Degrade pectin, causing softening of fruits and vegetables.
- **Proteases:** Break down proteins, causing texture loss in meat and dairy.
- These enzymes are **inactivated by blanching** (90 °C for 1–2 min).

---

## PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> ### Question A (Choice 1) **[14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the various **microbiological factors** responsible for food deterioration. Discuss in detail the **mechanism of microbial growth** and the **intrinsic and extrinsic parameters** that influence it. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Types of microorganisms causing food spoilage:**
- **Bacteria:** Most common; cause putrefaction in protein foods (meat, fish, eggs). Genera: *Pseudomonas, Clostridium, Bacillus, Salmonella, Listeria*.
- **Yeasts:** Cause fermentation, off-flavors, gas production in sugary foods. Genera: *Saccharomyces, Candida*.
- **Molds:** Grow on surfaces; produce visible mycelium and **mycotoxins** (e.g., aflatoxin from *Aspergillus flavus*).

**2. Mechanism of microbial growth (Growth Curve):**
- **Lag phase:** Adaptation, no division.
- **Log phase:** Exponential growth, $N_t = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/d}$.
- **Stationary phase:** Nutrient depletion, equilibrium.
- **Death phase:** Cell death dominant; autolysis releases enzymes.

**3. Intrinsic Parameters (in the food itself):**
- $a_w$, pH, redox potential ($E_h$), natural antimicrobials, nutrient content.

**4. Extrinsic Parameters (storage environment):**
- Temperature, relative humidity, oxygen, light, presence of other microbes.

**5. Implicit Parameters (processing-related):**
- Type and intensity of heat treatment, packaging atmosphere.

**Conclusion:** Effective preservation requires controlling at least one intrinsic and one extrinsic factor simultaneously (Hurdle Concept).

---

**(b)** Describe the **various physical and chemical methods** used for the control of food deterioration. Give suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**A. Physical Methods:**
1. **Temperature control** — refrigeration (0–4 °C), freezing (-18 °C), thermal processing (pasteurization/sterilization).
2. **Water activity reduction** — drying, spray drying, freeze drying, concentration.
3. **Irradiation** — γ-rays, e-beam, UV-C for surface sterilization.
4. **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** — replacement of O$_2$ with N$_2$/CO$_2$.
5. **High Pressure Processing (HPP)** — 400–600 MPa for microbial inactivation.
6. **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** — non-thermal preservation of liquid foods.
7. **Edible coatings and films** — barrier against O$_2$, moisture.

**B. Chemical Methods:**
1. **Class I preservatives** — salt, sugar, vinegar, spices (traditional).
2. **Class II preservatives** — chemical: **sorbic acid (2000 ppm max)**, **benzoic acid (1000 ppm max)**, **sulphur dioxide (SO$_2$, 2000 ppm)**, nitrites (in cured meats, 200 ppm).
3. **Antioxidants** — BHA, BHT, TBHQ (in oils), ascorbic acid (synergist).
4. **Chelating agents** — EDTA sequesters pro-oxidant metals.
5. **Acidulants** — citric, acetic, lactic, malic acids lower pH.

**Examples:** Sorbic acid extends shelf life of cheese; SO$_2$ prevents browning in dried fruits; EDTA + ascorbic acid preserves color in canned vegetables.

---

> ### Question B (Choice 2) **[14 Marks]**

**(a)** What is **water activity**? Discuss the **relationship between $a_w$ and food stability** with reference to microbial growth, enzymatic activity, and lipid oxidation. Plot a typical **$a_w$ stability map** for foods. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of $a_w$:**
$$a_w = P / P_0$$
It is the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in the food to the vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature. It quantifies the *free, available* water in a food system.

**2. Microbial stability vs. $a_w$:**
- Bacteria: growth only above $a_w = 0.91$.
- Yeasts: $a_w > 0.88$.
- Molds: $a_w > 0.80$.
- Halophiles and xerophiles: $a_w = 0.65$–0.75.

**3. Enzymatic stability vs. $a_w$:**
- Most native enzymes are active only at $a_w > 0.60$–0.70.
- Lipase, amylase, protease require $a_w > 0.70$.

**4. Lipid oxidation vs. $a_w$:**
- Maximum oxidation rate occurs at intermediate $a_w$ (0.40–0.70), due to mobility of catalysts. Below $a_w = 0.20$, oxidation slows because catalyst mobility is lost.

**5. Stability Map (verbal representation):**
- $a_w$ 0.0–0.20: Most stable (dehydrated foods).
- $a_w$ 0.20–0.40: Stable, low oxidation, no microbial growth.
- $a_w$ 0.40–0.70: Stability improves, oxidation may peak.
- $a_w$ 0.70–0.85: Moderate spoilage risk.
- $a_w$ 0.85–1.00: Highly perishable (fresh foods).

**Conclusion:** Water activity is the **single most powerful index** of food stability. A combined reduction in $a_w$, pH, and temperature provides robust preservation.

---

**(b)** Explain the **Hurdle Concept** in food preservation. Illustrate with a suitable example how multiple mild preservation techniques work synergistically. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept (Leistner, 1978):**
The Hurdle Concept states that **multiple mild preservation techniques (hurdles)**, when applied simultaneously, work synergistically to inhibit microbial growth and preserve food. Each hurdle inactivates a different target in the microbial cell, so their combination provides stable preservation **without the severe quality loss** caused by a single harsh treatment.

**2. Common Hurdles:**
- Low $a_w$ (salting, drying)
- Low pH (acidification, fermentation)
- Low temperature (chill, freeze)
- Low redox potential (vacuum, N$_2$ flush)
- Antimicrobials (sorbate, nitrite)
- Competitive flora (lactic acid bacteria)
- Heat (mild pasteurization)

**3. Example: Intermediate-Moisture Meat (e.g., Beef Jerky):**
- $a_w$ reduced to 0.75 by drying and salting.
- pH reduced to 5.0–5.5 by mild fermentation.
- Nitrite added (curing) — $E_h$ lowered, *Clostridium* inhibited.
- Sorbate added — mold inhibition.
- Vacuum packaged — O$_2$ excluded.
- **Result:** Shelf-stable product at ambient temperature, no severe heat damage.

**4. Engineering Significance:**
- Lower intensity of each hurdle = better sensory and nutritional retention.
- Redundant safety — failure of one hurdle does not cause product failure.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — Avoid These Mistakes**
> 1. **Confusing moisture with water activity:** A 3-mark "differentiate" question loses 2 marks if you say they are the same. State explicitly: *moisture = quantity; $a_w$ = availability*.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Peroxide value is in **meq O$_2$/kg**; $a_w$ is **dimensionless**; $D$ value is in **minutes**. Unit omission = 0.5 mark deduction per error.
> 3. **Listing without mechanism:** Naming PPO is not enough — you must state the *reaction sequence* (Monophenol → o-Diphenol → o-Quinone → Melanin).
> 4. **Missing the "Control" in 7-mark questions:** Half the marks are reserved for control/prevention methods. If you only describe spoilage and skip control, you cap at 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up intrinsic and extrinsic parameters:** Intrinsic = inside food ($a_w$, pH, $E_h$). Extrinsic = storage environment (T, RH, O$_2$). Confusing them = 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Hurdle Concept spelling:** Examiners expect "Hurdle" / "Barrier" Technology (Leistner). Writing "Hybrid" or "Mixed" is treated as incorrect terminology.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Food Deterioration Module 1**

- **Five classes of deterioration:** Microbial, Chemical, Physical, Enzymatic, Pest-related.
- **Water activity ($a_w$):** $a_w = P/P_0$; **dimensionless**; thresholds: bacteria 0.91, yeast 0.88, mold 0.80.
- **Shelf-life equation:** $t_s = (1/k) \ln(Q_0 / Q_e)$; combined with Arrhenius $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$.
- **$Q_{10}$ rule:** Shelf-life halves for every 10 °C rise (for $Q_{10} = 2$).
- **PPO mechanism:** Monophenol → o-Diphenol → o-Quinone → Brown Melanin (blanching inactivates PPO).
- **Lipid oxidation:** Free-radical chain (Initiation, Propagation, Termination); PV and TBA as QC metrics.
- **Maillard reaction:** Reducing sugar + amino acid → melanoidins; desirable in baking, undesirable in dried milk.
- **Preservation methods:** Thermal ($F_0$), low $a_w$, low pH, low T, MAP/vacuum, irradiation, HPP, chemical preservatives.
- **Common preservatives with FSSAI limits:** Sorbic acid 2000 ppm, Benzoic acid 1000 ppm, SO$_2$ 2000 ppm, Nitrite 200 ppm.
- **Hurdle Concept (Leistner):** Multiple mild techniques work synergistically; improves quality and provides safety redundancy.
- **Intrinsic factors** (in food): $a_w$, pH, $E_h$, antimicrobials, nutrients.
- **Extrinsic factors** (environment): T, RH, O$_2$, light, microbes.
- **Implicit factors** (processing): Heat treatment, packaging, sanitation.
- **Microbial growth curve:** Lag → Log → Stationary → Decline; doubling time $d = \ln 2 / \mu$.
- **Sterilization parameters:** $D$-value (decimal reduction time) and $z$-value (T-dependence).
- **Engineering mantra:** *Lower $a_w$ + lower pH + lower T + exclude O$_2$* = robust preservation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preliminary Processing Methods - Cleaning - Dry Cleaning Methods and Wet Cleaning Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Processing Methods - Cleaning - Dry Cleaning Methods and Wet Cleaning Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Cleaning" in Food Processing?

In the context of **Food Process Engineering**, *cleaning* is defined as the **preliminary unit operation** in which unwanted foreign matter (extraneous matter, dirt, dust, stones, chaff, husks, insects, larvae, rodent excreta, pesticide residues, microbial load, and surface contaminants) is physically separated from the raw food material — **without altering the inherent chemical composition, nutritional value, or physical integrity** of the food itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"Cleaning is the removal of foreign matter adhering to or mixed with the food raw material. It is the first and most essential step in any food processing chain because the quality, safety, and shelf-life of the finished product are determined at this stage."*

The cleaned food is called the **accept** (good product), while the removed material is termed the **reject** (foreign matter). The **cleaning efficiency (η)** is mathematically given as:

$$\eta_{\text{cleaning}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{removed foreign matter}}}{M_{\text{total foreign matter present}}} \times 100 \;[\%]$$

A high η implies superior cleaning performance. Industrial target for grains/cereals is **η ≥ 99.5 %** before milling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why Cleaning Matters"

Think of cleaning like **brushing your teeth before a dental check-up**. The dentist can only identify cavities if your teeth are free of plaque. Similarly, in food processing:
- A flour miller **cannot** produce premium atta if wheat still carries mud and stones.
- A dal processor **cannot** obtain split, clean lentils if the whole grain carries chaff and dust.
- A fruit juice plant **cannot** meet FSSAI microbial standards if incoming apples carry pesticide residues.

Cleaning is therefore the **"gatekeeper unit operation"** — if it fails, every downstream process (sorting, grading, milling, packaging) is compromised.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Broad Branches of Cleaning (KTU 2024 Module 1 Classification):**
> 1. **Dry Cleaning Methods** — use **air, screens, magnets, vibration, friction** (no water used).
> 2. **Wet Cleaning Methods** — use **water (with or without sanitizers)** as the cleaning medium.

## 1.3 When to Use Dry Cleaning vs. Wet Cleaning — A Decision Snapshot

| Parameter | Dry Cleaning | Wet Cleaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Food moisture** | Low moisture (< 15 % w.b.) preferred | Suitable for high-moisture foods (fruits, veg) |
| **Water-soluble dirt** | Cannot remove | Highly effective |
| **Energy use** | Lower (no drying needed afterwards) | Higher (requires dewatering/drying) |
| **Examples** | Wheat, rice, dal, spices, pulses | Fruits, vegetables, fish, meat, leafy greens |
| **Microbial reduction** | Limited (~1 log) | Significant (3–5 log with sanitizer) |

## 1.4 Standards & Metrics Used

- **Cleaning Efficiency (η):** ≥ 99.5 % for grains.
- **Foreign Matter Limit (FMM):** Typically **< 1 % by weight** for cleaned grains (Agmark/BIS standard).
- **Log Reduction (LR):** $LR = \log_{10}(N_i / N_f)$, where $N_i$ = initial microbial count, $N_f$ = final count. Wet cleaning with 50–200 ppm chlorine achieves **LR ≈ 2–3**.
- **Stokes Number (Stk):** Dimensionless number governing particle separation in air/water streams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DRY CLEANING METHODS — Operational Principles

Dry cleaning uses **mechanical forces** (gravity, centrifugal, magnetic, aerodynamic, frictional) to separate foreign matter **without water**. It is the first line of defense for **cereals, pulses, oilseeds, spices, nuts and dry fruits**.

### 2.1.1 Air Classification / Aspiration

**Principle:** Light impurities (chaff, dust, husk, straw, empty grains) have **lower terminal velocity** than the sound food grains. When the grain stream is subjected to an **upward air current**, the lighter impurities are carried away (lifted) while the heavier grains fall down.

**Equipment:** Aspirator, winnowing fan, gravity separator with air leg, pneumatic conveyor-classifier.

**Governing Physics — Stokes' Law for Terminal Velocity:**

$$v_t \;=\; \frac{d^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu} \quad \text{[for Re} < 0.2\text{]}$$

$$v_t \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4 \, d \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_f}} \quad \text{[for higher Re]}$$

where
- $v_t$ = terminal velocity (m/s)
- $d$ = particle diameter (m)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\rho_p$ = particle density (kg/m³)
- $\rho_f$ = fluid (air) density ≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at 20 °C
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of air ≈ **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (function of Reynolds number)

**Operating Rule (KTU Board Favourite):**
The air velocity **V_air** must be chosen such that:
$$v_t^{\text{(impurity)}} \;<\; V_{\text{air}} \;<\; v_t^{\text{(grain)}}$$

### 2.1.2 Screening / Sieving

**Principle:** Particles are separated by **size (and to some extent shape)** by passing them over vibrating or rotating screens with calibrated apertures.

**Equipment:** Rotary screen (trommel), vibrating screen (gyratory), flat reciprocating screen, oscillating sieve.

**Key Design Equations:**

$$S_{\text{efficiency}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Mass of undersize reporting correctly}}{\text{Total mass of undersize in feed}} \times 100$$

$$Q \;=\; A \cdot v \cdot \rho_b \quad \text{[throughput]}$$

where
- $Q$ = mass flow rate (kg/h)
- $A$ = effective screen area (m²)
- $v$ = linear velocity of material (m/s)
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density (kg/m³)

### 2.1.3 Magnetic Separation

**Principle:** Uses **permanent magnets (Nd-Fe-B / ferrite) or electromagnets** to extract ferrous (Fe) contaminants like metal shavings, wire pieces, nail fragments.

**Equipment:** Magnetic drum, magnetic pulley (over belt conveyor), plate magnet, drawer magnet, in-line pipeline magnet.

**Magnetic Force Equation:**

$$F_m \;=\; \frac{\chi \, V \, B \, (dB/dx)}{\mu_0}$$

where
- $\chi$ = magnetic susceptibility of the particle
- $V$ = particle volume
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (Tesla) — industrial values: **0.1–0.8 T**
- $dB/dx$ = magnetic field gradient

**For food-grade processing, B ≥ 0.3 T is recommended** for ferrous removal.

### 2.1.4 Gravity Separation / De-stoning

**Principle:** Exploits **density difference** between food grains (≈ 1.2–1.4 g/cm³) and stones (≈ 2.4–2.6 g/cm³). Using a vibrating inclined deck with air up-current (fluidization), lighter grains float up & travel uphill, while heavier stones sink & travel downhill.

**Equipment:** Destoner (gravity separator — Forsberg, SATAKE, Bühler).

**Terminal-velocity-based separation number:**

$$\psi \;=\; \frac{\rho_p - \rho_f}{\rho_w - \rho_p}$$

where $\rho_w$ = density of water (reference), $\rho_p$ = particle density.

A high $\psi$ indicates the particle is easily separable.

### 2.1.5 Other Dry Methods (Quick Reference)

- **Brushing/Scouring:** Rotating brushes remove surface dust & loose husk (used in wheat pre-cleaning).
- **Attrition:** Inter-particle friction peels off adhering dirt (e.g., abrasive scourer for paddy).
- **Specific Gravity Tables:** Stratification on inclined vibrating deck with airflow.

## 2.2 WET CLEANING METHODS — Operational Principles

Wet cleaning uses **water (plain or with sanitizer/detergent)** as the separating medium. Essential for **fruits, vegetables, root crops, fish, meat and leafy produce** where mud, latex, pesticide residues and microbial load are concerns.

### 2.2.1 Soaking

**Principle:** Food is **immersed in water** for a defined period, allowing soil to soften and settle. Detergents or mild acids (e.g., 0.5–1 % citric acid) may be added.

**Fickian Soaking Model (water uptake):**

$$\frac{M_t - M_0}{M_\infty - M_0} \;=\; 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D t}{4 L^2}\right]$$

For long times (simplified form):

$$\frac{M_t - M_0}{M_\infty - M_0} \;\approx\; 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D t}{4 L^2}\right)$$

where $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s), $L$ = half-thickness (m), $t$ = time.

### 2.2.2 Spray Washing

**Principle:** High-velocity water sprays (nozzles) impinge on the food surface and **dislodge adhering dirt by shear force**.

**Shear stress imparted by droplet:**

$$\tau_{\text{spray}} \;=\; \rho_w \, v_{\text{drop}}^2$$

Higher nozzle pressure ⇒ higher $\tau$ ⇒ better cleaning. Typical industrial range: **2–8 bar** nozzle pressure.

### 2.2.3 Flotation Washing

**Principle:** Immersed food is conveyed through a water tank; **buoyancy separates** light contaminants (insects, chaff, leaves) which float, while **heavier dirt and stones sink**. The food itself (e.g., peas, beans, grapes) is carried out by an inclined screw or paddle.

**Buoyancy Equation:**

$$F_b \;=\; \rho_w \, V_p \, g \;-\; \rho_p \, V_p \, g \;=\; (\rho_w - \rho_p)\, V_p \, g$$

If $\rho_w > \rho_p$, particle floats (buoyant). Food particles of $\rho_p \approx 1.0$ g/cm³ float; stones ($\rho_p \approx 2.5$ g/cm³) sink.

### 2.2.4 Drum Washing

**Principle:** Rotating **perforated drum** partially submerged in water; food tumbles inside while water spray rinses. Rotation = **0.5–3 rpm** for fruits.

### 2.2.5 Jet Washing (High-Pressure)

**Principle:** Water jets at **30–100 bar** blast away strongly adhering soil (carrots, potatoes, beetroot). Combines kinetic impact with shear.

$$P_{\text{jet}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\rho_w \, v^2 \quad \text{[dynamic pressure]}$$

### 2.2.6 Ultrasonic Cleaning

**Principle:** **High-frequency sound waves (20–100 kHz)** generate microscopic cavitation bubbles in water; their **violent collapse** produces localized shock waves (micro-jets up to **1000 atm**) that dislodge dirt from crevices (e.g., salad leaves, broccoli florets).

**Cavitation Threshold (Blake's criterion):**

$$P_b \;=\; P_0 + \frac{2\sigma}{R_0}$$

where $P_b$ = bubble collapse pressure, $\sigma$ = surface tension, $R_0$ = initial bubble radius.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chlorination:** Most common chemical adjunct to wet cleaning. Free chlorine at **50–200 ppm** for 5–15 min yields **2–3 log** microbial reduction. pH must be maintained **6.0–7.5** for optimal HOCl formation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables | Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{M_{\text{removed}}}{M_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $M$ = mass | % | Cleaning efficiency |
| 2 | $v_t = \dfrac{d^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18\mu}$ | $v_t$, $d$, $\rho$, $\mu$ | m/s | Air aspiration (Stokes) |
| 3 | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$ | $C_D$ = drag coeff. | m/s | Newton's regime |
| 4 | $S_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{undersize}}}{M_{\text{undersize,feed}}} \times 100$ | $M$ | % | Screen efficiency |
| 5 | $Q = A \, v \, \rho_b$ | Throughput | kg/h | Screening capacity |
| 6 | $F_m = \dfrac{\chi V B (dB/dx)}{\mu_0}$ | $\chi$ = susceptibility | N | Magnetic force |
| 7 | $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_p) V_p g$ | Buoyancy | N | Flotation |
| 8 | $\tau_{\text{spray}} = \rho_w v^2$ | $\tau$ = shear | Pa | Spray impact |
| 9 | $P_{\text{jet}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_w v^2$ | $P$ | Pa | Jet washing |
| 10 | $LR = \log_{10}(N_i / N_f)$ | $N$ = CFU/g | log | Microbial kill |
| 11 | $\psi = \dfrac{\rho_p - \rho_f}{\rho_w - \rho_p}$ | $\psi$ | — | Separability index |
| 12 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | — | Flow regime check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **Re range** when quoting Stokes' law. If $Re > 0.2$, you **MUST** use Newton's regime formula — a common board-exam trap.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wheat cleaning line** (Bühler/SATAKE): Dry cleaning (aspiration + magnetic + screening + destoning) **before** tempering → raises milling yield by **3–5 %**.
- **Apple juice plant**: Flotation wash → spray rinse → chlorination (100 ppm) → achieves **FSSAI microbial limits**.
- **Marine fisheries**: Flotation + ultrasonic cleaning of prawns/shrimp reduces bacterial load by **~4 logs** before IQF freezing.
- **Pulse processing**: Dry cleaning (aspiration + magnetic) is **mandatory** before dehusking — otherwise stone fragments damage the dehusking roll.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Terminal Velocity of a Food Particle in Air (Stokes' Law)

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of a Falling Particle
A spherical food particle falling under gravity in still air experiences:
1. **Gravitational force (downward):** $F_g = m_p g = V_p \rho_p g = \tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_p g$
2. **Buoyant force (upward):** $F_b = V_p \rho_f g = \tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_f g$
3. **Drag force (upward, opposes motion):** $F_D = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_f v^2 \cdot A_p$

For a sphere, projected area $A_p = \tfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$.

### Step 2 — Force Balance During Terminal (Steady) Fall
At terminal velocity, **acceleration = 0**, so:

$$F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_p g - \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_f g - C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_f v_t^2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = 0$$

### Step 3 — Algebraic Simplification
Multiply through by $\dfrac{24}{\pi d^2}$:

$$4 d (\rho_p - \rho_f) g - 3 C_D \rho_f v_t^2 = 0$$

Rearrange to solve for $v_t^2$:

$$v_t^2 \;=\; \frac{4 d (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 C_D \rho_f}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;v_t \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4 d (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 C_D \rho_f}}\;} \quad \text{(Newton's regime, Re} > 0.2\text{)}$$

### Step 4 — Stokes' Regime (Low Re)
For laminar flow, $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re} = \dfrac{24\mu}{\rho_f v_t d}$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = \frac{24\mu}{\rho_f v_t d} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_f v_t^2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 3 \pi \mu v_t d$$

Solving for $v_t$:

$$\boxed{\;v_t \;=\; \frac{d^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}\;} \quad \text{(Stokes' regime, Re} \leq 0.2\text{)}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Q:** A wheat grain (diameter $d = 5$ mm, density $\rho_p = 1350$ kg/m³) and a husk particle (d = 3 mm, $\rho_p = 600$ kg/m³) fall through still air. Determine (a) terminal velocity of each (b) the design air velocity for an aspirator to separate them. Take $\rho_f = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s.

**Solution:**

**Step 5.1 — Wheat grain:**
$$v_t^{\text{wheat}} = \frac{(0.005)^2 \times 9.81 \times (1350 - 1.2)}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 9.81 \times 1348.8}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= \frac{0.3308}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 1015 \,\text{m/s}$$

Wait — this is unrealistically high. Re-check:
$$Re = \frac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu} = \frac{1.2 \times 1015 \times 0.005}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 3.37 \times 10^{5}$$

$Re >> 0.2$, so **Stokes' law is invalid**. Use Newton's regime.

For grain-like particles in air, the empirical Allen equation is more accurate:

$$v_t^{\text{Allen}} \;=\; \left[ \frac{4 d^{1.6} g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{56 \, \rho_f} \right]^{1/1.6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Industrial Note:** Most food grains ($d$ = 2–8 mm, $\rho_p$ = 1200–1400 kg/m³) fall in the **intermediate / Newton's regime** when aspirated. Air velocities of **5–15 m/s** are typical for industrial grain aspirators. Engineers usually refer to experimental drag-coefficient charts or Allen's equation.

**Corrected numerical approach using Newton's formula with $C_D \approx 0.44$ (sphere, turbulent regime):**

$$v_t^{\text{wheat}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.005 \times 9.81 \times 1348.8}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{264.7}{1.584}}$$

$$\approx \sqrt{167.1} \;\approx\; 12.93 \,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5.2 — Husk particle:**
$$v_t^{\text{husk}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.003 \times 9.81 \times (600 - 1.2)}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{70.5}{1.584}} \;\approx\; 6.67 \,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5.3 — Design air velocity:**
$$v_t^{\text{husk}} \;<\; V_{\text{air}} \;<\; v_t^{\text{wheat}}$$

$$\boxed{\;6.67 \;<\; V_{\text{air}} \;<\; 12.93 \,\text{m/s}\;}$$

A safe industrial choice: **V_air ≈ 9 m/s** (geometric mean ≈ 9.3 m/s).

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Dry vs. Wet Methods

| Criterion | Dry Cleaning | Wet Cleaning |
|---|---|---|
| Operating cost | Low | Medium–High |
| Water footprint | Zero | High (10–100 L/kg) |
| Microbial reduction | 0.5–1 log | 2–4 log |
| Cleaning efficiency | 90–99 % | 95–99.9 % |
| Residual chemical risk | Low | Possible (sanitizer) |
| Effect on moisture content | Negligible | Increases significantly |
| Compatibility with sticky foods | Poor | Excellent |
| Energy footprint | Low–Medium | Medium–High (drying) |

### 3.3 Sequential Cleaning Logic — Typical Grain Mill

$$\text{Reception} \rightarrow \text{Pre-cleaning (Aspiration)} \rightarrow \text{Screening} \rightarrow \text{Magnetic} \rightarrow \text{Destoner} \rightarrow \text{Dry Scourer} \rightarrow \text{Tempering (water add)} \rightarrow \text{Milling}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why sequence matters:** Aspiration **before** screening removes dust that would otherwise clog screen apertures; magnetic **before** destoning prevents metal sparks in stone-impact zone; scourer **after** destoning removes dust introduced by attrition.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Cleaning Classification — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Incoming] --> B{Choose Cleaning Type}
    B -->|Low moisture food| C[DRY CLEANING]
    B -->|High moisture food| D[WET CLEANING]

    C --> C1[Aspiration / Air Classification]
    C --> C2[Screening / Sieving]
    C --> C3[Magnetic Separation]
    C --> C4[Gravity Separation / Destoning]
    C --> C5[Scouring / Brushing]

    D --> D1[Soaking]
    D --> D2[Spray Washing]
    D --> D3[Flotation Washing]
    D --> D4[Drum Washing]
    D --> D5[High Pressure Jet Washing]
    D --> D6[Ultrasonic Cleaning]
    D --> D7[Chlorination / Sanitizer Wash]

    C1 --> E1[Light Impurity Out]
    C2 --> E2[Oversize / Undersize Out]
    C3 --> E3[Ferrous Contaminant Out]
    C4 --> E4[Stones Out]
    D3 --> E5[Floating Chaff Out]
    D3 --> E6[Sunken Stones Out]
    D7 --> E7[Microbial Log Reduction]

    E1 --> F[Cleaned Food Accept]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    E7 --> F
```

## 4.2 Aspirator Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Dirty Grain Feed Hopper] --> R2[Vibratory Feeder]
    R2 --> R3[Inlet Air Stream]
    R3 --> R4[Separation Zone]
    R4 -->|Heavy grains fall| R5[Bottom Outlet Clean Grains]
    R4 -->|Light impurities lifted| R6[Cyclone Separator]
    R6 --> R7[Filter Bag / Dust Collector]
    R7 --> R8[Reject Disposal]
    R5 --> R9[Accept to Next Stage]
```

## 4.3 Wet Cleaning Cascade Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Raw Fruits / Vegetables] --> W2[Pre-Soak Tank]
    W2 --> W3[Flotation Washer]
    W3 -->|Stones / heavy dirt sink| W3a[Bottom Discharge]
    W3 -->|Light chaff floats| W3b[Skimming Overflow]
    W3 --> W4[Spray Rinse Tunnel]
    W4 --> W5[Chlorination Tank 100 ppm HOCl]
    W5 --> W6[Final Rinse with Potable Water]
    W6 --> W7[Dewatering Vibrating Screen]
    W7 --> W8[Cleaned Accept to Sorting]
```

## 4.4 Grain Cleaning Line — Industrial Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Receiving Pit] --> G2[Bucket Elevator] --> G3[Coarse Screen]
    G3 --> G4[Bucket Elevator] --> G5[Magnetic Separator]
    G5 --> G6[Aspirator No 1]
    G6 --> G7[Fine Screen]
    G7 --> G8[Destoner]
    G8 --> G9[Disc Separator]
    G9 --> G10[Scourer]
    G10 --> G11[Aspirator No 2]
    G11 --> G12[Cleaned Wheat Silo]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents an **equipment unit**. Arrows show **mass flow direction** (solid food) or **reject side-stream**. In the KTU exam, you may be asked to label such flow diagrams or justify the sequence.

## 4.5 Magnetic Separation Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Conveyor Belt] --> M2[Food Layer on Belt]
    M2 --> M3[Magnetic Head Pulley]
    M3 -->|Magnetic field deflects Fe| M4[Side Reject Chute]
    M3 -->|Non magnetic continues| M5[Discharge to Next Unit]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between dry cleaning and wet cleaning methods in food processing. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Dry Cleaning | Wet Cleaning |
|---|---|---|
| Medium used | Air, screens, magnets, gravity | Water ± sanitizer |
| Suitable for | Dry grains, pulses, spices | Fresh fruits, vegetables, fish |
| Water usage | None | High |
| Microbial reduction | Limited | Substantial (2–4 log) |

**Examples:**
- **Dry:** Aspiration of wheat, magnetic separation in flour mill.
- **Wet:** Flotation washing of apples, chlorination of leafy greens.

*(Full marks require a clean 4-point table + 2 examples each.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**State Stokes' law as applied to aspiration cleaning of food grains.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stokes' law gives the terminal velocity of a small spherical particle falling under gravity in a viscous fluid:

$$v_t = \frac{d^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$$

where $d$ = particle diameter, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $\rho_f$ = air density, $\mu$ = air viscosity.

**Condition:** Valid only when Reynolds number $Re \leq 0.2$.

**Application:** It governs the design of grain aspirators — the chosen air velocity must exceed $v_t$ of impurities but remain below $v_t$ of sound grains to ensure separation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Writing generic definitions without naming **specific equipment** (loses 2 marks).
> - Quoting Stokes' law without mentioning the **Re ≤ 0.2 validity condition** (loses 1 mark).
> - Failing to state **units** in numerical answers (loses 1 mark).
> - Confusing *cleaning* with *sorting* / *grading* (cleaning removes **foreign matter**, sorting separates **acceptable** from **unacceptable food**).
> - Skipping flow diagrams in long answers — KTU awards **2 marks** for well-labelled diagrams.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper based on 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the principle, working and applications of **air classification (aspiration)** in cleaning of food grains. Derive the expression for terminal velocity of a spherical particle under Stokes' regime.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A rice mill processes paddy at 10 tonnes/h. Design (with calculations) the **air velocity** needed to separate empty paddy kernels (d = 1.8 mm, ρ_p = 900 kg/m³) from sound paddy (d = 2.6 mm, ρ_p = 1180 kg/m³) using an aspirator. Assume air: ρ_f = 1.2 kg/m³, μ = 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s. Drag coefficient C_D ≈ 0.44.

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Aspiration Principle & Derivation (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
Aspiration exploits the difference in **terminal velocities** between sound food grains and lighter impurities (dust, chaff, husk, empty grains, straw). An upward stream of air carries the lighter impurities upward while the heavier sound grains fall by gravity.

**Equipment (1 mark):**
- Vertical air-leg aspirator
- Horizontal aspiration channel
- Pneumatic conveyor-classifier
- Cyclone-type aspirator

**Working (1 mark):**
Dirty grain is fed onto a vibrating tray. A controlled air stream is drawn through the falling curtain of grains. Light particles are lifted into the cyclone separator (where dust settles out), and clean grains discharge at the bottom.

**Derivation of Terminal Velocity (Stokes' regime) — 3 marks:**

For a particle falling under gravity in a viscous fluid, at steady (terminal) state:

$$\text{Net downward force} = \text{Drag force}$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 3 \pi \mu \, v_t \, d$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\;v_t = \frac{d^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}\;} \quad \text{(valid for Re} \leq 0.2\text{)}$$

For Re > 0.2, Newton's regime formula applies:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 d g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$$

**Design rule:** Choose $v_t^{\text{(impurity)}} < V_{\text{air}} < v_t^{\text{(grain)}}$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Empty kernel: $d_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho_{p1} = 900$ kg/m³
- Sound kernel: $d_2 = 2.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho_{p2} = 1180$ kg/m³
- Air: $\rho_f = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $C_D = 0.44$

**Step 1 — Terminal velocity of empty kernel (using Newton regime, expected Re ≫ 0.2):**

$$v_{t1} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 \times (900 - 1.2)}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.2}}$$

$$v_{t1} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 \times 898.8}{1.584}}$$

$$v_{t1} = \sqrt{\frac{63.46}{1.584}} = \sqrt{40.06}$$

$$\boxed{v_{t1} \approx 6.33 \,\text{m/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Terminal velocity of sound kernel:**

$$v_{t2} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 \times (1180 - 1.2)}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.2}}$$

$$v_{t2} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 \times 1178.8}{1.584}}$$

$$v_{t2} = \sqrt{\frac{120.2}{1.584}} = \sqrt{75.89}$$

$$\boxed{v_{t2} \approx 8.71 \,\text{m/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify Re assumption (for sound kernel):**

$$Re_2 = \frac{1.2 \times 8.71 \times 2.6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1501$$

$Re \gg 0.2$ ✓ — Newton's regime valid.

**Step 4 — Design air velocity:**

$$\boxed{6.33 \,\text{m/s} \;<\; V_{\text{air}} \;<\; 8.71 \,\text{m/s}}$$

**Recommended industrial choice:** $V_{\text{air}} \approx 7.5$ m/s (a safe midpoint allowing margin for grain size variation).

**Throughput context (1 mark):**
At 10 t/h throughput, volumetric feed ≈ $\dfrac{10000}{1180 \times 0.6} \approx 14.1$ m³/h. Aspirator cross-section chosen to give 7.5 m/s face velocity ⇒ area = 14.1 / (7.5 × 3600) ≈ 0.00052 m² (≈ 2.3 cm × 2.3 cm equivalent), but in practice a **wider aspirator channel** is used with multiple air inlets.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the principle and working of **flotation washing** for cleaning of fruits and vegetables. State the engineering parameters that govern the design.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete cleaning sequence** in a modern flour mill starting from raw wheat intake to clean wheat ready for tempering. List the impurities removed at each stage.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Flotation Washing (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
Flotation washing exploits the **density difference** between food particles and contaminants when immersed in water. Light contaminants (insects, leaves, chaff, hairs, lighter stones) **float**, while heavier contaminants (sand, grit, dense stones, metal pieces) **sink**. The food itself (fruits, vegetables with density close to water, ρ_p ≈ 0.95–1.05 g/cm³) is **carried horizontally** by an inclined paddle/screw conveyor and discharged at the outlet.

**Buoyancy foundation (1 mark):**
$$F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_p) V_p g$$

- If $\rho_p < \rho_w$ → particle **floats**
- If $\rho_p > \rho_w$ → particle **sinks**

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Dirty produce is fed into a long water-filled tank.
2. Paddles / augers / oscillating flights slowly transport food horizontally.
3. Floating debris is skimmed off the surface by an overflow weir.
4. Sunken heavy debris is collected and discharged via a bottom valve.
5. Washed food exits through an inclined ramp to the next unit (spray rinse / sorting).

**Design Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Residence time:** 3–8 min (typical for apples, pears)
- **Water-to-product ratio:** 5–20 L per kg
- **Water temperature:** 5–25 °C (cold for soft fruits)
- **Paddle speed:** 5–15 rpm
- **pH of water:** 6.5–7.5
- **Reuse of water:** Multi-stage counter-current to reduce volume.

**Application:** Cleaning of apples, pears, tomatoes, grapes, peas, beans, citrus, mangoes, carrots (combined with brushing), paddy (pre-soak).

#### Part (b) — Wheat Mill Cleaning Line (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Wheat Intake → Coarse Separator (sieve 1) → Bucket Elevator →
Magnetic Separator → Aspirator (Air Classifier) → Fine Screen →
Gravity Destoner → Disc Separator → Scourer → Aspiration No 2 →
Entoleter (Disinfestor) → Dampener (Tempering) → Clean Wheat Silo
```

**Impurities removed at each stage (4 marks):**

| Stage | Equipment | Impurity Removed |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coarse Separator | Large foreign matter (straw, paper, stones, wood) |
| 2 | Magnetic Separator | Ferrous metals (nails, wire, filings) |
| 3 | Aspirator | Light dust, chaff, empty grains, husk |
| 4 | Fine Screen | Small weed seeds, broken grains |
| 5 | Gravity Destoner | Stones, glass, mud lumps (density > wheat) |
| 6 | Disc Separator | Wild oats, barley, long-shaped impurities |
| 7 | Scourer | Surface dust, micro-flora, attached soil |
| 8 | Aspiration No 2 | Dust generated by scouring |
| 9 | Entoleter | Insect eggs, larvae (centrifugal impact) |
| 10 | Dampener | Adds controlled moisture (tempering for milling) |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Cleaning Methods: High-Yield Revision Points**

- ✅ **Cleaning** = removal of **foreign matter** (not sorting of food itself).
- ✅ Two categories: **Dry Cleaning** (no water) and **Wet Cleaning** (water used).
- ✅ **Dry Cleaning methods** (memorize): Aspiration / Air Classification, Screening / Sieving, Magnetic Separation, Gravity Separation / Destoning, Scouring / Brushing, Attrition.
- ✅ **Wet Cleaning methods**: Soaking, Spray Washing, Flotation, Drum Washing, Jet Washing, Ultrasonic Cleaning, Chlorination.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law** (laminar, Re ≤ 0.2): $v_t = \dfrac{d^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18\mu}$
- ✅ **Newton's regime** (turbulent): $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$
- ✅ **Design rule for aspiration:** $v_t^{\text{impurity}} < V_{\text{air}} < v_t^{\text{grain}}$
- ✅ **Buoyancy in flotation:** Float when $\rho_p < \rho_w$; sink when $\rho_p > \rho_w$.
- ✅ **Magnetic flux density for industrial food magnets:** $B \geq 0.3$ T.
- ✅ **Chlorination standard:** 50–200 ppm free chlorine, pH 6.0–7.5, contact 5–15 min → 2–3 log kill.
- ✅ **Log reduction formula:** $LR = \log_{10}(N_i / N_f)$.
- ✅ **Typical cleaning efficiency target:** $\eta_c \geq 99.5\%$ for grains.
- ✅ **Cleaning sequence in flour mill:** Intake → Coarse sieve → Magnetic → Aspirator → Fine screen → Destoner → Disc → Scourer → Aspiration → Entoleter → Tempering.
- ✅ Always check **Re** before applying Stokes' law — a KTU board favourite trap.
- ✅ Always label **flow diagrams** with equipment names and the impurity rejected at each stage.
- ✅ Flotation washing uses **density separation in water**; aspiration uses **density separation in air**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Note:** In KTU 14-mark answers, the structure that consistently scores full marks is:
> **Definition (2) + Principle (2) + Equipment / Diagram (3) + Working / Derivation (4) + Application / Numerical (3)**. Stick to this blueprint.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sorting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Processing and Technology**, **Sorting** is a primary **post-harvest unit operation** that involves the segregation of a heterogeneous bulk mass of food material (grains, fruits, vegetables, nuts, etc.) into two or more distinct **homogeneous fractions** or grades based on measurable differences in their **physical, optical, or physico-chemical properties**.

According to standard food engineering nomenclature, sorting is the **upstream quality control step** that precedes grading, cleaning, packaging, and storage. It is fundamentally different from *grading* — sorting *separates* undesirable material from desirable material, while grading *classifies* acceptable material into quality categories.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT747 – Module 1):** Sorting is treated as a foundational **separation operation** in the food processing chain. The 2024 scheme emphasizes understanding the **principle of separation**, the **classification of sorters** (size, shape, density, color, magnetic), and the **efficiency metrics** governing each technique.

### 1.2 Classification Basis (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)

Sorting techniques are classified by the **property exploited for separation**:

| Property Exploited | Sorting Technique | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Size (Diameter) | Screening, Sieving, Trommels | Wheat, rice, pulses |
| Shape | Disc separators, Cylinder separators | Lentils, seeds |
| Weight / Density | Gravity tables, Pneumatic separators | Coffee, beans |
| Color / Optical | Electronic color sorters | Rice, coffee, nuts |
| Magnetic Susceptibility | Magnetic separators | Removing iron filings |
| Surface Texture | Friction-based rollers | Stones vs. grains |
| Buoyancy | Floatation tanks | Fruits, peas |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Kitchen Counter" Model:**
> Imagine you bring home a mixed basket of items from a market — rice grains, stones, broken grains, dust, and chaff. Before you cook, you naturally **sort** them out. You might:
> - *Sieve* the rice to remove dust and stones (this is **size sorting**).
> - *Blow air* across the rice to blow away light chaff while grains fall back (this is **density/pneumatic sorting**).
> - *Pick out* blackened or discolored grains by hand (this is **manual color sorting**, mechanized as **optical sorting**).
> - *Use a magnet* (in industry) to remove any metallic contamination (this is **magnetic sorting**).
>
> Industrial sorting is simply this **kitchen counter intuition** scaled up using mechanical, pneumatic, optical, and electromagnetic engineering principles — designed for **tons per hour** rather than grams per minute.

### 1.4 Industrial Significance — Why Sorting Matters

- **Food Safety:** Removes physical contaminants (stones, glass, metal, plastic).
- **Quality Assurance:** Ensures uniform grade for downstream processing (milling, canning, baking).
- **Process Efficiency:** Uniform size improves heat transfer in drying, roasting, and blanching.
- **Economic Value:** Sorted premium-grade produce fetches higher market price.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Adheres to **FSSAI (India)**, **FDA (USA)**, and **Codex Alimentarius** international food safety standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant in Sorting:** The standard acceleration due to gravity **g = 9.81 m/s²** governs the design of all gravity-based and pneumatic sorting systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "How" and "Why" of Sorting

The sorting operation, irrespective of the method, follows a universal **three-stage logic**:

1. **Stage 1 — Feed Presentation:** The bulk food material is fed as a thin, uniform, mono-layered stream to ensure **every particle is exposed** to the separating field. This is the **Why** behind vibratory feeders, inclined conveyors, and rotating drums in front of a sorter.
2. **Stage 2 — Differential Response:** Particles in the feed exhibit **different physical responses** to the separating field. A stone is heavier than a grain (gravitational field), a red apple reflects a different wavelength than a green one (optical field), a ferrous particle is attracted to a magnet (magnetic field). The **How** lies in amplifying this tiny differential into a macroscopic trajectory deviation.
3. **Stage 3 — Collection / Divergence:** The diverged fractions are collected in separate chutes, hoppers, or bins. A sharp **trajectory cut-point** is essential; otherwise, separation efficiency drops.

### 2.2 Detailed Working of Major Sorters

#### 2.2.1 Size Sorting (Screening)
- A **screen** is a surface with apertures of defined geometry.
- Particles **smaller than the aperture** pass through (called *undersize* or *fines*).
- Particles **larger than the aperture** are retained (called *oversize*).
- Particles of **intermediate size** (between 0.5 and 1.0 times the aperture) tend to **plug the screen** and are called *near-mesh* or *critical-size* particles — this is the **biggest practical problem** in screening.

#### 2.2.2 Density (Gravity) Sorting
- A **gravity table** (or *dry stoner*) uses a combination of **vibration, airflow, and inclined deck**.
- Light particles (chaff, husk) ride on top of the air-fluidized bed and are carried up-table by vibration; dense particles (stones, heavy grains) sink to the deck and move down-table.
- The **terminal velocity** difference is the underlying physical mechanism.

#### 2.2.3 Optical / Electronic Color Sorting
- High-speed **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device) cameras** scan each particle as it free-falls through a viewing chamber.
- A microprocessor compares pixel color/intensity against a pre-set **acceptance threshold**.
- A **pneumatic ejector** (solenoid + air nozzle) blasts a precise 50–100 µs air pulse to deflect **rejected** particles out of the main stream.

#### 2.2.4 Magnetic Sorting
- Exploits **ferromagnetic susceptibility** to remove tramp iron.
- Two main types: **Plate magnets** (capture ferrous over a flat surface) and **Drum/Overband magnets** (rotating permanent magnet).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU University Exam numerical and derivations. Memorize the variable definitions and units carefully.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacity of a Vibratory Screen | $Q = A \cdot v \cdot \rho_b \cdot k$ | $Q$ = capacity (kg/h); $A$ = screen area (m²); $v$ = feed velocity (m/s); $\rho_b$ = bulk density (kg/m³); $k$ = efficiency factor (dimensionless, 0.5–0.8) | Valid for free-flowing granular foods |
| 2 | Screening Effectiveness | $E_s = \dfrac{m_u}{m_t} \times 100\%$ | $E_s$ = efficiency (%); $m_u$ = mass of true undersize in overflow (kg); $m_t$ = total mass of undersize in feed (kg) | $0 \leq E_s \leq 100$ |
| 3 | Overall Separation Efficiency | $\eta = R \times P$ | $R$ = Recovery (fraction of desired material in product); $P$ = Purity (fraction of product that is desired material) | Used for all binary sorters |
| 4 | Terminal Velocity — Stokes' Law (Re < 0.2) | $v_t = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g}{18 \cdot \mu}$ | $v_t$ = terminal velocity (m/s); $d$ = particle diameter (m); $\rho_p$ = particle density (kg/m³); $\rho_f$ = fluid density (kg/m³); $g$ = 9.81 m/s²; $\mu$ = fluid viscosity (Pa·s) | Re < 0.2, valid for fine powders in air |
| 5 | Terminal Velocity — Newton's Law (Re > 1000) | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \cdot d \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g}{\rho_f}}$ | Same as above | Re > 1000, valid for coarse grains |
| 6 | Probability of Particle Passage | $P = \left(\dfrac{a}{d}\right)^2$ | $a$ = aperture size (m); $d$ = particle diameter (m) | For $a > d$, $P \to 1$ |
| 7 | Reynolds Number (Particle) | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot v \cdot d}{\mu}$ | All as above | Dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rice Mills:** Indented cylinders + optical sorters remove discolored, broken, and chalky grains.
- **Coffee Industry:** Gravity separators and electronic color sorters remove defective beans (sour, black, insect-damaged).
- **Spice Industry:** Magnetic separators + air classifiers remove stones and stem fragments from turmeric, cumin, coriander.
- **Fruit Packhouses:** Roller sizers + color sorters grade apples, tomatoes, citrus by diameter and ripeness.
- **Pulse Milling:** Disc separators split chickpea/lentil mixtures based on shape rolling properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Screening Effectiveness ($E_s$)

The **screening effectiveness** is the most important performance metric for any size-based sorter. It is derived from the **mass balance** across the screen.

**Given:**
- Feed mass flow = $F$ (kg/h), containing undersize fraction $x_f$ (mass fraction).
- Overflow (oversize) mass flow = $O$ (kg/h), containing undersize fraction $x_o$.
- Underflow (undersize) mass flow = $U$ (kg/h), containing undersize fraction $x_u$.

**Step 1 — Write the total mass balance** across the screen.

$$F = O + U$$

**Step 2 — Write the component (undersize) mass balance.**

$$F \cdot x_f = O \cdot x_o + U \cdot x_u$$

**Step 3 — Define screening effectiveness** as the fraction of the true undersize in the feed that **actually reports to the underflow** (i.e., passes through the screen). Formally:

$$E_s = \dfrac{\text{Mass of undersize in underflow}}{\text{Mass of undersize in feed}}$$

$$E_s = \dfrac{U \cdot x_u}{F \cdot x_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** $U = F - O$ and rearrange.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \dfrac{(F - O) \cdot x_u}{F \cdot x_f} \\
&= \dfrac{F \cdot x_u - O \cdot x_u}{F \cdot x_f} \\
&= \dfrac{x_u}{x_f} - \dfrac{O \cdot x_u}{F \cdot x_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize** that $x_u \approx 1$ (underflow is essentially all undersize in ideal screening) and $x_o \approx 0$ (overflow is essentially all oversize in ideal screening).

For the **ideal case**:

$$E_s^{ideal} = \dfrac{x_u}{x_f} = \dfrac{1}{x_f}$$

**Step 6 — Realistic case (imperfect separation):** Introducing a **misdistribution factor** $m$, we get the practical form used in industry:

$$E_s = \dfrac{1}{x_f} \left[ x_u - m \cdot x_o \right]$$

where $m = O / F$ is the **overflow-to-feed ratio**.

**Engineering Interpretation:** $E_s$ ranges from 0% (no undersize passes through) to 100% (perfect separation). Real industrial screens operate at $E_s = 70$–$95\%$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Terminal Velocity for Density Sorting (Stokes' Regime)

In pneumatic (air) sorting, a particle falling through still air reaches a constant **terminal velocity** when gravitational force equals drag force.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a spherical particle in steady state.**

$$F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} = F_{drag}$$

**Step 2 — Express each force** in terms of fundamental properties.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{gravity} &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6} \cdot \rho_p \cdot g \\
F_{buoyancy} &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6} \cdot \rho_f \cdot g \\
F_{drag} &= 3 \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_t \quad \text{(Stokes' drag, Re < 0.2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the force balance.**

$$\dfrac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6} \cdot \rho_p \cdot g - \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6} \cdot \rho_f \cdot g = 3 \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_t$$

**Step 4 — Factor out common terms** and simplify.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g &= 3 \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_t \\
v_t \cdot (3 \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d) &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g \\
v_t &= \dfrac{d^3 \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g}{6 \cdot 3 \cdot \mu \cdot d} \\
v_t &= \dfrac{d^2 \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g}{18 \cdot \mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Stokes' Law Result:**

$$v_t = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of units (dimensional analysis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[v_t] &= \dfrac{m^2 \cdot (kg/m^3) \cdot (m/s^2)}{Pa \cdot s} \\
&= \dfrac{m^2 \cdot kg \cdot m}{m^3 \cdot s^2 \cdot (kg / (m \cdot s)) \cdot s} \\
&= \dfrac{kg/(m \cdot s)}{kg/(m \cdot s)} = m/s \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Since $v_t \propto d^2$, **doubling the particle diameter quadruples its terminal velocity**. This is why a small stone falls much faster than a grain of the same density in an air column — the principle exploited in **pneumatic separators** and **aspiration columns** used for cleaning wheat, rice, and pulses.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example (Worked Out — Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A vibratory screen receives **2000 kg/h** of wheat feed containing **15%** by mass of broken kernels (undersize). The overflow (oversize) contains **2%** broken kernels. Find the screening effectiveness.

**Given:**
- $F = 2000$ kg/h, $x_f = 0.15$, $x_o = 0.02$
- Assume $x_u = 0.98$ (underflow is mostly undersize)
- Overflow ratio: $m = 0.85$ (since 15% reports as undersize)

**Solution:**

$$E_s = \dfrac{1}{x_f} \left[ x_u - m \cdot x_o \right]$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \dfrac{1}{0.15} \left[ 0.98 - 0.85 \times 0.02 \right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.15} \left[ 0.98 - 0.017 \right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.15} \times 0.963 \\
&= 6.42 \quad \text{(or 642\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
