
---

## 4.4 Quality Decision Tree — Mill Process Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Paddy Incoming] --> Q2{Moisture Check}
    Q2 -- Less than 14 percent --> Q3[Direct Storage]
    Q2 -- Greater than 14 percent --> Q4[Drying Required]
    Q4 --> Q5{Parboil or Raw}
    Q5 -- Parboil --> Q6[Soak Steam Dry Route]
    Q5 -- Raw --> Q7[Pre-cleaning Direct Hulling]
    Q3 --> Q7
    Q6 --> Q8[Mill Line]
    Q7 --> Q8
    Q8 --> Q9{Quality Grade}
    Q9 -- Premium --> Q10[Color Sort and Polish]
    Q9 -- Standard --> Q11[Direct Pack]
    Q10 --> Q12[Export Silo]
    Q11 --> Q13[Domestic Market]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (T1A, Q3, etc.). Labels contain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags. Reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are not used as standalone node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1.** Define "Head Rice Recovery (HRR)". Why is it considered a more critical quality parameter than total milling recovery?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### ✅ Model Answer:
**Head Rice Recovery (HRR)** is defined as the percentage ratio of whole grains (length ≥ 3/4 of original grain) obtained after milling relative to the weight of paddy fed.

$$
HRR = \dfrac{W_{head}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100
$$

**Why it is more critical:**
1. Commercial value: Whole grains fetch **higher market price** than brokens.
2. **Consumer preference:** Whole grains cook uniformly and look better.
3. **Quality indicator:** HRR is directly affected by **grain variety, moisture, drying cracks, and parboiling quality** — making it a sensitive barometer of process control.
4. **Basmati export grade** requires HRR > 55%.

---

### **Q2.** List any **three** physico-chemical changes that occur during parboiling of paddy.
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### ✅ Model Answer:
1. **Starch gelatinization:** Granular starch swells and loses birefringence when heated above 60 °C in presence of water — endosperm becomes compact and translucent.
2. **Vitamin B₁ migration:** Thiamine diffuses from the aleurone layer into the endosperm, raising milled-rice nutritional value from 0.07 to 0.21 mg/100 g.
3. **Pore / fissure sealing:** Hydrothermal treatment seals micro-cracks in endosperm → reduces breakage during whitening.

*(Optional 4th point: Lipase enzyme denaturation → longer shelf-life.)*

---

## 📕 PART B: 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

> **KTU Instruction (Standard Format):**
> *Answer any ONE full question from the following. Each full question carries 14 marks and contains sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
`(a)` Describe the **conventional raw rice milling process** with a labeled flow diagram. Explain the principle and operation of the **rubber-roll huller** used for de-husking. `[7 Marks]`

`(b)` Define **Milling Recovery** and **Head Rice Recovery**. A rice mill processes 2000 kg of paddy and yields 1380 kg of total milled rice (out of which 1180 kg is head rice). Calculate both parameters and comment on the quality. `[7 Marks]`

---

### ✅ Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a): Conventional Raw Rice Milling + Rubber-Roll Huller

**Conventional Rice Milling Flow (must be drawn):**

```
Paddy → Pre-cleaner → Destoner → Husker → Paddy Separator
       → Whitener (Abrasive Cone) → Polisher → Length Grader
       → Color Sorter → Weighing → Storage / Packing
```

**Sub-Components Explanation (each line = 1 mark):**

1. **Pre-cleaning:** Removes straw, dust, immature grains (using scalperators and indented cylinders).
2. **De-stoning:** Density-based separation using vibratory destoner; critical to protect rollers.
3. **De-husking:** Performed by **rubber-roll huller** — two rubber rolls rotate at **differential speeds (slow = 200 rpm, fast = 800 rpm)**, generating friction that strips off husk.
4. **Paddy separation:** Tray separator returns unhusked paddy via re-circulation.
5. **Whitening:** Abrasive emery cone removes bran layers by friction + mild impact.
6. **Polishing:** Friction polisher with water mist gives glossy appearance.
7. **Grading & color sorting:** Indented cylinders + optical sorters separate head rice, brokens, and discolored grains.

**Rubber-Roll Huller Principle:**
- Two rolls of **diameter 200–250 mm** with **shore-A hardness 75–85**.
- Roll lengths: **150–380 mm**.
- Rolls at **different speeds** create **shearing + friction** between paddy and roll surface.
- The husk is gripped by the faster roll and torn from the grain.
- **Husk aspiration**: aspirator draws husks via airflow into cyclone collector.
- **Husking efficiency:** 80–90% per pass.

---

#### Part (b): Milling Recovery & HRR Calculation

**Given:**
$$
W_{paddy} = 2000 \text{ kg}, \quad W_{head} = 1180 \text{ kg}, \quad W_{total} = 1380 \text{ kg}
$$

**Milling Recovery:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MR &= \dfrac{W_{total\,rice}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1380}{2000} \times 100 \\
&= 69 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Head Rice Recovery:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRR &= \dfrac{W_{head}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1180}{2000} \times 100 \\
&= 59 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Quality:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Broken rice fraction} &= 1380 - 1180 = 200 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Broken \% of total} &= \dfrac{200}{1380} \times 100 = 14.49\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Examiner-style verdict:** HRR = 59% falls in the **"Good Quality"** category (50–60% standard; ≥ 60% premium). The mill is operating within acceptable FCI norms for parboiled rice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**
`(a)` What is **parboiling**? Explain the **three stages** of parboiling in detail, mentioning the temperatures, times, and physico-chemical changes at each stage. `[7 Marks]`

`(b)` Parboiled paddy leaves the steam cooker at **32% moisture (w.b.)** and must be dried to **13% (w.b.)** before storage. For a **1000 kg batch**:
  - (i) Calculate the **mass of water to be removed**.
  - (ii) Estimate the **steam required** for drying at latent heat 540 kcal/kg and thermal efficiency 65%. `[7 Marks]`

---

### ✅ Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a): Parboiling — Definition & Three Stages

**Definition:** Parboiling is a **hydrothermal treatment** of paddy in which the grain is first soaked in hot water, then steamed, and finally dried — *before de-husking*. The result is a hardened, translucent endosperm and improved milling yield.

**Stage 1 — SOAKING (Hydration)**
- **Temperature:** 65–75 °C (traditional) / 80 °C (accelerated).
- **Time:** 36–72 h traditional / 8–10 h accelerated.
- **Process:** Paddy submerged in water; moisture rises from ~12% to ~30%.
- **Physical change:** Water penetrates endosperm; starch granule swelling begins below gelatinization temp.

**Stage 2 — STEAMING (Gelatinization)**
- **Pressure:** 0.5–1.5 kg/cm² (saturated steam).
- **Time:** 10–20 minutes.
- **Process:** Soaked paddy exposed to live steam in an open kettle or steam chest.
- **Physico-chemical change:** Starch **gelatinizes** at 60–80 °C; α-amylase denatured; vitamins migrate inward; pores seal.

**Stage 3 — DRYING (Equilibration)**
- **Method:** LSU (Low-temperature Updraft) dryer, fluidized bed, or solar dryer.
- **Moisture target:** 35% → 12–14% (w.b.).
- **Drying phases:** Constant rate (free water), then falling rate (bound water).
- **Significance:** Prevents fissuring; prepares for safe storage & milling.

> **Mnemonic:** *Soak → Cook → Dry* (KTU students remember with: **SCD = Safe, Cooked, Dried**).

---

#### Part (b): Drying Calculation

**Given:**

$$
W_{wet,\,initial} = 1000 \text{ kg}, \quad M_i = 32\%, \quad M_f = 13\% \text{ (w.b.)}, \quad L = 540 \text{ kcal/kg}, \quad \eta_{th} = 65\%
$$

**Step 1: Dry matter content.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{dry} &= W_{wet,\,i} \times (1 - M_i) \\
&= 1000 \times (1 - 0.32) \\
&= 680 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Initial water mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{w,\,i} &= 1000 - 680 = 320 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final water mass at 13% w.b.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{w,\,f} &= W_{dry} \times \dfrac{M_f}{1 - M_f} \\
&= 680 \times \dfrac{0.13}{0.87} \\
&= 101.61 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Water to be evaporated.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta W_w &= 320 - 101.61 = 218.39 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Steam mass required.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{steam} &= \dfrac{\Delta W_w \times L}{\eta_{th} \times L} \\
&= \dfrac{\Delta W_w}{\eta_{th}} \\
&= \dfrac{218.39}{0.65} \\
&\approx 336 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Alternative calculation (using $Q$):**
> $Q_{req} = 218.39 \times 540 = 117{,}930.6$ kcal
> $m_{steam} = \dfrac{117{,}930.6}{540 \times 0.65} = 336$ kg ✅

#### ✅ Final Answer:
- **(i) Water to remove = ~218.4 kg**
- **(ii) Steam required = ~336 kg**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing w.b. and d.b.** — Always state the basis clearly. Most "wrong answer" cases occur when students compute final mass directly without converting.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $(1 - M_i)$** when finding dry matter.
> 3. **Missing the thermal efficiency factor** — students often compute $m_{steam} = \Delta W_w$ directly. Examiner deducts **2 marks**.
> 4. **No flow diagram in raw-milling question** — Examiner deducts **2 marks** for missing diagram.
> 5. **Using "yield" instead of "recovery"** — KTU expects precise nomenclature: HRR ≠ yield ratio.
> 6. **Not stating temperatures and times** in parboiling stages — Examiner expects at least **one numeric value** per stage.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **✔️ HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU FACTS (Must Memorize):**
>
> - **Family of rice:** Poaceae (Gramineae); Species: *Oryza sativa* L.
> - **Composition of paddy:** Husk ~20–22% | Bran ~5–8% | Endosperm ~70% | Germ ~2–3%.
> - **Starch gelatinization range:** **60 – 80 °C** (peak ~70 °C).
> - **Safe moisture for storage:** **12 – 14%** (w.b.).
> - **Head Rice Recovery (HRR) target:** ≥ 50% (standard); ≥ 60% (premium); ≥ 65% (parboiled).
> - **Parboiling steps:** Soak (65–75 °C, 36–72 h) → Steam (0.5–1.5 kg/cm², 10–20 min) → Dry (35% → 13%).
> - **Differential roll speed in rubber huller:** **4 – 6 m/s**.
> - **Basmati HRR export standard:** ≥ 55%.
> - **Gluten:** Rice is **gluten-free**.
> - **Limiting amino acid in rice:** **Lysine**.
> - **Most important parboiling vitamin migration:** **Thiamine (B1)** from aleurone → endosperm.
> - **GI (Glycemic Index):** Raw rice ~70, Parboiled rice ~55.
> - **Husk application:** Boiler fuel, silica, building board.
> - **Bran application:** Rice bran oil (γ-oryzanol, tocopherol).
> - **Broken rice application:** Brewing, rice flour, animal feed.
> - **Standard shape classifications:** Long-slender (Basmati), Medium, Short-bold (e.g., Gobindobhog).

> **🔁 Master Formulas (Revise Last):**
>
> $$MR = \dfrac{W_{total\,rice}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100$$
> $$HRR = \dfrac{W_{head}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100$$
> $$\eta_H = \dfrac{W_{dehusked}}{W_{paddy\,fed}} \times 100$$
> $$W_{dry} = W_{wet} \times (1 - M_{wb})$$
> $$W_{w,\,f} = W_{dry} \times \dfrac{M_{f,\,wb}}{1 - M_{f,\,wb}}$$
> $$m_{steam} = \dfrac{\Delta W_w \times L}{\eta_{th} \times L} = \dfrac{\Delta W_w}{\eta_{th}}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wheat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wheat** (*Triticum aestivum* and related species) is a monocotyledonous cereal grass belonging to the family **Poaceae (Gramineae)** and the genus *Triticum*. It is the world's most widely cultivated staple food crop, providing approximately **20% of the total dietary calories and protein** for the global population. Botanically, the wheat grain is a **caryopsis** — a dry, single-seeded fruit in which the seed coat (pericarp) is fused with the fruit wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT747 / Module 3):**
> "Wheat — origin, classification, structure, chemical composition, varieties, milling, flour grades and quality, products (bread, biscuits, pasta, semolina), and storage."

From a **food-processing engineering** standpoint, wheat is the principal raw material for producing **flour, semolina, bran, germ, and a vast array of value-added baked goods** such as bread, biscuits, cakes, noodles, and pasta.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a wheat kernel as a **tiny, naturally packaged three-layer lunch box**:

- 🥣 **The Bran (Outer shell)** — the cardboard box: rich in fiber, vitamins (B-complex), minerals, and antioxidants. It is the protective outer layer.
- 🍚 **The Endosperm (Middle bulk)** — the rice inside the box: starchy, energy-rich tissue containing **~70–75% starch** and **~10–14% protein (gluten)**. This is the part humans mostly eat as white flour.
- 🌱 **The Germ (Tiny seed embryo)** — the future plant tucked into the corner: full of healthy fats, vitamin E, and enzymes. If left in flour, it reduces shelf-life (because the oils go rancid).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Milling is essentially the engineered process of carefully "unpacking this lunch box" — separating the bran and germ from the endosperm to obtain refined white flour of consistent quality.

---

## 1.3 Key Botanical & Industrial Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Metric | Remark |
|---|---|---|
| **Moisture content (safe storage)** | **< 14%** | Critical for preventing fungal/mycotoxin growth |
| **Kernel weight (1000-kernel)** | **30–50 g** | Indicator of milling yield |
| **Test weight (hectolitre mass)** | **> 76 kg/hL** | Premium milling grade |
| **Gluten content (wet basis)** | **25–35%** | Bread wheat benchmark |
| **Protein content (whole grain)** | **10–15%** | On dry basis |
| **Extraction rate (refined flour)** | **70–72%** | Straight-grade white flour |
| **Hard vitreous kernels (HVK)** | **> 75%** | Durum wheat quality index |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** Every food engineer working with wheat must internalize these metrics because **payment to farmers, flour pricing, and end-product quality all depend on them**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wheat (Taxonomic + Industrial)

### 2.1.1 Botanical Classification

$$\text{Kingdom: Plantae} \rightarrow \text{Division: Magnoliophyta} \rightarrow \text{Class: Liliopsida} \rightarrow \text{Family: Poaceae} \rightarrow \text{Genus: Triticum}$$

### 2.1.2 Industrial / Commercial Classification

| Class | Species | Protein / Gluten | Primary Use | Kernel Texture |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bread Wheat (Common)** | *T. aestivum* | 10–14% (strong gluten) | Bread, chapati, biscuits | Hard / Soft |
| **Durum Wheat (Macaroni)** | *T. durum* | 12–15% (very strong) | Semolina, pasta, couscous | Extra-hard, vitreous |
| **Club Wheat** | *T. compactum* | 8–11% (weak) | Cakes, pastries, crackers | Soft |
| **Spelt / Emmer** | *T. spelta / T. dicoccum* | 12–17% | Specialty health foods | Hard, hulled |

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of the Wheat Kernel — A Layer-by-Layer Engineering View

The wheat caryopsis is structurally composed of **three major morphological regions** and several sub-layers. Each fraction has distinct chemical and functional properties.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PERICARP (Fruit coat)        — ~5% kernel wt.   │
│  TESTA (Seed coat)            — Pigmented layer  │
│  ALEURONE LAYER               — ~7% (rich protein, enzyme) │
│  STARCHY ENDOSPERM            — ~80–85% (starch + gluten) │
│  GERM (Embryo)                — ~2.5–3% (lipid-rich) │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2.1 Detailed Structural Sub-Layers (Outside → Inside)

1. **Pericarp** — fibrous outer husk.
2. **Testa** — pigmented (red/white) seed coat; controls bran color.
3. **Nucellar epidermis** — thin layer beneath testa.
4. **Aleurone layer** — single cell layer, rich in protein, minerals, B-vitamins, enzymes (phytase, lipase).
5. **Starchy endosperm** — bulk of the kernel; contains **large & small starch granules** embedded in a **gluten protein matrix**.
6. **Germ (scutellum + embryo)** — high lipid (~10%), vitamin E, and enzymatic activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aleurone vs Endosperm:** During roller milling, the aleurone is generally separated into the bran stream, even though nutritionally it is rich in protein — this is why refined flour loses nutritional density.

---

## 2.3 Chemical Composition of Whole Wheat Kernel

The proximate composition determines milling strategy, nutritional labeling, and end-product behavior.

$$\text{Whole Wheat (dry basis)} = \text{Starch} + \text{Protein} + \text{Lipid} + \text{Fiber} + \text{Minerals} + \text{Moisture}$$

| Constituent | Whole Kernel (%) | Endosperm (%) | Bran (%) | Germ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Starch** | 60–75 | 70–82 | ~10 | ~30 |
| **Protein** | 10–15 | 8–13 | 14–18 | 22–28 |
| **Lipid (Fat)** | 2–3 | 0.7–1.5 | 4–6 | 8–12 |
| **Crude Fiber** | 2–3 | 0.3–0.5 | 10–15 | 2–3 |
| **Ash (Minerals)** | 1.5–2.0 | 0.3–0.5 | 5–8 | 4–5 |
| **Moisture** | 12–14 | 12–14 | 12–14 | 12–14 |

### 2.3.1 Wheat Proteins — The Gluten Complex (Engineering Significance)

Wheat is unique among cereals because its storage proteins form a **viscoelastic dough** when hydrated and mechanically worked. Two protein families are responsible:

- **Glutenins** → provide **strength & elasticity** (responsible for dough stability and gas retention).
- **Gliadins** → provide **extensibility & viscosity** (allow dough to stretch without rupturing).

$$\text{Wet Gluten} \approx 2 \times \text{Dry Gluten} \quad \text{(empirical ratio used in lab testing)}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU high-yield** — they appear frequently in derivation, numerical, and short-answer questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Extraction Rate (Flour Yield) | $E = \dfrac{M_{\text{flour}}}{M_{\text{wheat cleaned}}} \times 100$ | Straight-grade: **70–72%**; Wholemeal: **100%** |
| 2 | Test Weight (Bulk Density) | $\rho_b = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | **> 76 kg/hL** = premium grade |
| 3 | 1000-Kernel Weight | $W_{1000} = \dfrac{M_{\text{grains}}}{N_{\text{grains}}} \times 1000$ | Typical: **30–50 g** |
| 4 | Moisture Wet Basis (w.b.) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | Safe storage: **< 14%** |
| 5 | Moisture Dry Basis (d.b.) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ |
| 6 | Conversion w.b. ↔ d.b. | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$ | Use for drying/milling mass balance |
| 7 | Hard Vitreous Kernels (HVK) | $HVK = \dfrac{N_{\text{vitreous}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Durum: **> 75%** |
| 8 | Gluten Yield (Wet) | $YG_w = \dfrac{m_{\text{wet gluten}}}{m_{\text{flour}}} \times 100$ | Bread flour: **25–35%** |
| 9 | Ash Content (Flour Grade) | $A = \dfrac{m_{\text{ash}}}{m_{\text{dry flour}}} \times 100$ | Patent flour: **< 0.55%** |
| 10 | Protein Conversion | $N_{\text{protein}} = N \times 5.7$ | Wheat-specific N factor (Kjeldahl) |
| 11 | Flour Stream Mass Balance | $\sum m_{\text{streams}} = m_{\text{clean wheat}} - m_{\text{losses}}$ | Energy + mass balance |
| 12 | Bread Loaf Volume | $V_{\text{loaf}} \propto (\text{Gluten quality}) \times (\text{CO}_2 \text{ retention})$ | Measured by **rapeseed displacement** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion note:** Use factor **5.7 (not 6.25)** for wheat protein because wheat proteins are notably glutamic-acid rich with a different nitrogen-to-protein conversion coefficient.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Concepts in Engineering

- **Baking Industry** → Gluten quality dictates dough rheology and loaf volume.
- **Pasta Industry** → Durum wheat HVK index governs semolina yield and cooking quality.
- **Storage Engineering** → Moisture & temperature psychrometric control prevents **Aspergillus**-produced aflatoxins.
- **Flour Fortification Programs** → WHO/FSSAI mandate iron, folic acid, vitamin B12 addition; mass balance determines feeder rate.
- **Animal Feed** → Off-spec bran & germ fractions become feed — economics depends on accurate extraction rates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Extraction Rate & Mass Balance Across a Roller Mill

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A roller flour mill receives **10,000 kg/hr of cleaned wheat** at 14% moisture (wet basis). The mill produces the following streams:

- **Patent flour:** 5,500 kg/hr at 14.5% moisture
- **Second clear flour:** 1,400 kg/hr at 14.0% moisture
- **Bran:** 1,700 kg/hr at 13.5% moisture
- **Germ:** 250 kg/hr at 12.0% moisture
- **Shorts (fine bran + germ mix):** 900 kg/hr at 13.5% moisture
- **Unaccounted loss:** ?

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Total Output Stream Summation**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{out}} &= m_{\text{patent}} + m_{\text{second clear}} + m_{\text{bran}} + m_{\text{germ}} + m_{\text{shorts}} \\
&= 5500 + 1400 + 1700 + 250 + 900 \\
&= 9750 \ \text{kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Mass Balance to Determine Loss**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{loss}} &= m_{\text{in}} - m_{\text{out}} \\
&= 10000 - 9750 \\
&= 250 \ \text{kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Extraction Rate of Total Flour**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total flour}} &= \dfrac{m_{\text{patent}} + m_{\text{second clear}}}{m_{\text{cleaned wheat}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{5500 + 1400}{10000} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{6900}{10000} \times 100 = 69.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to Dry Basis (Engineering Significance)**

For mass balance on the dry solid (since moisture enters/exits independently):

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{dry, in}} &= 10000 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{14}{100}\right) = 10000 \times 0.86 = 8600 \ \text{kg/hr} \\
m_{\text{dry, patent}} &= 5500 \times 0.855 = 4702.5 \ \text{kg/hr} \\
m_{\text{dry, second}} &= 1400 \times 0.86 = 1204 \ \text{kg/hr} \\
m_{\text{dry, bran}} &= 1700 \times 0.865 = 1470.5 \ \text{kg/hr} \\
m_{\text{dry, germ}} &= 250 \times 0.88 = 220 \ \text{kg/hr} \\
m_{\text{dry, shorts}} &= 900 \times 0.865 = 778.5 \ \text{kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{dry, out}} &= 4702.5 + 1204 + 1470.5 + 220 + 778.5 = 8375.5 \ \text{kg/hr} \\
\text{Dry loss} &= 8600 - 8375.5 = 224.5 \ \text{kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Overall Mass Closure**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Closing error} &= \dfrac{\text{Dry loss}}{m_{\text{dry, in}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{224.5}{8600} \times 100 \approx 2.61\% \ \ (\text{acceptable for industrial mill} < 3\%)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Losses of 2–3% in a roller mill are typical and arise from (a) dust aspiration, (b) microbial respiration, (c) sampling, and (d) thermal evaporation at rollers. Anything above 5% indicates inefficient separation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Wet Gluten Yield from Flour

A flour sample of mass **m_flour = 10 g** is washed with 2% NaCl solution following the **ICC 137/1 method**. After washing, the wet gluten mass is **m_wet = 2.85 g**, and after drying at 105 °C it becomes **m_dry = 1.05 g**.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Step 1 — Wet Gluten Content (w.b.)**

$$YG_w = \dfrac{m_{\text{wet gluten}}}{m_{\text{flour}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.85}{10} \times 100 = 28.5\%$$

**Step 2 — Dry Gluten Content**

$$YG_d = \dfrac{1.05}{10} \times 100 = 10.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Gluten Hydration Capacity (Quality Index)**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{gluten}} &= \dfrac{m_{\text{wet}} - m_{\text{dry}}}{m_{\text{dry}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{2.85 - 1.05}{1.05} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1.80}{1.05} \times 100 \approx 171.4\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Quality Classification**

| Hydration Capacity | Quality Class |
|---|---|
| **< 150%** | Weak gluten (cake flour) |
| **150–200%** | Medium (all-purpose) |
| **> 200%** | Strong (bread flour) |

Our computed **171.4%** → **Medium-strong flour**, suitable for bread & chapati.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Wheat Quality Parameters vs End Use

| Quality Parameter | Test Method | Bread Wheat | Biscuit Wheat | Durum (Pasta) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Protein content | Kjeldahl (N × 5.7) | **12–14%** | **8–10%** | **12–15%** |
| Wet gluten | Glutomatic | **30–35%** | **20–25%** | **30–40%** |
| Test weight | Hectolitre balance | **> 76 kg/hL** | **> 75 kg/hL** | **> 78 kg/hL** |
| HVK | Visual cut test | — | — | **> 75%** |
| Falling Number | Hagberg-Perten | **> 250 s** | **> 250 s** | **> 300 s** |
| Ash (flour) | Muffle furnace (550 °C) | **< 0.65%** | **< 0.50%** | **< 0.80%** |
| Moisture | Oven (105 °C) | **< 14%** | **< 14%** | **< 13.5%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Falling Number (FN)** is the time (seconds) for a stirrer to fall through a hot flour gel. **Low FN = sprouted wheat = high α-amylase activity = sticky crumb.** KTU examiners frequently ask about FN because it directly affects storage decisions.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Wheat Drying Energy Requirement (Psychrometric Engineering)

Wheat enters a continuous dryer at **m_1 = 1000 kg/hr** with **M_1 = 18% moisture (w.b.)** and leaves at **M_2 = 13% (w.b.)**.

### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Convert to Dry Basis**

$$M_{db,1} = \dfrac{0.18}{1 - 0.18} = 0.2195 = 21.95\%$$

$$M_{db,2} = \dfrac{0.13}{1 - 0.13} = 0.1494 = 14.94\%$$

**Step 2 — Dry Solid Mass (Invariant)**

$$m_{\text{dry}} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.18) = 820 \ \text{kg dry matter/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Final Wet Mass**

$$m_2 = \dfrac{m_{\text{dry}}}{1 - M_2} = \dfrac{820}{0.87} = 942.5 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Water to be Removed**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{water}} &= m_1 - m_2 \\
&= 1000 - 942.5 = 57.5 \ \text{kg water/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Energy Estimate (Assuming Latent Heat Only)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= m_{\text{water}} \times L_v \\
Q &= 57.5 \times 2257 \ \text{kJ/hr} \\
Q &\approx 129{,}778 \ \text{kJ/hr} \approx 36.05 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real plant consideration:** Actual energy is 1.2–1.5× this value due to sensible heating of grain and inefficiencies. Engineers design dryers with **> 40 kW** capacity and add **15% safety factor**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wheat Kernel Anatomy — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram maps how the **3 anatomical regions** of the wheat kernel map to the **4 milling fractions** produced in industry. Each block is annotated with composition and downstream use.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KERNEL INPUT: Cleaned Wheat Grain 100 kg] --> B[Outer Bran Layers]
    A --> C[Aleurone Plus Sub-aleurone]
    A --> D[Starchy Endosperm]
    A --> E[Germ Plus Scutellum]

    B --> B1[Pericarp and Testa]
    B1 --> B2[Composition: Cellulose, Pentosans, Lignin]
    B2 --> B3[Industrial Stream: BRAN ~ 14 percent]

    C --> C1[Composition: Protein 32 percent, Minerals, B Vitamins]
    C1 --> C2[Industrial Stream: BRAN FRACTION Merged With B3]

    D --> D1[Composition: Starch 82 percent, Gluten 12 percent]
    D1 --> D2{Industrial Stream Decision}
    D2 --> D3[PATENT FLOUR ~ 45 percent]
    D2 --> D4[SECOND CLEAR ~ 18 percent]
    D2 --> D5[STRAIGHT RUN FLOUR ~ 70 percent]

    E --> E1[Composition: Lipid 10 percent, Vitamin E, Sugars]
    E1 --> E2[Industrial Stream: GERM ~ 2.5 percent]

    B3 --> F1[Stream: Animal Feed and Breakfast Cereal]
    C2 --> F1
    E2 --> F2[Stream: Germ Oil Extraction and Nutraceuticals]
    D3 --> F3[Stream: Bread and Premium Bakery]
    D4 --> F4[Stream: Biscuits, Noodles, and Gravy Thickener]
    D5 --> F5[Stream: General Purpose Bakery and RTE Foods]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The chart emphasizes that **one cleaned wheat input yields four value-added streams**, but the highest economic return comes from clean **patent flour separation**.

---

## 4.2 Wheat Milling Process — Roller Mill Block Diagram

This functional architecture diagram represents the **sequential unit operations** of an industrial roller flour mill, from receiving to finished flour bagging.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step A: Receiving and Weighbridge] --> S2[Step B: Pre-cleaning and Magnetic Separation]
    S2 --> S3[Step C: Destoning and Gravity Separator]
    S3 --> S4[Step D: Scourer and Aspirator]
    S4 --> S5[Step E: Conditioning and Tempering 14 to 16 percent Moisture]
    S5 --> S6[Step F: First Break Roll B1]
    S6 --> S7[Step G: Sifting and Grading Through Plansifters]
    S7 --> S8{Step H: Quality Decision Node}
    S8 --> S9[Step I: Reduction Rolls R1 to R5]
    S8 --> S10[Step J: Second Break B2 B3 B4 B5]
    S10 --> S9
    S9 --> S11[Step K: Final Sifting and Air Classification]
    S11 --> S12[Step L: Flour Blender and Fortification Feeder]
    S12 --> S13[Step M: Storage Silos]
    S13 --> S14[Step N: Automatic Bagging and Dispatch]
    S11 --> S15[By-Product: Bran and Germ Pneumatic Conveyors]
```

### 4.2.1 Operational Significance of Each Stage

| Stage | Function | Engineering Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| A | Weighbridge accounting | Mass flow per truck |
| B | Remove ferrous metals, straw, stones | Magnetic field strength |
| C | Destone by density | Airflow velocity |
| D | Scour outer dust, fungi spores | Brush rpm |
| E | **Tempering** to 14–16% moisture | Time: **8–24 h**; **critical for bran–endosperm separation** |
| F | First break — opens kernel | Roll gap: **0.6–0.8 mm**; differential **2.5:1** |
| G | Plansifters grade by particle size | Mesh sizes **132–450 µm** |
| I | Smooth rolls progressively reduce endosperm | Roll gap narrows 0.4 → 0.1 mm |
| L | Iron, folic acid fortification | Feeder accuracy ± 1% |

---

## 4.3 Wheat-to-Product Value Chain

This schematic illustrates the complete **value chain** from raw wheat grain to finished consumer product — emphasizing the engineering interventions at each step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Field: Wheat Cultivation] --> A2[Harvest: Moisture 18 to 22 percent]
    A2 --> A3[Post-Harvest Drying to 14 percent]
    A3 --> A4[Storage Silos with Aeration]
    A4 --> A5[Transport to Roller Mill]
    A5 --> A6[Cleaning and Tempering]
    A6 --> A7[Milling and Fractionation]
    A7 --> A8a[Flour Stream]
    A7 --> A8b[Bran Stream]
    A7 --> A8c[Germ Stream]
    A8a --> P1[Bread Bakery]
    A8a --> P2[Biscuit and Cookie Plant]
    A8a --> P3[Pasta Extrusion Line]
    A8a --> P4[Ready to Eat Cereal]
    A8b --> P5[Breakfast Bran Cereal]
    A8b --> P6[Animal Feed Pelletizer]
    A8c --> P7[Wheat Germ Oil Extraction]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** When asked to "draw a flow diagram of wheat processing," always show **(i) tempering, (ii) break rolls, (iii) reduction rolls, (iv) plansifters, (v) flour blending & fortification, (vi) by-product utilization.** Skipping tempering is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Q.** Define the term **"test weight"** of wheat. State its significance and minimum acceptable value for premium milling grade.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Test weight (also called hectolitre mass or specific weight) is defined as the mass of wheat grains occupying a fixed volume of **one hectolitre (100 L)** under standard packing conditions. It is determined using the **Schopper chondrometer or standard test weight apparatus**.

- **Significance:** It is a proxy for grain density, kernel plumpness, and milling yield. High test weight → better endosperm-to-bran ratio → higher flour extraction.
- **Standard value:** For premium milling-grade bread wheat, test weight must be **> 76 kg/hL** as per FCI/BIS specifications.
- **Low test weight (< 70 kg/hL)** indicates shriveled kernels (drought stress, frost damage) leading to lower extraction rates.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for significance, 1 mark for numeric value.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Q.** Differentiate between **hard wheat** and **soft wheat** in terms of endosperm texture, gluten content, and end use.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Hard Wheat | Soft Wheat |
|---|---|---|
| Endosperm texture | **Vitreous, translucent, tightly bound starch-protein matrix** | **Mealy, opaque, loosely packed** |
| Protein content | **11–15%** (strong gluten) | **8–10%** (weak gluten) |
| Starch damage during milling | High | Low |
| End use | Bread, chapati, hard rolls | Cakes, biscuits, pastries |
| Example varieties | HD-2967, Lok-1, Sonalika | Himgiri, NP-200 |

*(1 mark per row, max 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1(A) — Internal Choice Option (a) [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q.** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete roller milling process** of wheat. Explain the role of **tempering** and **break rolls** in achieving high flour extraction.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks: Milling Process Flow**

**Model Answer:**

The roller flour milling process converts cleaned wheat into graded flour and by-products through a series of **gradual reduction and separation** steps. The principal unit operations are:

1. **Receiving and Pre-cleaning (1 mark):** Wheat is unloaded via weighbridge, passed through magnetic separators to remove ferrous metals, then through vibrating screens to remove straw and large impurities.
2. **Destoning and Scouring (1 mark):** Specific gravity separators remove stones of similar size but higher density. Scourers use rotating beaters and air aspiration to remove surface dust, microbial spores, and loose hairs from the brush end of the kernel.
3. **Tempering / Conditioning (1 mark):** Water is added to raise moisture to **15–16%**, followed by **8–24 hours rest** in tempering bins. Hard wheat receives longer tempering than soft wheat.
4. **Break System (B1 → B5) (2 marks):** Five break rolls with progressively finer corrugations and tighter gaps fracture the kernel and scrape endosperm away from bran. Each break stage is followed by plansifters grading by particle size.
5. **Reduction System (R1 → R5) (1 mark):** Smooth rolls progressively grind the purified endosperm particles into fine flour, again followed by sieving.
6. **Flour Blending and Fortification (1 mark):** Multiple flour streams are pneumatically blended to target ash and protein specs; iron, folic acid, vitamin B12 are dosed per FSSAI 2020 norms.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks: Role of Tempering and Break Rolls**

**Role of Tempering:**

1. **Moisture Migration (2 marks):** Water penetrates the bran layers, making them **tough and pliable** while loosening the endosperm–bran adhesion. This prevents bran from fragmenting into tiny dark specks in the flour.
2. **Milling Efficiency (2 marks):** Toughened bran resists breakage → fewer bran particles contaminate flour → **higher grade flour with lower ash (< 0.55%)** is obtained.
3. **Optimization (1 mark):** Hard wheat: temper to **16%** for 18–24 h; soft wheat: **14.5%** for 8–12 h.

**Role of Break Rolls:**

1. **Progressive Fracture (1 mark):** Each successive break roll has **flatter corrugations** and a **smaller gap**, allowing controlled opening of the kernel rather than crushing.
2. **Differential Speed (1 mark):** Rolls rotate at **2.5:1 differential**, providing a **shearing/scraping action** that releases endosperm cleanly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - ❌ Do NOT confuse **break rolls (corrugated)** with **reduction rolls (smooth)** — both serve distinct purposes.
> - ❌ Do NOT skip tempering in your flow diagram — examiners allocate **1 dedicated mark** for it.
> - ❌ Do NOT write the answer as a paragraph without a block diagram — always include the **flow chart** for full marks.

---

### Question B1(B) — Internal Choice Option (b) [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q.** Explain the **chemical composition of wheat** in detail. How do **glutenin and gliadin** proteins differ, and how do they jointly contribute to **bread-making quality**?

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks: Chemical Composition**

**Model Answer:**

Whole wheat grain contains (on dry basis):
- **Starch — 60–75% (1 mark):** Composed of amylose (~25%) and amylopectin (~75%). Two granule populations exist: large lenticular (A-type, 25–40 µm) and small spherical (B-type, 5–10 µm).
- **Protein — 10–15% (1 mark):** Classified into four Osborne fractions: albumins (water-soluble), globulins (salt-soluble), **gliadins (70% ethanol-soluble)**, and **glutenins (acid/alkali-soluble)**.
- **Lipids — 2–3% (1 mark):** Predominantly non-polar triglycerides in germ; polar lipids in endosperm contribute to gas-cell stability in dough.
- **Crude Fiber — 2–3% (1 mark):** Mostly arabinoxylans and β-glucan in cell walls.
- **Minerals (Ash) — 1.5–2% (1 mark):** Concentrated in bran; phytic acid chelates minerals.
- **Vitamins (1 mark):** B-complex (thiamine, niacin, riboflavin) and vitamin E (in germ).

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks: Glutenin vs Gliadin and Bread Quality**

| Property | Glutenin | Gliadin |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | **High (100,000 – several million Da)** — polymeric | **Low (30,000 – 75,000 Da)** — monomeric |
| Structure | Disulfide-bonded polymer chain | Single polypeptide, intramolecular S-S bonds |
| Functional role | **Elasticity & strength** | **Extensibility & viscosity** |
| Dough behavior | Provides "rubbery" recovery | Allows dough to stretch and flow |

**Joint Role in Bread-Making (5 marks):**

1. **Hydration (1 mark):** Upon water addition, glutenins and gliadins form a continuous viscoelastic network — the gluten matrix.
2. **Dough Development (1 mark):** Mechanical mixing aligns the protein chains; disulfide bonds form and reform, creating an elastic structure.
3. **Gas Retention (1 mark):** During fermentation, CO₂ from yeast is trapped in the gluten film, causing dough to rise.
4. **Oven Spring (1 mark):** At oven temperature, gas expansion + starch gelatinization sets the crumb structure.
5. **Final Loaf Volume (1 mark):** Strength-to-extensibility ratio (P/L value from alveograph) determines the loaf volume: too strong → resistant dough → small loaf; too weak → collapse.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - ❌ **Do NOT state "gluten is a single protein"** — it is a complex of two protein families.
> - ❌ **Do NOT confuse gliadin with glutenin** in functional roles — this is a 2-mark loser.
> - ❌ **Do NOT forget the Kjeldahl factor 5.7** when calculating protein — generic factor 6.25 is wrong for wheat.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wheat (KTU PECHT747 / Module 3)**

### 🌾 Botanical & Compositional
- ✅ Wheat = caryopsis (Poaceae family); species: *T. aestivum* (bread), *T. durum* (pasta).
- ✅ Three anatomical regions: **Bran (14%)**, **Endosperm (83%)**, **Germ (3%)**.
- ✅ Key proximate composition: starch 60–75%, protein 10–15%, lipid 2–3%, ash 1.5–2%.
- ✅ Wheat-specific protein factor = **5.7** (not 6.25).

### 🌾 Quality Parameters
- ✅ Test weight **> 76 kg/hL** = premium.
- ✅ 1000-kernel weight **30–50 g**.
- ✅ Wet gluten: bread flour **25–35%**.
- ✅ Safe storage moisture **< 14%**.
- ✅ Hard Vitreous Kernels (HVK) **> 75%** for durum.
- ✅ Falling Number **> 250 s** (low FN → sprouted → poor crumb).
- ✅ Ash content of patent flour **< 0.55%**.

### 🌾 Engineering
- ✅ Tempering: 14–16% moisture for 8–24 h — toughens bran, prevents flour contamination.
- ✅ Break rolls: corrugated, differential 2.5:1; reduce gradually (B1 → B5).
- ✅ Reduction rolls: smooth rolls, narrower gap; reduce endosperm to flour.
- ✅ Extraction rates: Patent ~45%, Straight-grade ~70%, Wholemeal 100%.
- ✅ Flour fortification (FSSAI 2020): Iron + Folic Acid + Vitamin B12 mandatory.

### 🌾 Gluten Functionality
- ✅ Glutenins → elasticity & strength.
- ✅ Gliadins → extensibility & flow.
- ✅ Joint action → viscoelastic dough → gas retention → oven spring → loaf volume.

### 🌾 Common Pitfalls (Avoid These in Exams)
- ❌ Mixing up **glutenin and gliadin** functional roles.
- ❌ Forgetting the **N × 5.7** conversion.
- ❌ Confusing **wet basis** vs **dry basis** moisture in drying calculations.
- ❌ Skipping **tempering** in the milling flow diagram.
- ❌ Stating that wheat protein factor is **6.25** (that's for general food).
- ❌ Claiming **"gluten is bad for everyone"** — celiac patients are the affected group only.
- ❌ Confusing **Falling Number** direction (lower = worse, not better).

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory anchor:** *"Bran protects, Endosperm feeds, Germ grows — milling separates, gluten transforms."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bread - Pulses - Vegetables - Spices - Microbial Decontamination of Spices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bread - Pulses - Vegetables - Spices - Microbial Decontamination of Spices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bread — Definition & Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Bread is a baked product manufactured from a dough made of wheat flour, water, salt, and a leavening agent (yeast or chemical), characterized by a porous, aerated crumb structure formed by the retention of carbon dioxide gas during fermentation and baking.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of bread-making as **building a house of bubbles**. The gluten protein network in wheat flour acts like a *scaffolding*, while yeast acts as a *tiny balloon-inflating crew* — they release $\text{CO}_2$ that gets trapped inside this scaffold. When heated, the scaffold solidifies (starch gelatinization and protein denaturation), leaving behind the airy structure we recognize as bread.

**Standard Metric (KTU Highlight):**

- **Moisture content of fresh bread:** $\mathbf{35\text{–}40\%}$ (wb)
- **pH of bread crumb:** $\mathbf{5.0\text{–}6.0}$
- **Specific loaf volume (SLV):** $\geq \mathbf{6.5 \text{ cc/g}}$ (high-quality bread)

---

## 1.2 Pulses — Definition & Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Pulses are the dried, edible seeds of plants belonging to the family **Leguminosae (Fabaceae)**, harvested solely for their dry grain. They are characterized by a high protein content ($\mathbf{20\text{–}30\%}$), low fat, and a two-layered seed coat (hull) tightly adhered to the cotyledon.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Pulses are like **tiny nature-sealed protein capsules**. They protect their protein-rich interior with a tough, fibrous hull. However, humans cannot digest this hull — so pulse processing is essentially the act of *unwrapping* this natural capsule to access the protein inside, while simultaneously removing anti-nutritional factors (ANFs) like trypsin inhibitors and phytic acid.

**Key Standard Metrics:**

- Protein content: $\mathbf{20\text{–}30\%}$
- Moisture (safe storage): $\leq \mathbf{12\%}$
- Dehusking efficiency: $\mathbf{85\text{–}95\%}$ (target industry benchmark)

---

## 1.3 Vegetables — Definition & Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Vegetables are the edible parts of herbaceous plants (roots, stems, leaves, fruits, flowers) that are consumed either raw or after minimal processing. They are perishable, high-moisture ($\mathbf{80\text{–}95\%}$ water) commodities that undergo rapid post-harvest deterioration.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a vegetable as a **living battery that is slowly self-discharging**. After harvest, the cells continue to respire, consume oxygen, and break down tissues. Processing acts as a *pause button* — by removing water (drying), lowering temperature (freezing), or adding preservatives (pickling), we slow the discharge rate to nearly zero.

---

## 1.4 Spices — Definition & Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Spices are dried aromatic substances obtained from the vegetative parts (bark, roots, leaves, flower buds, fruits, seeds) of tropical plants, used primarily to flavor, color, or preserve food. The term excludes herbaceous leaves (which are classified as herbs).*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Spices are the **perfume factories of the plant kingdom**. Each spice contains volatile essential oils (eugenol in clove, piperine in pepper, curcumin in turmeric) that are highly susceptible to microbial contamination due to their open-field harvesting and sun-drying on the ground.

**Key Standard Metric:**

- Microbial load in raw spices: $\mathbf{10^5\text{–}10^7 \text{ CFU/g}}$ (often exceeds food safety limits)

---

## 1.5 Microbial Decontamination of Spices — Definition & Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Microbial decontamination of spices refers to a controlled physical or chemical treatment applied post-harvest to reduce the natural microbial load (bacteria, yeasts, moulds, and spore-formers like **Bacillus** and **Clostridium**) to levels compliant with food safety standards (e.g., $\leq \mathbf{10^4 \text{ CFU/g}}$ for total plate count), without significantly altering the spice's volatile oil profile, color, or flavor.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of decontamination as a **spa treatment for spices** — gentle enough to preserve the *fragrance* (essential oils) but powerful enough to evict the *unwanted guests* (microbes). The challenge lies in choosing the right "spa method" that disinfects without destroying the very essence that makes the spice valuable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** *This entire module aligns with **CO3** — "Apply the principles of food processing to the manufacture of bakery, pulse, vegetable, and spice products." Cognitive level: **Apply / Analyze** (Bloom's Level III–IV).*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Twelve Steps of Bread Making (KTU High-Yield)

The industrial bread-making process is universally tested in KTU exams. Master the sequence:

1. **Selection & Weighing** — Hard wheat flour ($\mathbf{11\text{–}13\%}$ protein), yeast ($\mathbf{2\%}$ of flour weight), salt ($\mathbf{1.5\text{–}2\%}$), sugar ($\mathbf{5\text{–}8\%}$), shortening ($\mathbf{2\text{–}3\%}$).
2. **Sieving** — Aerates flour and removes lumps.
3. **Mixing** — Hydration of flour; gluten network begins to form.
4. **Kneading** — Develops gluten (15–20 min); window-pane test indicates proper gluten development.
5. **Fermentation (Bulk)** — Yeast metabolizes sugars via:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$
6. **Punching & Folding** — Redistributes yeast, expels excess $\text{CO}_2$, equalizes temperature.
7. **Division & Rounding** — Divides dough into uniform pieces.
8. **Intermediate Proofing** — 10–15 min rest.
9. **Moulding & Panning** — Shapes the loaf.
10. **Final Proofing** — $\mathbf{35\text{–}40°C}$, $\mathbf{85\text{–}90\% RH}$, 45–60 min; dough doubles in volume.
11. **Baking** — $\mathbf{200\text{–}230°C}$ for $\mathbf{25\text{–}45 min}$. The oven-spring and crust formation occur here.
12. **Cooling & Slicing** — Cooling on racks to prevent crumb gumminess; moisture equilibrates.

**The "Why" Behind Each Key Step:**

- *Why knead?* — To align gluten strands into a continuous viscoelastic film.
- *Why punch?* — To prevent over-acidification and collapse of gas cells.
- *Why bake at 200°C+?* — Above $\mathbf{60°C}$, yeast dies; above $\mathbf{74°C}$, starch gelatinizes; above $\mathbf{100°C}$, crust forms via Maillard browning.

---

## 2.2 Pulse Processing Flow — Theoretical Breakdown

| Stage | Unit Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Cleaning | Air-classifier, destoner | Removes stones, dust, chaff |
| 2. Pitting / Scarification | Mechanical / chemical (oil + water) | Loosens husk from cotyledon |
| 3. Dehusking | Roller / abrasive / under-runner disk | Removes outer husk |
| 4. Splitting | Disc splitter | Splits cotyledon into *dal* |
| 5. Polishing | Polishing cone / emery | Improves appearance |
| 6. Grading | Sieve-based | Size classification |
| 7. Packaging | Burlap / PP bags | Market-ready form |

**Pretreatments to Improve Dehusking Efficiency:**

- **Water soaking:** $4\text{–}8$ hours at room temperature.
- **Oil + water treatment:** $\mathbf{0.5\text{–}1.0\%}$ oil + $\mathbf{2\text{–}4\%}$ water for $5\text{–}10$ min.
- **Hot water / lye treatment:** $1\text{–}2\%$ NaOH solution, $90\text{–}95°C$, $2\text{–}5$ min.

---

## 2.3 Vegetable Processing — Key Preservation Principles

The **Hurdle Technology** concept applies — combining mild preservation techniques to inactivate microbes without destroying quality:

$$F_T^{\text{process}} = \int_0^t 10^{\frac{T(t) - T_{\text{ref}}}{z}} \, dt$$

Where:
- $F_T$ = sterilization value (min)
- $T(t)$ = temperature profile
- $T_{\text{ref}} = \mathbf{121.1°C}$
- $z = \mathbf{10°C}$ (for *Clostridium botulinum*)

**Common Unit Operations for Vegetables:**

- **Blanching:** $80\text{–}100°C$ for $\mathbf{2\text{–}5 min}$; inactivates enzymes (PPO, POD).
- **Canning:** Thermal sterilization to commercial sterility.
- **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing):** $\leq \mathbf{-30°C}$ with fast air blast; minimizes ice crystal damage.
- **Dehydration:** Hot-air drying, freeze-drying, osmotic dehydration.
- **Pickling:** Fermentation in brine (Lactic acid bacteria); pH $\leq \mathbf{4.6}$.

---

## 2.4 Spice Processing — The Post-Harvest Pipeline

```
Harvesting → Field Drying → Cleaning → Sorting → 
Grading → Size Reduction (Grinding) → Sieving → 
Packing → Decontamination → Storage
```

**Critical Concern:** Field-drying spices on bare soil leads to **heavy microbial contamination** (often $> 10^7$ CFU/g). Spices also carry **aflatoxins** (Aspergillus flavus) — a serious KTU focus area.

---

## 2.5 Microbial Decontamination Methods — Comparative Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Core Topic:** The selection of a decontamination method is governed by the **dual constraint** of *microbial safety* AND *sensory preservation*.

| Method | Principle | Microbial Reduction | Quality Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steam Pasteurization** | Moist heat kills vegetative cells | $\mathbf{3\text{–}5 \log}$ | Modest color loss |
| **Gamma Irradiation** | Ionizing radiation damages DNA | $\mathbf{5\text{–}8 \log}$ | Dose-dependent; legal limits apply |
| **Electron Beam (e-beam)** | Same as gamma; non-radioactive | $\mathbf{4\text{–}7 \log}$ | Minimal color change |
| **Ethylene Oxide (EtO)** | Alkylation of proteins/DNA | $\mathbf{3\text{–}5 \log}$ | **Banned in EU**; residue toxicity |
| **Microwave (MW)** | Dielectric heating | $\mathbf{1\text{–}3 \log}$ | Uneven penetration |
| **Ozone Treatment** | Strong oxidizer | $\mathbf{2\text{–}4 \log}$ | Strong odor; degrades if overdosed |
| **Plasma (Cold)** | Reactive oxygen/nitrogen species | $\mathbf{3\text{–}6 \log}$ | Emerging technology |
| **Dry Heat Roasting** | Conductive heat | $\mathbf{1\text{–}3 \log}$ | Color and aroma changes |

**Dose Limit Reference (KTU Highlight):**

$$\boxed{D_{\text{max, India}} = 10 \text{ kGy for spices (FSSAI / Atomic Energy Regulatory Board)}}$$

> **Note (FSSAI 2011):** India permits irradiation of spices up to **10 kGy** for microbial decontamination.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{\text{wb}} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % |
| 2 | $M_{\text{db}} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % |
| 3 | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Water activity (spice stability) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $D_{\text{value}} = t_{10\times \text{ reduction}}$ | Decimal reduction time | min |
| 5 | $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{\frac{T-121.1}{z}}$ | Sterilization value (canning) | min |
| 6 | $\text{SLV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{loaf}}}{m_{\text{loaf}}}$ | Bread loaf quality | cc/g |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{dehusk}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{dal}}}{m_{\text{raw pulse}}} \times 100$ | Pulse dehusking efficiency | % |
| 8 | $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$ | Survivor curve (irradiation/heat) | CFU/g |
| 9 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature coefficient | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\log_{10} N = \log_{10} N_0 - \dfrac{D_{\text{abs}}}{D_{10}}$ | Microbial log-reduction (irradiation) | — |

> **Critical Pitfall:** In the **Survivor Curve** equation above, $\vert N_0 \vert$ written as plain $\lvert N_0 \rvert$ in the formula column. Use $\lvert N_0 \rvert$ or $N_0$ (always positive) — never use the literal `|` character inside markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Moisture Content Conversion (KTU Frequent Type)

**Problem (Model Q):** A sample of turmeric powder weighs $\mathbf{250 \text{ g}}$ in its wet state. After oven drying at $105°C$ to constant weight, the dry mass is found to be $\mathbf{218 \text{ g}}$. Calculate the moisture content on (a) wet basis, (b) dry basis, and (c) the mass of water that must be removed to bring it down to a safe moisture content of $\mathbf{10\% (wb)}$.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- Wet mass: $m_w = 250$ g
- Dry mass: $m_d = 218$ g

**Mass of moisture in sample:**
$$m_{\text{moisture}} = m_w - m_d = 250 - 218 = 32 \text{ g}$$

**(a) Moisture content on wet basis (wb):**
$$M_{\text{wb}} = \frac{m_{\text{moisture}}}{m_w} \times 100 = \frac{32}{250} \times 100$$
$$M_{\text{wb}} = \mathbf{12.8\%}$$

**(b) Moisture content on dry basis (db):**
$$M_{\text{db}} = \frac{m_{\text{moisture}}}{m_d} \times 100 = \frac{32}{218} \times 100$$
$$M_{\text{db}} = \mathbf{14.68\%}$$

**(c) Mass of water to remove to reach 10% (wb):**

Let the final mass of turmeric be $m_f$. At $10\%$ wb, moisture is $10\%$ of $m_f$ and dry matter is $90\%$ of $m_f$.

Since dry matter is conserved:
$$0.90 \cdot m_f = 218 \text{ g}$$
$$m_f = \frac{218}{0.90} = 242.22 \text{ g}$$

**Mass of water to remove:**
$$m_{\text{water, remove}} = m_w - m_f = 250 - 242.22 = \mathbf{7.78 \text{ g}}$$

**Verification (sanity check):**
$$M_{\text{wb, final}} = \frac{250 - 7.78 - 218}{250 - 7.78} = \frac{24.22}{242.22} \times 100 = 10.00\% \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Pulse Dehusking Efficiency (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A pulse mill processes $1000$ kg of whole green gram per day. The output consists of $780$ kg of dehusked split dal, $50$ kg of powder (brokens), and the rest is husk. Calculate (a) dehusking efficiency, (b) dal yield percentage, (c) total loss percentage.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Input mass: $m_{\text{raw}} = 1000$ kg
- Whole dal obtained: $m_{\text{dal}} = 780$ kg
- Brokens/powder: $m_{\text{powder}} = 50$ kg
- Husk: $m_{\text{husk}} = 1000 - 780 - 50 = 170$ kg

**(a) Dehusking efficiency** (mass of recovered dal as % of input):
$$\eta_{\text{dehusk}} = \frac{m_{\text{dal}}}{m_{\text{raw}}} \times 100 = \frac{780}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{78\%}$$

**(b) Dal yield** (recoverable product, dal + brokens):
$$\eta_{\text{yield}} = \frac{m_{\text{dal}} + m_{\text{powder}}}{m_{\text{raw}}} \times 100 = \frac{830}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{83\%}$$

**(c) Total loss** (husk + unaccounted):
$$\eta_{\text{loss}} = \frac{m_{\text{husk}}}{m_{\text{raw}}} \times 100 = \frac{170}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{17\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** A well-run pulse mill targets dehusking efficiency of $85\text{–}90\%$ and dal yield of $82\text{–}88\%$. A value of $78\%$ indicates significant mechanical damage or improper pretreatment (under-soaking or excessive moisture).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 3 — Microbial Log Reduction by Irradiation (KTU High-Yield)

**Problem:** A batch of black pepper has an initial microbial load of $\mathbf{5 \times 10^6 \text{ CFU/g}}$. It is irradiated with a dose of $\mathbf{8 \text{ kGy}}$. The $D_{10}$ value for the dominant spore-former (*Bacillus subtilis*) in black pepper is $\mathbf{1.5 \text{ kGy}}$. Calculate: (a) the log reduction, (b) the final microbial count, and (c) whether the spice is safe as per FSSAI standard ($\leq 10^4$ CFU/g).

### Solution

**Given:**
- $N_0 = 5 \times 10^6$ CFU/g
- $D_{\text{abs}} = 8$ kGy
- $D_{10} = 1.5$ kGy

**(a) Log reduction (in decades):**
$$\log_{10} \text{reduction} = \frac{D_{\text{abs}}}{D_{10}} = \frac{8}{1.5} = 5.333 \text{ logs}$$

**(b) Final microbial count** using survivor equation:
$$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-D_{\text{abs}}/D_{10}}$$
$$N = (5 \times 10^6) \cdot 10^{-5.333}$$
$$\log_{10} N = \log_{10}(5 \times 10^6) - 5.333$$
$$\log_{10} N = 6.6990 - 5.333 = 1.366$$
$$N = 10^{1.366} = \mathbf{23.23 \text{ CFU/g}}$$

**(c) Safety check:**
$$N = 23.23 \text{ CFU/g} \ll 10^4 \text{ CFU/g (FSSAI limit)} \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The dose of 8 kGy reduces the microbial load by **5.33 log cycles**, far below the safety threshold. This demonstrates why **8–10 kGy** is the industrial benchmark for spice irradiation.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 4 — Bread Loaf Specific Volume (Quick Test)

**Problem:** A loaf of bread weighs $\mathbf{450 \text{ g}}$ and has a volume of $\mathbf{3100 \text{ cc}}$ (measured by rapeseed displacement). Calculate the Specific Loaf Volume (SLV) and comment on bread quality.

### Solution

$$\text{SLV} = \frac{V_{\text{loaf}}}{m_{\text{loaf}}} = \frac{3100 \text{ cc}}{450 \text{ g}} = \mathbf{6.89 \text{ cc/g}}$$

> **Comment:** SLV of $6.89$ cc/g exceeds the quality benchmark of $\mathbf{6.5}$ cc/g. Therefore, the bread has an **open, fine crumb structure** — indicating good gluten development and proper fermentation.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 5 — F₀ Value for Canned Vegetable (Canning)

**Problem:** A canned vegetable retort operates at $T = 121.1°C$ for $t = 15$ min. With $z = 10°C$ for *Clostridium botulinum*, calculate $F_0$.

### Solution

$$F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - T_{\text{ref}}}{z}}$$
$$F_0 = 15 \cdot 10^{\frac{121.1 - 121.1}{10}} = 15 \cdot 10^{0} = 15 \cdot 1 = \mathbf{15 \text{ min}}$$

> **Note:** When process temperature equals the reference temperature, the $F_0$ value equals the holding time. This is a **trivial verification**; for non-reference temperatures the exponent becomes nonzero.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Problem 6 — Sterilization at Elevated Temperature (Canning)

**Problem:** A vegetable canning process runs at $T = 130°C$ for $t = 5$ min. With $T_{\text{ref}} = 121.1°C$ and $z = 10°C$, calculate the equivalent $F_0$.

### Solution

$$F_0 = 5 \cdot 10^{\frac{130 - 121.1}{10}} = 5 \cdot 10^{0.89}$$
$$10^{0.89} = 7.762$$
$$F_0 = 5 \times 7.762 = \mathbf{38.81 \text{ min}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A $5$ min process at $130°C$ is equivalent (in lethality) to a $38.81$ min process at $121.1°C$. This far exceeds the minimum $F_0 = 3$ min required for low-acid canned foods, ensuring **commercial sterility**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.5 Functional Block — Microbial Decontamination Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Initial Microbial Load N0] --> M2{Spice Type}
    M2 -- Black Pepper --> M3A[Gamma or E-beam 8-10 kGy]
    M2 -- Turmeric --> M3B[Steam 100C 30 min]
    M2 -- Clove --> M3C[Low Dose 3-5 kGy Sensitive to Heat]
    M2 -- Cardamom --> M3D[Steam Preferred Preserves Volatiles]
    M2 -- Chilli Powder --> M3E[E-beam Avoids Color Loss]
    M3A --> M4[Log Reduction Verification N final]

    M3C --> M4
    M3D --> M4
    M3E --> M4
    M4 --> M5{Compliance Check FSSAI}
    M5 -- Pass --> M6[Approved]
    M5 -- Fail --> M7[Re-treat or Reject]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the *spice type* dictates the *method*. Heat-labile spices (clove, cardamom) prefer ionizing radiation, while robust powders (turmeric) tolerate steam. This is a classic **KTU Apply-level** concept.
