# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**List the 12 steps involved in the manufacture of bread. Why is kneading considered a critical step?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 12 steps are: (1) Selection of ingredients, (2) Weighing, (3) Sieving, (4) Mixing, (5) Kneading, (6) Bulk fermentation, (7) Punching, (8) Division and rounding, (9) Intermediate proofing, (10) Moulding and panning, (11) Final proofing, (12) Baking, cooling, and packaging. **Kneading (1.5 marks)** is critical because it develops the gluten network through alignment of glutenin and gliadin proteins into a continuous viscoelastic film. Without proper kneading, the dough cannot retain $\text{CO}_2$ produced by yeast, resulting in a dense, low-volume bread. The "window-pane test" is used to confirm proper gluten development. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define microbial decontamination of spices. Why is it necessary, and what are the conventional methods used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Microbial decontamination is a controlled post-harvest treatment to reduce the microbial load of spices to safe levels ($\leq 10^4$ CFU/g) while preserving sensory quality. **Necessity (1 mark):** Spices harvested and dried in open fields carry $10^5$–$10^7$ CFU/g including spore-formers (*Bacillus*, *Clostridium*) and toxigenic moulds (*Aspergillus* producing aflatoxins). This poses food safety risks and is the leading cause of microbial rejection in international spice trade. **Methods (1 mark):** Conventional methods include steam pasteurization, gamma irradiation, electron beam, ethylene oxide fumigation (now restricted), ozone, and emerging cold plasma technology.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> 1. *Bread Q:* Students often forget to mention **oven-spring** and **Maillard browning** during baking — both critical for crust quality.
> 2. *Pulse Q:* Confusing *dehusking efficiency* with *dal yield* — they are **not the same**.
> 3. *Spice Decontamination Q:* Failing to cite the **10 kGy FSSAI limit** in India leads to a 2-mark cut.
> 4. *Numerical Q:* Unit errors in $D_{10}$ problems (mixing kGy and min) are the most common reason for full-mark loss.
> 5. *Diagram Q:* Skipping the **flow diagram** when asked for "explain the process" — 2 marks are reserved for the diagram.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Bread + Spices Combined Theme

**[a] (7 Marks)** Describe the industrial manufacturing process of bread with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of yeast and the importance of fermentation.

**[b] (7 Marks)** Discuss in detail the various methods of microbial decontamination of spices. Compare steam pasteurization, gamma irradiation, and electron beam with respect to efficacy, quality, and regulatory status.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Bread Manufacturing (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Bread is a fermented baked product traditionally made from wheat flour, water, salt, and yeast. The principal functional component of wheat flour is **gluten**, a viscoelastic protein complex formed by glutenin and gliadin upon hydration.

**Flow Diagram (2 marks):** *(Draw the 12-step flow as in Section 4.1 of these notes; examiners grant 2 marks for a correct sequential flow with key parameters.)*

**Role of Yeast and Fermentation (2 marks):**
Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) ferments the simple sugars (glucose, fructose, maltose) in the dough:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{zymase}} 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$
The $\text{CO}_2$ gets trapped in the gluten mesh, producing the characteristic porous crumb. Fermentation also produces secondary metabolites (ethanol, organic acids, diacetyl) that contribute to **flavor development** and **crumb texture**. Bulk fermentation is conducted at $27\text{–}30°C$ for $2\text{–}4$ hours, during which pH drops from $\sim 6.0$ to $\sim 4.5$–$5.0$, improving dough extensibility.

**Baking (1 mark):** Baking at $200\text{–}230°C$ accomplishes four simultaneous events: (i) **Oven-spring** (final expansion by thermal gas expansion), (ii) **Starch gelatinization** (above $60°C$), (iii) **Protein denaturation** (above $74°C$, setting the crumb), and (iv) **Crust formation** via Maillard browning.

**Quality Control (1 mark):** SLV ($\geq 6.5$ cc/g), moisture ($35\text{–}40\%$), pH, and crumb grain are the standard quality indices.

#### Part (b) — Spice Decontamination (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Raw spices harbor $10^5$–$10^7$ CFU/g, including pathogens. Decontamination is mandatory for export-quality spices.

**Method 1: Steam Pasteurization (1.5 marks):** Spices are exposed to saturated steam at $100\text{–}105°C$ for $15\text{–}30$ min in a closed vessel. Achieves $3\text{–}5$ log reduction of vegetative cells; less effective on spores. Causes moderate darkening of color. Low capital cost; widely used in India for turmeric, cumin, and coriander.

**Method 2: Gamma Irradiation (2 marks):** Uses Cobalt-60 ($\text{Co}^{60}$) source emitting $\gamma$-photons ($1.17$ and $1.33$ MeV). Approved internationally up to $10$ kGy (FSSAI/Codex). Achieves $5\text{–}8$ log reduction. Penetration is excellent, ensuring uniform treatment. Minimal flavor change at $\leq 5$ kGy; higher doses may cause off-odors. Cold process — no thermal degradation.

**Method 3: Electron Beam (e-beam) (1.5 marks):** Uses accelerated electrons ($5\text{–}10$ MeV). Same microbial effect as gamma but **does not induce radioactivity** (a key consumer perception advantage). Penetration is limited to $\sim 5$ cm, suitable for packaged spices. Faster throughput than gamma.

**Comparative Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Steam | Gamma | E-beam |
|---|---|---|---|
| Log reduction | $3\text{–}5$ | $5\text{–}8$ | $4\text{–}7$ |
| Color change | Moderate | Minimal | Minimal |
| Cost | Low | High | High |
| Regulatory | Widely accepted | $10$ kGy limit | $10$ kGy limit |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Pulses + Vegetables + Spices Combined Theme

**[a] (7 Marks)** Explain the industrial process flow of pulse milling. What pretreatments are used to improve dehusking efficiency?

**[b] (7 Marks)** Describe the principles and methods of vegetable preservation. Explain the concept of Hurdle Technology with suitable examples.

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Pulse Milling (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Pulses are processed into *dal* (split, dehusked cotyledons) to improve digestibility, palatability, and shelf life. The challenge lies in separating the tightly adhered husk (testa) from the cotyledon without breaking the latter.

**Process Flow (2.5 marks):** Refer to the schematic in Section 4.2. The sequence is: **Cleaning → Pretreatment → Drying → Dehusking → Splitting → Polishing → Grading → Packaging.**

Each step is critical. Cleaning (air-screen + destoner) removes stones, dust, and chaff. Pretreatment (oil-water or hot water) loosens the husk by plasticizing the gum layer between husk and cotyledon. Dehusking (using abrasive rollers or under-runner disks) removes $\sim 80\text{–}90\%$ of husk in a single pass. Splitting uses disc splitters. Polishing with emery cones gives the glossy finish. Grading classifies by size.

**Pretreatments (2 marks):**
- **Oil + Water Treatment (Most common):** Pulses are mixed with $0.5\text{–}1.0\%$ edible oil for $2\text{–}3$ min, then sprayed with $2\text{–}4\%$ water. Conditioning for $4\text{–}6$ hours in a bin. Oil penetrates the husk-cotyledon interface, water softens the gum layer.
- **Hot Water Treatment:** Soaking in $70\text{–}80°C$ water for $2\text{–}4$ min, then drying.
- **Lye (NaOH) Treatment:** $1\text{–}2\%$ NaOH at $90\text{–}95°C$ for $2\text{–}5$ min, followed by washing. Highly effective but risks residual alkali.

**Dehusking Efficiency (1.5 marks):** The standard equation:
$$\eta_{\text{dehusk}} = \frac{m_{\text{dal}}}{m_{\text{raw pulse}}} \times 100$$

A well-operated mill achieves $\mathbf{85\text{–}90\%}$ dehusking efficiency. The recovery of brokens and powder is the *dal yield* — typically $82\text{–}88\%$. Loss beyond $15\%$ indicates poor pretreatment or excessive drying.

#### Part (b) — Vegetable Preservation (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Vegetables are highly perishable due to high moisture ($80\text{–}95\%$) and active metabolism. Preservation methods aim to extend shelf life by controlling microbial growth, enzymatic activity, and chemical deterioration.

**Principles of Preservation (1.5 marks):**
1. **Inhibition of microbial growth** — Low temperature, low pH, low $a_w$.
2. **Inactivation of microbes** — Thermal sterilization, irradiation.
3. **Prevention of recontamination** — Hermetic sealing, aseptic packaging.
4. **Inhibition of enzymes** — Blanching, chemical treatment (e.g., $\text{SO}_2$).
5. **Removal of moisture** — Dehydration, concentration.

**Preservation Methods (2 marks):**
- **Blanching + Freezing:** Blanching at $90\text{–}100°C$ for $2\text{–}5$ min inactivates PPO and POD enzymes; subsequent IQF at $\leq -30°C$ preserves color, texture, and nutrients.
- **Canning:** Retort sterilization at $121.1°C$ for commercial sterility; $F_0 \geq 3$ min for low-acid vegetables.
- **Dehydration:** Hot-air drying ($60\text{–}80°C$) to moisture $\leq 8\%$; freeze-drying preserves heat-sensitive nutrients.
- **Pickling:** Fermentation by lactic acid bacteria lowers pH to $\leq 4.6$, inhibiting *Clostridium botulinum*.
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** Altered $\text{O}_2/\text{CO}_2$ ratio to suppress respiration and spoilage flora.

**Hurdle Technology (2.5 marks):**
The *Hurdle Technology* concept, developed by Lothar Leistner, combines **multiple mild preservation factors** ("hurdles") to achieve microbial safety without compromising sensory quality. The principle is that each hurdle contributes a small lethal effect, but their combination is synergistically effective.

**Equation representation:**
$$F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + F_2 + F_3 + \ldots + F_n$$
where each $F_i$ is the contribution of an individual hurdle (e.g., low pH, low $a_w$, mild heat).

**Example (Indian vegetable product — Mango pickle):**
- *Hurdle 1:* High salt ($\geq 15\% \text{ NaCl}$) → low $a_w$
- *Hurdle 2:* Low pH ($\leq 4.0$) from acetic acid + lactic fermentation
- *Hurdle 3:* Spices (antimicrobial essential oils — eugenol, allyl isothiocyanate)
- *Hurdle 4:* Anaerobic environment (oil layer sealing)

No single hurdle is sufficient, but their combination yields a shelf-stable product without thermal sterilization. This is a **classic KTU exam example**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullet Points:**

### 🔹 Bread
- 12 steps in order — memorize the sequence.
- Yeast fermentation equation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \to 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$
- Baking temperature: $\mathbf{200\text{–}230°C}$; SLV benchmark: $\mathbf{\geq 6.5 \text{ cc/g}}$.
- Maillard browning is the crust color reaction.
- Window-pane test = proper gluten development.

### 🔹 Pulses
- Definition: Dried seeds of Leguminosae; protein $\mathbf{20\text{–}30\%}$.
- Industry target: dehusking efficiency $\mathbf{85\text{–}90\%}$; dal yield $\mathbf{82\text{–}88\%}$.
- Pretreatment: oil ($0.5\text{–}1\%$) + water ($2\text{–}4\%$) conditioning.
- Anti-nutritional factors: trypsin inhibitors, phytic acid, lectins — reduced by **soaking + cooking**.

### 🔹 Vegetables
- Moisture $\mathbf{80\text{–}95\%}$; highly perishable.
- Blanching: $80\text{–}100°C$ for $\mathbf{2\text{–}5 min}$; inactivates **PPO** and **POD**.
- IQF: $\leq \mathbf{-30°C}$ rapid freezing.
- Canning reference: $\mathbf{F_0 \geq 3 \text{ min}}$ at $121.1°C$ for low-acid foods.
- Pickle pH critical limit: $\mathbf{\leq 4.6}$.

### 🔹 Spices
- Microbial load in raw form: $\mathbf{10^5\text{–}10^7 \text{ CFU/g}}$.
- Major pathogens: *Bacillus cereus*, *Clostridium perfringens*, *Salmonella*.
- Aflatoxin source: *Aspergillus flavus* — field contamination.

### 🔹 Microbial Decontamination
- **FSSAI/Codex limit for spice irradiation:** $\mathbf{10 \text{ kGy}}$ (know this number).
- $\log_{10} \text{reduction} = D_{\text{abs}} / D_{10}$.
- Steam: $3\text{–}5$ log; Gamma: $5\text{–}8$ log; E-beam: $4\text{–}7$ log.
- **EtO banned in EU** due to toxic residues.
- Cold plasma = emerging green technology.
- Survivor equation: $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-D_{\text{abs}}/D_{10}}$.

### 🔹 Master Equations (One-Line Recall)
- $M_{\text{wb}} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$
- $\text{SLV} = \dfrac{V}{m}$
- $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{z}}$
- $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-D_{\text{abs}}/D_{10}}$
- $\eta_{\text{dehusk}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{dal}}}{m_{\text{raw}}} \times 100$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harvesting and Processing of Black Pepper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Harvesting and Processing of Black Pepper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Botanical and Botanical Origin

**Black Pepper (Piper nigrum L.)** is a perennial climbing vine belonging to the family **Piperaceae**, and is universally recognized as the "**King of Spices**" — a title conferred due to its pre-eminent position in global spice trade, historical significance, and unparalleled commercial value. Black pepper is the dried mature but unripe fruit (drupe) of the pepper vine, typically measuring **4–6 mm** in diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The topic encompasses harvesting maturity, manual/chemical harvesting techniques, post-harvest handling, primary processing (drying, cleaning, grading), and value addition of black pepper — **the most heavily traded spice globally**.

The plant is native to the **Western Ghats of Kerala, India**, where it grows wild in the evergreen forests. The major commercial varieties cultivated in India are **Panniyur-1, Panniyur-5, Karimunda, Sreekara, Subhakara, IISR Thevam, and IISR Shakthi**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** Black pepper is defined as the **dried whole or ground mature unripe berries of *Piper nigrum L.*, having a characteristic pungent flavor due to the alkaloid piperine (C₁₇H₁₉NO₃)** and an aroma contributed by essential oils (1–3%), chiefly **α-pinene, β-caryophyllene, and sabinene**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a coffee bean before and after roasting: the **green bean** and the **roasted bean** are the same fruit, but processing transforms their chemical, sensory, and economic value. Similarly, in black pepper:

- The **fresh green berry** harvested from the vine is the **raw material** (analogous to the green coffee bean).
- The **drying process** is the "**transformation step**" — it shrinks the berry, develops the characteristic **wrinkled black pericarp**, activates enzymatic browning, and concentrates the essential oils and piperine.
- Without proper drying and post-harvest handling, the berry would simply **rot** rather than become the aromatic black pepper of commerce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Core Truths of Pepper Processing:**
> 1. **Harvesting at correct maturity** = maximum piperine & essential oil yield.
> 2. **Rapid post-harvest blanching & drying** = prevention of mold & aflatoxin contamination.
> 3. **Uniform drying** (moisture from **~70% wb** to **<12% wb**) = long shelf life & premium grade.

---

**Composition of Fresh vs. Processed Black Pepper (Approximate):**

| Component | Fresh Green Berry | Dried Black Pepper |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | **65–75% wb** | **<12% wb** |
| Piperine | 2–4% (dry basis) | **5–9%** |
| Essential Oil | 0.5–1% (fresh) | **1–3%** |
| Starch | 30–35% | **30–40%** |
| Crude Fiber | 10–15% | **10–18%** |
| Protein | 8–10% | **10–13%** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Harvesting Maturity Indices

Harvesting black pepper at the correct maturity stage is the single most critical determinant of final quality. Pepper berries follow a **non-climacteric ripening pattern**, meaning they **do not ripen further after harvest**.

**Visual & Physical Maturity Indicators:**
- **One or two berries per spike turn yellow-orange** (optimal harvest window).
- Berries are **firm, green, and well-developed**, with one or two berries beginning to turn red.
- Latex oozing reduces significantly upon maturity.
- Spike becomes **compact** and the berries appear **plump**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept — Harvesting Window:** If harvested too early → low piperine content, low weight, shriveled dried product. If too late → berries turn red, fall off, become susceptible to fungal attack, and produce **white pepper** (when decorticated) instead of premium black pepper.

### 2.2 Harvesting Methods

**A. Manual Harvesting (Traditional & Most Common)**
- Spikes are **plucked by hand** using ladders/climbing techniques.
- Skilled climbers can harvest **40–80 kg of fresh berries/day**.
- Provides the **highest selectivity** for maturity.

**B. Mechanical Harvesting**
- Limited adoption due to the climbing vine architecture.
- Tractor-mounted platforms and pneumatic shakers are experimental.

**C. Chemical Harvesting (Under Research)**
- Application of **ethephon (2-chloroethyl phosphonic acid)** at **100–200 ppm** to defoliate and loosen berries for once-over harvest.
- Not commercially recommended due to residue concerns.

### 2.3 Post-Harvest Operations — The Processing Chain

The post-harvest processing chain for black pepper consists of the following sequential unit operations:

1. **Collection & Transport** — harvested spikes are collected in baskets; transported within **4–6 hours** to processing site to avoid microbial spoilage.

2. **Threshing / De-spiking** — separation of berries from spikes using **mechanical threshers** or **manual foot-trampling** (traditional) in clean gunny/cement yards. **Losses should be <2%.**

3. **Cleaning & Washing** — removal of leaves, dirt, immature berries; washing in **clean potable water** to remove surface contaminants.

4. **Blanching (Optional but Recommended)** — dipping berries in **boiling water (95–100 °C) for 60–90 seconds**. This:
   - Inactivates **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** and prevents enzymatic browning (retains uniform black color).
   - Destroys microbial load.
   - Softens the pericarp for uniform drying.

5. **Drying** — the **most critical operation**. Moisture is reduced from **~70% wb** to **<12% wb**.

6. **Cleaning & Grading** — removal of foreign matter; size grading using **sieves/shifters**.

7. **Packaging & Storage** — in clean jute bags / polypropylene bags; stored in cool, dry godowns.

### 2.4 Drying Methods — Engineering Analysis

**A. Sun Drying (Traditional)**
- Berries spread in **thin layers (2–3 cm thick)** on clean cement yards or polyethylene sheets.
- Turned periodically (**2–3 times/day**) for uniform drying.
- Duration: **7–15 days** depending on solar intensity.
- **Disadvantages:** Contamination, slow, weather-dependent, non-uniform → **KTU examiner's favourite discussion point**.

**B. Mechanical / Artificial Drying**
- **Tray dryers** at **50–60 °C** for **24–48 hours**.
- **Fluidized bed dryers** — rapid drying in **6–8 hours**, superior quality.
- **Solar-cum-mechanical hybrid dryers** — recommended for Kerala conditions.

**C. Advanced Methods**
- **Infrared drying**, **microwave drying**, **vacuum drying** — under research for premium-grade pepper.

### 2.5 Quality Standards (Agmark / ASTA / ISO)

| Grade Parameter | Grade A (Special) | Grade B (Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| Extraneous Matter | **<1.0%** | **<2.0%** |
| Light Berries | **<2.0%** | **<5.0%** |
| Pinheads | **<2.0%** | **<4.0%** |
| Moisture | **<12.0%** | **<13.0%** |
| Piperine (min) | **>6.0%** | **>5.0%** |
| Bulk Density | **>500 g/L** | **>450 g/L** |

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture Content (Wet Basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight |
| Moisture Content (Dry Basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Used for drying calculations |
| Drying Rate | $R = \dfrac{dM}{dt} = \dfrac{m_s (M_{t} - M_{t+dt})}{A \cdot dt}$ | kg water / m²·h |
| Equilibrium Moisture Content | $M_e = f(T, RH)$ | Depends on EMC curve |
| Piperine Content | $Pip(\%) = \dfrac{A_{sample}}{A_{std}} \times C_{std} \times \dfrac{V}{W}$ | UV-Spectrophotometry at **345 nm** |
| Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_{container}}$ | **g/L** |
| Shrinkage Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{V_{dried}}{V_{fresh}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Energy for Drying (latent heat) | $Q = m_w \cdot L_v$ | $L_v = 2400$ kJ/kg at 100 °C |
| Aflatoxin Limit (ASTA/Codex) | $AF < 20\ \mu g/kg$ | Total aflatoxins |
| ASTA Cleanliness Spec | Light berries $<2\%$ | Grade A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion:** $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{1 + MC_{db}}$ and $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}$ — frequently tested in KTU exams.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The drying kinetics of black pepper follow the classical **thin-layer drying models** (Newton, Page, Henderson-Pabis, Midilli). The **Page Model** is empirically the best fit for black pepper:

$$MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n)$$

where $MR = \dfrac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$ is the **moisture ratio**, $t$ is time (h), and $k$, $n$ are drying constants. This model enables:
- **Designing industrial dryers** for specific throughput.
- **Predicting drying time** under variable solar conditions.
- **Quality optimization** in solar-mechanical hybrid systems adopted by Spices Board of India.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters & Outcomes

| Process Stage | Input Parameter | Operating Condition | Output Quality Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Harvesting | Spike maturity | 1–2 berries yellow | Piperine >6%, plump berries |
| Threshing | Spike integrity | Mechanical, <2% loss | Whole berry retention |
| Blanching | Water temp & time | 95–100 °C, 60–90 s | Uniform black color, microbe kill |
| Drying | Bed thickness & temp | 2–3 cm; 50–60 °C | Final MC <12% wb |
| Grading | Sieve size | Mesh 4.0–4.75 mm | Grade A/B/C classification |
| Storage | RH & temp | <60% RH, <25 °C | Shelf life 18–24 months |

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation — Drying Rate Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
A batch of fresh black pepper weighing **1000 kg** with initial moisture content of **70% (wb)** is dried in a tray dryer to a final moisture content of **10% (wb)**. Calculate:
1. The amount of water to be removed (kg).
2. The drying time if the drying rate is **constant at 12 kg water/m²·h** and the dryer tray area is **5 m²**.
3. The final weight of dried pepper.

---

**Step 1: Express moisture contents on dry basis (mandatory for mass balance).**

The formula for wet basis to dry basis conversion is:

$$MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}$$

For initial moisture:

$$MC_1 = \dfrac{0.70}{1 - 0.70} = \dfrac{0.70}{0.30} = 2.333\ \text{kg water / kg dry matter}$$

For final moisture:

$$MC_2 = \dfrac{0.10}{1 - 0.10} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.90} = 0.111\ \text{kg water / kg dry matter}$$

---

**Step 2: Calculate the dry matter mass in the batch.**

The dry matter mass is invariant during drying (only water is removed):

$$W_d = W_w \times (1 - MC_{wb, initial})$$

$$W_d = 1000 \times (1 - 0.70) = 1000 \times 0.30 = 300\ \text{kg}$$

---

**Step 3: Calculate water removed per kg of dry matter.**

$$\Delta M = MC_1 - MC_2 = 2.333 - 0.111 = 2.222\ \text{kg water / kg dry matter}$$

---

**Step 4: Calculate the total water to be removed.**

$$m_w = W_d \times \Delta M = 300 \times 2.222 = 666.6\ \text{kg water}$$

> **Answer (1):** Approximately **666.6 kg** of water must be removed.

---

**Step 5: Calculate the drying time.**

The drying rate equation is:

$$R = \dfrac{m_w}{A \times t}$$

Rearranging for time:

$$t = \dfrac{m_w}{R \times A}$$

Substituting values:

$$t = \dfrac{666.6}{12 \times 5} = \dfrac{666.6}{60} = 11.11\ \text{hours}$$

> **Answer (2):** Total drying time ≈ **11.11 hours**.

---

**Step 6: Calculate final weight of dried pepper.**

$$W_f = W_w - m_w = 1000 - 666.6 = 333.4\ \text{kg}$$

Cross-verification using dry basis:

$$W_f = W_d \times (1 + MC_2) = 300 \times (1 + 0.111) = 300 \times 1.111 = 333.3\ \text{kg}$$

> **Answer (3):** Final weight ≈ **333.3 kg** of dried black pepper.

---

**Step 7: Calculate the drying efficiency / energy requirement (extended).**

Total latent heat required:

$$Q = m_w \times L_v = 666.6 \times 2400 = 1,599,840\ \text{kJ}$$

This energy corresponds to roughly **444 kWh** of thermal energy. For a tray dryer with thermal efficiency of **40%**, the actual energy input needed is:

$$Q_{input} = \dfrac{Q}{\eta} = \dfrac{1,599,840}{0.40} = 3,999,600\ \text{kJ} \approx 1111\ \text{kWh}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quality Loss vs. Process Deviation

| Deviation | Direct Quality Impact | Economic Loss |
|---|---|---|
| Harvesting too late | Red berries, fall-off, white pepper | Grade down to B/C |
| No blanching | Uneven color, moldy berries | Rejection in ASTA |
| Sun drying >15 days | Aflatoxin contamination | Export ban |
| Drying temp >70 °C | Volatile oil loss (scorched) | Aroma loss, low grade |
| Improper storage (high RH) | Mold, insect infestation | 15–30% loss in 6 months |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the term **"maturity index"** as applied to black pepper harvesting. Mention any **two visual indicators** of optimum harvest maturity.

**Model Answer:**
The maturity index is a **set of physical, chemical, and visual parameters** that indicate the optimal stage for harvesting the crop to obtain maximum yield and quality. For black pepper, optimum maturity is reached when **one or two berries per spike turn yellow-orange** while the rest remain green and firm. Two visual indicators are:
1. **Color change** of 1–2 berries per spike from green to yellow-orange.
2. **Spike compactness** — the spike becomes firm and the berries appear plump with reduced latex oozing.

*(3 marks: definition 1 mark + 2 indicators 2 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Why is **blanching** recommended as a pre-drying treatment for black pepper? State any **two reasons**.

**Model Answer:**
Blanching (immersion in boiling water at **95–100 °C for 60–90 seconds**) is recommended before drying because:
1. It **inactivates polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** and other enzymes, preventing enzymatic browning and ensuring a **uniform black color** of the dried product.
2. It **reduces the initial microbial load** on the berry surface, lowering the risk of mold and **aflatoxin** development during the prolonged drying period.

*(3 marks: 1 for naming blanching + 2 reasons × 1 mark each)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **post-harvest processing of black pepper** including the unit operations from harvesting to packaging. Add a note on the **changes in composition during drying**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **sun drying and mechanical drying methods** for black pepper. Compare them with respect to **drying time, quality, microbial safety, and capital investment**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a):**

The post-harvest processing chain of black pepper consists of the following sequential unit operations:

1. **Harvesting**: Spikes are manually harvested by skilled climbers when **1–2 berries turn yellow-orange**. Latex content is reduced at this stage.

2. **Collection and Transport**: Harvested spikes are collected in clean baskets and transported to the processing yard within **4–6 hours** to prevent microbial spoilage.

3. **Threshing (De-spiking)**: Berries are separated from spikes by **mechanical threshers** or by gentle foot-trampling. Loss should be **<2%**.

4. **Cleaning and Washing**: Foreign matter (leaves, twigs, immature berries) is removed, followed by washing in **potable water**.

5. **Blanching**: Berries are dipped in **boiling water (95–100 °C) for 60–90 seconds**. This destroys surface microflora and inactivates **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)**, ensuring uniform black color development.

6. **Drying**: Moisture is reduced from **~70% (wb)** to **<12% (wb)**. The dried berry shows shrinkage and the characteristic **wrinkled black pericarp**.

7. **Cleaning and Grading**: Sieves of **4.0–4.75 mm mesh** are used for size grading; foreign matter is removed to comply with **Agmark/ASTA/ISO standards**.

8. **Packaging and Storage**: Dried pepper is packed in **clean jute or polypropylene bags** (25–50 kg) and stored at **<60% RH and <25 °C**.

**Composition changes during drying:**
- **Moisture** drops from 70% to <12% (wb).
- **Piperine concentration** increases from ~2–4% (fresh, dry basis) to **5–9%** due to water removal.
- **Essential oil** becomes concentrated from 0.5–1% to **1–3%**.
- **Color**: green → brown → deep black due to **Maillard browning and enzymatic oxidation**.
- **Texture**: soft → hard and wrinkled.

*(7 marks: 5 marks for sequential operations + 2 marks for composition changes)*

---

**Model Answer (b):**

| Parameter | Sun Drying | Mechanical Drying |
|---|---|---|
| Drying Time | **7–15 days** | **24–48 hours** |
| Quality (Color/Aroma) | Variable, weather-dependent | Uniform, controllable |
| Microbial Safety | **Low** (high aflatoxin risk) | **High** (closed system) |
| Capital Investment | **Low** (yard only) | **High** (dryer, energy) |
| Operational Skill | Minimal | Moderate–High |
| Recommended Use | Small farmers, traditional | Export-grade pepper |

**Sun Drying**:
- Berries are spread on clean cement yards or **polyethylene sheets** in thin layers (2–3 cm).
- Turned **2–3 times/day** for uniform drying.
- Highly dependent on weather; contamination by dust, birds, insects is common.
- Risk of **aflatoxin contamination >20 µg/kg** is significant if rains interrupt drying.

**Mechanical Drying**:
- **Tray dryers** at **50–60 °C**, 24–48 h.
- **Fluidized bed dryers** at 60 °C, **6–8 h** — superior quality.
- Provides uniform moisture, controls microbial growth, and meets **ASTA/ISO standards**.
- Higher capital and operational cost but **economically justified for export-oriented processing**.

**Recommendation**: For premium export-quality black pepper, **mechanical drying is preferable**, while sun drying remains the practical option for smallholder farmers in Kerala.

*(7 marks: 3 marks each method + 1 mark comparison/conclusion)*

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What are the **different value-added products** that can be prepared from black pepper? Describe the **process of oleoresin extraction** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **quality standards for black pepper** as per **Agmark / ASTA / ISO** specifications. Mention the **physical, chemical, and microbiological parameters** and their permissible limits. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a):**

**Value-Added Products from Black Pepper:**
1. Ground black pepper powder
2. White pepper (decorticated)
3. Steam-sterilized pepper
4. **Oleoresin** (solvent extract)
5. Pepper oil (essential oil)
6. Freeze-dried / IQF green pepper
7. Pepper in brine / vinegar
8. Pepper-flavored sauces and pickles

**Process of Oleoresin Extraction:**

Oleoresin is a **concentrated extract** containing both the **volatile essential oil** and the **non-volatile resinous matter (piperine + fixed oils)**. The standard process is:

1. **Raw Material Preparation**: Cleaned, dried black pepper (MC <12% wb) is ground to a coarse powder.

2. **Solvent Extraction**: The powder is loaded into an **extractor (Soxhlet / continuous extractor)** and treated with **food-grade solvent — acetone, ethanol, or hexane** at **50–60 °C**.

3. **Filtration / Miscella Recovery**: The **solvent + extract** (miscella) is filtered to remove solids.

4. **Solvent Evaporation**: The miscella is passed through an **evaporator** under vacuum to recover the solvent; the residue is **crude oleoresin**.

5. **Secondary Concentration**: Further vacuum distillation removes residual solvent to **<30 ppm**.

6. **Standardization**: Oleoresin is standardized to required **piperine content (typically 40–55%)** and essential oil content.

**Yield**: Approximately **10–12%** of the weight of dried pepper.

**Applications**: Flavor industry, pharmaceutical formulations, processed foods, and perfumery.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for listing products + 5 marks for extraction process steps)*

---

**Model Answer (b):**

| Parameter | Agmark Grade I | ASTA (USA) | ISO 959-1 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extraneous Matter | **<1.0%** | **<1.0%** | **<1.5%** |
| Light Berries | **<2.0%** | **<2.0%** | **<3.0%** |
| Pinheads | **<2.0%** | **<3.0%** | **<4.0%** |
| Moisture | **<12.0%** | **<12.0%** | **<13.0%** |
| Total Ash | **<5.0%** | **<6.0%** | **<6.0%** |
| Piperine (min) | **>5.0%** | **>5.0%** | **>5.0%** |
| Volatile Oil (min) | **>1.5%** | **>1.0%** | **>1.0%** |
| Bulk Density | **>500 g/L** | — | **>450 g/L** |
| Aflatoxin (Total) | **<30 µg/kg** | **<20 µg/kg** | **<20 µg/kg** |
| Insect Damage | **<1.0%** | **<1.0%** | **<1.0%** |

**Physical Parameters**: Extraneous matter, light berries, pinheads, bulk density, color, size uniformity.

**Chemical Parameters**: Moisture, piperine content (UV-spectrophotometry at **345 nm**), volatile oil (hydro-distillation), total ash, acid-insoluble ash.

**Microbiological Parameters**: Total plate count (<10⁴ CFU/g), yeast & mold (<10³ CFU/g), absence of *Salmonella* and *E. coli*, aflatoxin B1 <10 µg/kg.

**Conclusion**: Compliance with these standards is **mandatory for export to EU, USA, Japan**. The **Spices Board of India** operates quality evaluation labs at Kochi, Mumbai, and Guntur for certifying export consignments.

*(7 marks: 3 marks parameter table + 2 marks physical/chemical/microbial + 2 marks concluding note)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing maturity indices of black pepper with cardamom or cloves** — students often quote "spike fully red" for pepper, which is WRONG. Pepper is harvested when berries are **green with 1–2 yellow**.
> 2. **Writing drying temperature >70 °C** — this causes essential oil loss and is heavily penalized.
> 3. **Omitting blanching step** — examiners specifically look for this in processing flowcharts.
> 4. **Writing aflatoxin limit loosely** — must specify **<20 µg/kg (ASTA/Codex)** and not just "low aflatoxin".
> 5. **Mixing up moisture basis formulas** — KTU frequently tests the **wb ↔ db conversion**; expect 2 marks for this alone.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Botanical Name**: *Piper nigrum L.* | Family: **Piperaceae** | Title: **King of Spices**.
- **Active Components**: **Piperine** (C₁₇H₁₉NO₃) — pungency; **Essential Oil** — aroma.
- **Maturity Index**: **1–2 berries turn yellow-orange per spike** (non-climacteric fruit).
- **Harvesting**: Manual by skilled climbers; **40–80 kg/day** per person.
- **Blanching**: **95–100 °C for 60–90 seconds** → uniform color + microbial safety.
- **Drying**: MC from **~70% wb → <12% wb**; tray dryer at **50–60 °C** for 24–48 h.
- **Sun Drying**: 7–15 days; cheap but **aflatoxin-prone**.
- **Mechanical Drying**: 6–48 h; **ASTA/ISO compliant**; higher cost.
- **Value Addition**: Powder, white pepper, oleoresin (yield 10–12%), pepper oil.
- **Oleoresin Extraction**: Solvent (acetone/ethanol/hexane) → evaporation → standardization.
- **Quality Standards**: Agmark Grade I requires **piperine >5%, MC <12%, extraneous matter <1%, aflatoxin <30 µg/kg** (Agmark) / **<20 µg/kg** (ASTA).
- **Storage**: **<60% RH, <25 °C**; shelf life **18–24 months** in jute/PP bags.
- **Key Formulas**: $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}$ and $MR = \exp(-k t^n)$ (Page model).

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the **block flow diagram** of the processing chain in long-answer questions — examiners award **2–3 extra marks** for clear, labelled process flowcharts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
