<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Botanical and Industrial Identity of Cardamom

**Cardamom** (*Elettaria cardamomum* Maton) is a perennial herbaceous plant belonging to the family **Zingiberaceae** (the ginger family). It is commercially cultivated for its intensely aromatic fruits (capsules) and is globally acclaimed as the **"Queen of Spices"** — third most expensive spice in the world after saffron and vanilla.

In the context of the KTU Fats & Oils module, cardamom occupies a unique dual position:
- A source of **volatile (essential) oils** used in flavor, fragrance, and pharmaceutical industries.
- A source of **fixed oils (non-volatile fats)** used in culinary and nutraceutical applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cardamom contains **6–10% fixed oil** (in seeds) and **2–8% volatile essential oil**. These two lipid fractions are fundamentally different in chemistry, extraction, and end-use — students must NOT confuse them.

## 1.2 Three Commercial Cultivars of Cardamom

| Cultivar | Region (Kerala Focus) | Capsule Appearance | Oil Yield |
|----------|----------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Malabar** | Idukki, Wayanad (Kerala) | Small, round, pale green | **6–8%** essential oil |
| **Mysore** | Karnataka (Western Ghats) | Bold, elongated, dark green | **2–4%** essential oil |
| **Vazhukka** | Hybrid zone (Kerala–Karnataka border) | Intermediate, oval, green | **4–6%** essential oil |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Pockets" Model

Imagine each cardamom seed as a **tiny double-pocket pouch**:

- **Pocket A (Volatile/Essential Oil):** Contains the *fragrant perfume* of the spice — light, aromatic, evaporates easily. This is what gives cardamom its characteristic aroma when you crush it between your fingers.
- **Pocket B (Fixed Oil/Fat):** Contains the *cooking-oil-like* lipid that remains behind after the aroma escapes — heavier, non-volatile, fatty.

Just as you cannot extract perfume by squeezing (you need steam) and you cannot make ghee from rose petals (you need pressing), each oil fraction requires a **fundamentally different extraction technology**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** Industry-grade cardamom capsules are graded as **AGEB (Alleppey Green Extra Bold)** — measuring ≥ 7 mm, with a premium oil yield benchmark of **≥ 3.5%** (v/w) by steam distillation.

## 1.4 Classification of Cardamom-Derived Lipids

$$\text{Cardamom Lipids} = \underbrace{\text{Essential (Volatile) Oil}}_{\text{2–8\% of capsule}} + \underbrace{\text{Fixed (Non-Volatile) Oil}}_{\text{1–2\% of capsule (up to 6–10\% in seeds alone)}}$$

**Essential oils** are complex mixtures of low-molecular-weight **terpenes** and **terpenoids** — they evaporate at room temperature and possess the characteristic aroma.

**Fixed oils** are predominantly **triglycerides** of fatty acids (oleic, palmitic, linoleic) — they do not evaporate and resemble common cooking oils in physical behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Composition of Cardamom Essential Oil

The volatile oil of cardamom is dominated by oxygenated monoterpenes. The KTU-relevant major constituents are tabulated below:

| Compound | Chemical Class | Approximate % | Sensory Contribution |
|----------|---------------|---------------|----------------------|
| **1,8-Cineole (Eucalyptol)** | Monoterpene ether | **30–45%** | Strong camphoraceous, fresh |
| **α-Terpinyl acetate** | Monoterpene ester | **25–35%** | Sweet, floral, fruity |
| **Linalool** | Monoterpene alcohol | **2–5%** | Citrusy, floral |
| **Limonene** | Monoterpene hydrocarbon | **2–6%** | Lemon-like |
| **Sabinene** | Monoterpene hydrocarbon | **2–5%** | Spicy, woody |
| **Myrcene** | Monoterpene hydrocarbon | **1–3%** | Earthy, resinous |
| **Terpinene-4-ol** | Monoterpene alcohol | **1–3%** | Herbal, musty |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **1,8-Cineole : α-Terpinyl Acetate ratio** is the chemical fingerprint used to distinguish cultivar authenticity. Genuine Malabar cardamom oil shows a ratio closer to **1.5:1**, while Mysore variety shows approximately **1:1**.

## 2.2 Chemical Composition of Cardamom Fixed Oil

The fixed oil fraction is a triglyceride matrix with the following typical fatty acid profile:

$$\text{Fixed Oil Composition} = \underbrace{\text{Oleic Acid (C18:1)}}_{\approx 42\text{–}50\%} + \underbrace{\text{Linoleic Acid (C18:2)}}_{\approx 25\text{–}35\%} + \underbrace{\text{Palmitic Acid (C16:0)}}_{\approx 8\text{–}12\%} + \underbrace{\text{Stearic Acid (C18:0)}}_{\approx 1\text{–}3\%} + \text{Others}$$

**Physicochemical properties** of cardamom fixed oil:
- **Iodine value:** 75–95 g I₂/100 g (semi-drying oil category)
- **Saponification value:** 160–190 mg KOH/g
- **Acid value:** < 5 mg KOH/g (refined grade)
- **Specific gravity at 25°C:** 0.91–0.93 g/mL

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|-------------|
| **Oil Yield (% v/w)** | $$\text{Yield} = \frac{V_{\text{oil}}}{W_{\text{seed}}} \times 100$$ | % | Industrial extraction efficiency |
| **Mass Balance (Input)** | $$W_{\text{seed}} = W_{\text{oil}} + W_{\text{spent cake}} + W_{\text{moisture loss}}$$ | kg | Process accounting |
| **Refractive Index Check** | $$\eta_D^{25} \in [1.4600,\, 1.4680]$$ | — | Essential oil purity test |
| **Steam Requirement** | $$M_{\text{steam}} \approx 3 \times M_{\text{charge}}$$ | kg | Distillation design rule |
| **Recovery Efficiency** | $$\eta_{\text{recovery}} = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$$ | % | Extraction method comparison |
| **Acid Value** | $$\text{AV} = \frac{56.1 \times V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}}}{W_{\text{oil}}}$$ | mg KOH/g | Fixed oil quality |
| **Saponification Value** | $$\text{SV} = \frac{56.1 \times (V_{\text{blank}} - V_{\text{sample}}) \times N_{\text{KOH}}}{W_{\text{oil}}}$$ | mg KOH/g | Average molecular weight |
| **Degree of Unsaturation** | $$\text{Iodine Value} \propto \text{double bond count}$$ | g I₂/100 g | Oxidative stability indicator |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above formulas, $V_{\text{oil}}$ is the volume of oil recovered, $W_{\text{seed}}$ is the dry weight of cardamom seeds charged, and all concentrations follow standard volumetric conventions. The constant **56.1** is the molecular weight of KOH expressed as **mg/mmol**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cardamom oil extraction bridges **flavor chemistry, process engineering, and pharmacology**:
- **Food industry:** Cardamom oleoresin (essential oil + fixed oil + natural resin) is encapsulated for instant flavor powders.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** 1,8-Cineole exhibits bronchodilatory, anti-inflammatory, and gastroprotective activity.
- **Perfumery:** α-Terpinyl acetate is a premium floral note base in luxury fragrances.
- **Cosmeceuticals:** Cardamom fixed oil is marketed as a carrier oil for Ayurvedic massage formulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Essential Oil Yield Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A batch of 250 kg dried Malabar cardamom capsules is steam-distilled. The recovered essential oil volume is **8.75 L** at 25 °C. Determine (a) the percentage oil yield (v/w), (b) the mass of essential oil assuming standard density, and (c) compare against the KTU quality benchmark.

**Given:**
- $W_{\text{seed}} = 250 \text{ kg} = 250{,}000 \text{ g}$
- $V_{\text{oil}} = 8.75 \text{ L} = 8750 \text{ mL}$
- $\rho_{\text{oil}} \approx 0.92 \text{ g/mL}$ at 25 °C

### Step 1 — Compute Volumetric Yield (v/w basis)

The yield is calculated as the volume of oil per 100 grams of seed:

$$\text{Yield}_{\text{v/w}} = \frac{V_{\text{oil}}}{W_{\text{seed}}} \times 100$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\text{Yield}_{\text{v/w}} = \frac{8750 \text{ mL}}{250{,}000 \text{ g}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Yield}_{\text{v/w}} = \frac{8750}{250{,}000} \times 100 = 0.035 \times 100 = 3.5\%$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Mass (m/m basis)

Mass of essential oil is recovered using density:

$$m_{\text{oil}} = V_{\text{oil}} \times \rho_{\text{oil}}$$

$$m_{\text{oil}} = 8750 \text{ mL} \times 0.92 \text{ g/mL} = 8050 \text{ g} = 8.05 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Verify Against KTU Quality Benchmark

The KTU reference value for premium Malabar cardamom essential oil is **≥ 3.5% (v/w)**.

Since our calculated yield is **3.5%**, the batch **meets the benchmark exactly**, indicating it qualifies as **AGEB-grade commercial cardamom**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Volumetric yield (v/w) is the industry standard for essential oils because oils are sold by volume. The mass-based conversion becomes essential when computing material balances for downstream fixed oil extraction from the spent cake.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Steam Requirement for Distillation

**Problem Statement:** Design a steam distillation unit for processing 100 kg/h of crushed cardamom seeds. Using the **3:1 steam-to-charge ratio** rule of thumb, calculate the steam consumption per hour.

### Step 1 — State the Design Rule

For cardamom essential oil recovery via steam distillation, empirical process design uses:

$$M_{\text{steam}} = k \times M_{\text{charge}}$$

where $k \approx 3$ for ground seed material.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Operating Parameters

$$M_{\text{steam}} = 3 \times 100 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$M_{\text{steam}} = 300 \text{ kg/h}$$

### Step 3 — Interpret Engineering Implication

This means every 1 kg of cardamom seed requires 3 kg of saturated steam. For a continuous plant operating 20 h/day, the daily steam load would be:

$$M_{\text{steam, daily}} = 300 \text{ kg/h} \times 20 \text{ h/day} = 6000 \text{ kg/day} = 6 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

This sizing parameter is critical for selecting the **boiler capacity** and the **condenser heat-exchange area** in the plant design.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Acid Value Determination of Fixed Oil

**Problem Statement:** 2.5 g of cardamom fixed oil is titrated with 0.1 N KOH. The volume consumed is **1.8 mL**. Compute the acid value.

### Step 1 — Recall the Standard Equation

$$\text{AV} = \frac{56.1 \times V \times N}{W}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$\text{AV} = \frac{56.1 \times 1.8 \times 0.1}{2.5}$$

$$\text{AV} = \frac{56.1 \times 0.18}{2.5} = \frac{10.098}{2.5}$$

$$\text{AV} = 4.04 \text{ mg KOH/g}$$

### Step 3 — Quality Interpretation

Since AV < 5 mg KOH/g, this fixed oil qualifies as **refined-grade** and is suitable for direct food and cosmetic applications.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Extraction Technologies

| Parameter | Steam Distillation | Hydrodistillation | Solvent Extraction | Supercritical CO₂ |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Oil Quality** | High (standard) | Moderate (thermal) | Lower (solvent residue) | **Premium** |
| **Yield (%)** | 3–5 | 2.5–4 | 5–10 | **4–8** |
| **Operating Temp (°C)** | 100–110 | 100 | 40–60 | 35–50 |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate | Low | Moderate | **High** |
| **Energy Intensity** | High | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Solvent Residue Risk** | None | None | **Yes (must strip)** | None |
| **Aroma Fidelity** | Good | Acceptable | Variable | **Excellent** |
| **KTU-Exam Favourite?** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cardamom Processing — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Cardamom Capsules] --> B[Cleaning and Grading]
    B --> C[Drying 8 to 12 percent moisture]
    C --> D[Decortication Capsule Removal]
    D --> E[Seed Crushing and Flaking]
    E --> F{Extraction Route}
    F --> G[Steam Distillation Unit]
    F --> H[Hydrodistillation Unit]
    F --> I[Supercritical CO2 Unit]
    F --> J[Solvent Extraction Unit]
    G --> K[Volatile Oil Condenser]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Essential Oil Storage Tank]
    J --> M[Solvent Stripper]
    M --> N[Oleoresin Concentrator]
    E --> O[Mechanical Cold Press]
    O --> P[Fixed Oil Filter]
    P --> Q[Fixed Oil Storage Tank]
    L --> R[Quality Lab GC MS and Refractive Index]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Packaging and Dispatch]
    N --> S
    L --> S
    Q --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The central decision node `{Extraction Route}` represents the engineering choice that determines downstream product identity — volatile oil, oleoresin, or fixed oil. Each branch leads to a distinct downstream processing line.

## 4.2 Steam Distillation Apparatus — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steam Boiler] -->|Saturated Steam 100 deg C| B[Distillation Still]
    B -->|Charged with Crushed Seeds| B
    B -->|Vapor Mixture Steam plus Oil| C[Condenser Coil]
    C -->|Cooling Water| C
    C -->|Condensate plus Oil| D[Separator Florentine]
    D -->|Oil Layer Upper| E[Essential Oil Receiver]
    D -->|Water Layer Lower| F[Hydrosol Collection]
    B -->|Spent Cake| G[Drying and Discharge]
    E --> H[Storage Amber Glass]
    F --> I[Recycle or Aroma Water Sale]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Conceptual Mass Stream Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input 100 kg Dry Seeds] --> S2[Steam Distillation]
    S2 --> S3[Output 3.5 L Essential Oil]
    S2 --> S4[Output 96 kg Spent Cake with 5 percent moisture]
    S3 --> S5[Sales to Flavor Industry]
    S4 --> S6[Sold as Cattle Feed or Composted]
    S4 --> S7[Optional Cold Press for Fixed Oil]
    S7 --> S8[Output 1.8 L Fixed Oil]
    S8 --> S9[Sales to Cosmetic Industry]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **essential oil** and **fixed oil** as obtained from cardamom seeds.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Essential Oil | Fixed Oil |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| **Nature** | Volatile, aromatic | Non-volatile, fatty |
| **Chemistry** | Terpenes & terpenoids | Triglycerides |
| **Yield from Cardamom** | 2–8% (v/w) | 1–2% (capsule basis) |
| **Extraction** | Steam/hydro-distillation | Cold pressing, solvent |
| **Use** | Flavor, perfume, pharma | Culinary, cosmetics |

**(3 marks awarded for clear differentiation across at least 3 parameters.)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State **any three** major chemical constituents of cardamom essential oil with their approximate percentage composition.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **1,8-Cineole** — 30–45% (monoterpene ether, camphoraceous aroma)
2. **α-Terpinyl acetate** — 25–35% (monoterpene ester, sweet floral aroma)
3. **Linalool** — 2–5% (monoterpene alcohol, citrus-floral aroma)

**(3 marks — 1 mark for each correctly named compound with class and range.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> - Marks are deducted when students confuse **essential oil** with **oleoresin** — they are NOT the same. Oleoresin = essential oil + fixed oil + natural resins (solvent-extracted).
> - Students often quote incorrect yield percentages; **always specify** whether it is on a **whole-capsule (v/w)** or **decorticated-seed** basis.
> - Diagrams of distillation apparatus are mandatory for full marks — examiners award 2 marks specifically for the labelled sketch.
> - When discussing extraction, **always justify** the method choice in terms of thermal sensitivity of the oil components (1,8-cineole is mildly heat-stable; delicate esters like α-terpinyl acetate can hydrolyse).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** Describe the **steam distillation** method for the extraction of essential oil from cardamom. With a neat labelled diagram, explain the principle, process steps, and operating parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **chemical composition** of cardamom essential oil. Explain how the **1,8-cineole : α-terpinyl acetate ratio** serves as an authenticity marker for different cultivars. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Steam Distillation of Cardamom (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):** Steam distillation works on the principle that when a mixture of two immiscible liquids (here, water and essential oil) is heated, the total vapor pressure equals the sum of their individual vapor pressures. This causes the oil to boil at a temperature **below 100 °C**, preventing thermal degradation.

**Process Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Pre-treatment:** Cardamom capsules are decorticated (husk removed), seeds crushed to expose oil cells.
2. **Charging:** Crushed seeds loaded into the distillation still on a perforated grid above the steam inlet.
3. **Steam Injection:** Saturated steam at 100–110 °C is injected from below, vaporizing the volatile oil.
4. **Vapor Transport:** The mixed steam + oil vapor rises to the condenser.
5. **Condensation:** Vapor is condensed using cooling water in a coil condenser.
6. **Separation:** The condensate is collected in a **Florentine separator**, where the lighter essential oil floats above the water layer (hydrosol).

**Operating Parameters (2 marks):**
- Steam-to-charge ratio: **3:1** (kg/kg)
- Distillation time: **3–5 hours** per batch
- Temperature: **100–110 °C**
- Pressure: Atmospheric (1.013 bar)
- Recovery rate: **85–92%** of theoretical oil content

**Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.2 schematic — must be reproduced and labelled for full marks.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Chemical Composition & Authenticity Marker (7 marks)

**Major Constituents (3 marks):** As tabulated in SECTION 2.1, with 1,8-cineole (30–45%) and α-terpinyl acetate (25–35%) being the dominant compounds.

**Cultivar Differentiation via GC-MS (4 marks):**

$$\text{Authenticity Ratio} = R = \frac{\%\ \text{1,8-Cineole}}{\%\ \text{α-Terpinyl Acetate}}$$

- **Malabar cultivar:** $R \approx 1.5$ (high cineole, sharper aroma)
- **Mysore cultivar:** $R \approx 1.0$ (balanced, sweeter)
- **Vazhukka cultivar:** $R \approx 1.2$ (intermediate)

GC-MS (Gas Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry) fingerprinting of the oil's terpene profile allows **adulteration detection** — synthetic cineole addition to inflate oil volume can be identified by an abnormally high $R$ value.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the **fixed oil** component of cardamom — its composition, properties, and extraction method. Compare cold pressing with solvent extraction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write notes on: (i) **Oleoresin** of cardamom, (ii) **Hydrosol** as a by-product, (iii) Quality parameters specified by **AGMARK** for cardamom capsules. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Cardamom Fixed Oil (7 marks)

**Composition (2 marks):** Predominantly triglycerides of oleic (42–50%), linoleic (25–35%), palmitic (8–12%), and stearic (1–3%) acids. Minor components include phytosterols (β-sitosterol) and tocopherols.

**Properties (2 marks):**
- Appearance: Golden-yellow liquid
- Specific gravity: 0.91–0.93 g/mL at 25 °C
- Iodine value: 75–95 g I₂/100 g
- Saponification value: 160–190 mg KOH/g

**Extraction — Cold Pressing (1.5 marks):**
Mechanical pressing at ambient temperature (≤ 40 °C) preserves heat-labile compounds. Lower yield (~1–2%), but **no solvent residue** and **high bioactive retention**.

**Extraction — Solvent (1.5 marks):**
Uses hexane/acetone to extract oil + resin together → **oleoresin**. Higher yield, but requires **solvent stripping** to meet food safety limits (hexane residue < 5 ppm per FSSAI).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Short Notes (7 marks)

**(i) Oleoresin (2.5 marks):**
A dark-green viscous semi-solid obtained by **solvent extraction** of crushed cardamom seeds. Contains both essential oil (10–15%) and fixed oil + resinous matter (85–90%). Used in **industrial flavor formulations** (instant chai, ready-to-eat curries, pickles). Standardized to specific cineole content.

**(ii) Hydrosol (2.5 marks):**
The **aromatic water** separated out after steam distillation. Contains trace amounts (~0.1%) of dissolved essential oil and water-soluble aroma compounds. Commercial applications:
- Aromatherapy mist
- Skincare toner
- Flavoring in beverages (no synthetic chemicals)

**(iii) AGMARK Quality Parameters (2 marks):**
The **AGMARK (Agricultural Marketing)** certification for cardamom specifies:
- **Grade designations:** AGEB, AGB, AGS, AGS-1, AGS-2 (based on capsule size)
- **Moisture content:** < 13%
- **Essential oil content:** ≥ 3.5% for premium grade
- **Extraneous matter:** < 2%
- **Empty/lighter capsules:** < 3%

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Cardamom belongs to **Zingiberaceae** family; Kerala's Malabar variety is the most premium.
- ✅ Two distinct lipid fractions exist: **essential (volatile) oil** [2–8%] and **fixed oil** [1–2% in capsule; up to 10% in seeds].
- ✅ **1,8-Cineole (30–45%)** and **α-Terpinyl acetate (25–35%)** are the two marker compounds.
- ✅ **Steam distillation** is the gold-standard extraction method; supercritical CO₂ is the premium alternative.
- ✅ Steam-to-charge ratio is **3:1 (kg/kg)**; distillation time is **3–5 hours**.
- ✅ **AGEB** grade requires capsules ≥ 7 mm and oil yield ≥ 3.5% (v/w).
- ✅ **Oleoresin ≠ Essential Oil** — oleoresin includes fixed oil + resin + volatile oil.
- ✅ Fixed oil is rich in **oleic and linoleic** acids (semi-drying oil).
- ✅ **Florentine separator** exploits density difference to recover oil from condensate.
- ✅ **Authenticity ratio** (1,8-cineole : α-terpinyl acetate) detects cultivar fraud and adulteration.
- ✅ **AGMARK** and **FSSAI** govern quality and safety standards for commercial cardamom products.
- ✅ Hydrosol is a value-added by-product — never discard it in industrial practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering 14-mark questions, always structure your response as — *Definition → Principle → Process → Parameters → Diagram → Applications → Conclusion*. Examiners award **2 marks specifically for a well-labelled diagram** in process questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beverages - Carbonated Non-Alcoholic Beverages or Soft Drinks Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Beverages - Carbonated Non-Alcoholic Beverages or Soft Drinks Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Carbonated Non-Alcoholic Beverages (CNABs)**, popularly called **soft drinks**, are potable beverages (containing less than **0.5% alcohol by volume**) in which **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** gas has been dissolved under pressure to impart effervescence, tanginess, and a characteristic pungent bite. They are formulated from treated water, sugar (or non-nutritive sweeteners), edible acids, natural/synthetic flavours, permitted colours, and preservatives.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a soft drink like a **"pressurised party balloon of water with flavour"**. When you dissolve CO₂ into chilled, sweetened, flavoured water under high pressure, the gas gets trapped inside the liquid (just like guests are trapped inside a crowded party). The moment you pop the cap (release the pressure), the gas rushes out as bubbles — that is the *fizz* you feel on your tongue. The "bite" of the drink is actually mild **carbonic acid (H₂CO₃)** formed when CO₂ reacts with water.

### Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

- **Brix (°Bx)**: Sugar concentration of the syrup, measured with a refractometer. Standard soft drink = **10–13 °Bx** finished product.
- **Carbonation Volumes**: One "volume" = amount of CO₂ equal to the volume of the liquid at STP.
  - Cola drinks = **3.0 – 3.5 volumes** of CO₂
  - Sparkling water = **3.5 – 4.5 volumes**
- **FSSAI Limit (India)**: Soft drinks must contain **not less than 3.0 volumes of CO₂** at 15.5 °C.
- **pH of soft drink**: typically **2.5 – 3.5** (acidic to inhibit microbial growth).
- **Serving temperature**: **4 °C – 6 °C** (chilled for maximum fizz perception).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why carbonation matters?**
> - Provides characteristic mouthfeel (tingling sensation).
> - Acts as a mild preservative (lowers pH, displaces O₂).
> - Enhances flavour perception by stimulating trigeminal nerve receptors.
> - Suppresses flat-note off-flavours.

### Classification of Soft Drinks (KTU Module Favourite)

| Type | Example | Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Cola-type** | Coca-Cola, Pepsi | Caramel colour, phosphoric acid, caffeine |
| **Clear/Transparent** | 7-Up, Sprite | Lemon-lime flavour, citric acid |
| **Fruit-flavoured** | Fanta, Mirinda | Added fruit pulp/essence |
| **Tonic Water** | Schweppes | Contains **quinine** |
| **Club Soda** | Soda water | Plain carbonated water with mineral salts |
| **Diet / Sugar-free** | Diet Coke | Aspartame / Sucralose as sweetener |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Raw Materials & Their Functional Role

- **Water (≈ 90–92 %)** → Must be demineralised, dechlorinated, and microbiologically pure. Hardness must be **< 50 mg/L CaCO₃**.
- **Sugar (Sucrose, 8–12 %)** → Provides sweetness, body, and mouthfeel. Measured in **°Brix**.
- **CO₂ (Food grade, purity ≥ 99.9 %)** → Provides carbonation. The **single most critical ingredient**.
- **Acidulants** → Phosphoric acid (cola) or citric acid (fruit-flavoured). Maintains pH 2.5–3.5.
- **Flavours** → Natural / Nature-identical / Artificial (as per FSSAI INS Nos.).
- **Colours** → Caramel (E150d), Sunset Yellow (E110), Tartrazine (E102).
- **Preservatives** → **Sodium Benzoate (0.012 % max)** or **Potassium Sorbate**.
- **Acidity Regulators** → Citrate / phosphate buffers.

### 2.2 The Five Pillars of Soft Drink Processing

1. **Water Treatment** → Sedimentation → Sand filtration → Activated Carbon → Ion-exchange softener → UV/ozone sterilisation → Polishing filter (1 µm).
2. **Sugar Syrup Preparation** → Dissolve sugar in treated water at **70–80 °C**, filter through 50 µm screen, cool to **4 °C**.
3. **Syrup–Water Mixing (Brix Standardisation)** → Inline proportioner mixes syrup with treated water to achieve target **°Brix**.
4. **Carbonation** → Syrup–water blend is chilled to **4 °C** and mixed with CO₂ in a **carbonator** at **3.5–4.5 bar (50–65 psi)**.
5. **Filling & Packaging** → Counter-pressure filler fills PET/glass bottles/can at 4 °C, **below 2 °C helps retention**; immediately crowned/capped.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Examination**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Henry's Law (CO₂ dissolution)** | $C = k_H \cdot P$ | $C$ = dissolved CO₂ (g/L), $k_H$ = Henry's constant, $P$ = partial pressure of CO₂ (atm) |
| 2 | **Carbonation Volume** | $V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{\rho_{CO_2}^{STP}}$ | $m_{CO_2}$ mass dissolved (g), $\rho_{CO_2}^{STP}$ = **1.977 g/L** at STP |
| 3 | **Brix Dilution (Pearson's Square)** | $\dfrac{S_{syrup}}{S_{water}} = \dfrac{R_{final} - R_{water}}{R_{syrup} - R_{final}}$ | $R$ = °Brix values |
| 4 | **Pressure-Temp relation for CO₂** | $P_{CO_2} \propto e^{+\Delta H / RT}$ | Higher temperature → **lower CO₂ retention** |
| 5 | **Sugar mass calculation** | $m_{sugar} = V_{drink} \times B \times \rho_{syrup}$ | $B$ = Brix fraction, $V$ in L |
| 6 | **Caffeine limit (FSSAI)** | $\le 145 \text{ mg/L}$ (cola-type) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often write Henry's Law as $P = kC$ instead of the correct **$C = k_H P$** (concentration proportional to partial pressure). Examiners will deduct marks.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Food Engineering

- **Solubility Science**: Lowering temperature from **20 °C → 4 °C** increases CO₂ solubility by **~30 %** — which is why all soft drink factories are kept cold.
- **Microbial Safety**: pH 2.5–3.5 + cold fill + benzoate gives a **Hurdle Technology** barrier — even if bottling equipment has minor contamination, spoilage is prevented.
- **Shelf Life**: Typical shelf life = **6–9 months** in glass; **4–6 months** in PET (CO₂ slowly permeates through PET walls at ~0.1 %/week).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CO₂ Requirement for a Soft Drink Batch

**Given**: Produce **10,000 L** of finished soft drink at **12 °Brix** and **3.5 volumes of CO₂** at **15.5 °C**.

**Step 1 — Convert °Brix to mass of sugar per litre.**
At 12 °Brix, finished drink contains approximately **120 g solids per kg of solution**. With $\rho \approx 1.04 \text{ kg/L}$:

$$
m_{sugar} = 10{,}000 \text{ L} \times 1.04 \text{ kg/L} \times 0.12 = 1248 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Carbonation volume to mass of CO₂.**
One "volume" means **1 L of CO₂ at STP per 1 L of drink**. Density of CO₂ at STP is **1.977 g/L**. So for 3.5 volumes:

$$
m_{CO_2} = 10{,}000 \text{ L} \times 3.5 \text{ vol} \times 1.977 \text{ g/L} = 69{,}195 \text{ g} \approx 69.2 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Henry's Law to determine required carbonator pressure.**
At 4 °C, the Henry's constant for CO₂ in water is approximately $k_H \approx 1.45 \text{ g/(L·atm)}$.

$$
P = \frac{C}{k_H} = \frac{69{,}195 \text{ g} / 10{,}000 \text{ L}}{1.45 \text{ g/(L·atm)}} = \frac{6.92 \text{ g/L}}{1.45} \approx 4.77 \text{ atm}
$$

Adding head-space and friction losses, the **carbonator is operated at ≈ 5.2 atm (≈ 76 psi)**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Process Variables & Their Effect

| Process Variable | Direction of Change | Effect on CO₂ Retention | Effect on Sweetness | Effect on Microbial Load |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature ↓ | Decrease | **↑ Retention** (more soluble) | Slight ↑ perception | ↓ (cold inhibits microbes) |
| Pressure ↑ | Increase | **↑ Retention** (Henry's Law) | None | None directly |
| °Brix ↑ | Increase | Slight ↓ retention (salting-out effect) | **↑ Sweetness** | None |
| pH ↓ | Decrease | Slight ↑ retention | ↑ Tartness | **↓ Spoilage** |
| Headspace O₂ ↓ | Decrease | Preserves ascorbic acid / flavour | None | ↓ Aerobic spoilage |
| Agitation ↑ | Increase | None (equilibrium) | None | Possible O₂ pickup |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Pearson's Square for Syrup Dilution

A factory prepares a **60 °Brix** sugar syrup and needs to dilute it with water (0 °Brix) to make finished drink at **11 °Brix**. Find the syrup-to-water ratio.

$$
\text{Syrup} : \text{Water} = (R_{final} - R_{water}) : (R_{syrup} - R_{final})
$$

$$
\text{Syrup} : \text{Water} = (11 - 0) : (60 - 11) = 11 : 49
$$

**Verification**: Total parts = 60. Final Brix = $\frac{11 \times 60 + 49 \times 0}{60} = 11 \text{ °Brix}$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Soft Drink Production Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Water Intake] --> B[Sand Filtration]
    B --> C[Activated Carbon Filter]
    C --> D[Ion Exchange Softener]
    D --> E[UV Sterilisation Unit]
    E --> F[Polishing Filter 1 Micron]
    F --> G[Carbonator Feed Water Tank]
    H[Sugar Storage Silo] --> I[Sugar Dissolution Tank at 75 C]
    I --> J[Syrup Filter 50 Micron]
    J --> K[Cooler to 4 C]
    K --> L[Inline Proportioner]
    G --> L
    L --> M[Carbonator 5 atm CO2]
    M --> N[Buffer Tank]
    N --> O[Counter Pressure Bottle Filler]
    P[CO2 Cylinder Bank] --> M
    Q[PET Bottle Blower] --> O
    O --> R[Crowning and Capping]
    R --> S[Date Coding and Shrink Wrap]
    S --> T[Storage at 4 C]
    T --> U[Distribution]
```

### 4.2 Functional Interaction Map — Quality Control Loops

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WT[Water Treatment] -->|Residual chlorine monitor| QC1[QC Lab]
    SY[Syrup Preparation] -->|Brix refractometer| QC1
    CA[Carbonator] -->|Pressure and CO2 analyser| QC1
    FI[Filler] -->|Fill height and torque tester| QC1
    QC1 -->|Feedback| WT
    QC1 -->|Feedback| SY
    QC1 -->|Feedback| CA
    QC1 -->|Feedback| FI
```

### 4.3 Process State Diagram — Carbonation Equilibrium

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pressurising
    Pressurising --> Saturating: P reaches 5 atm
    Saturating --> Equilibrated: 3.5 volumes CO2
    Equilibrated --> Filling: At 4 C
    Filling --> Bottled: Counter pressure fill
    Bottled --> ShelfStorage: Capped and labelled
    ShelfStorage --> Consumption: Opened
    Consumption --> [*]
    Equilibrated --> Repressurise: CO2 drops below 3.0 vol
    Repressurise --> Saturating
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Define "carbonation volume" of a soft drink. What is the FSSAI minimum requirement?

**Model Answer:**
One **carbonation volume** is defined as the volume of CO₂ (measured at STP) dissolved per unit volume of the beverage. For example, a drink with 3.5 volumes contains 3.5 L of CO₂ at STP per 1 L of beverage. The **FSSAI minimum requirement** for carbonated non-alcoholic beverages is **not less than 3.0 volumes of CO₂ at 15.5 °C**.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
List any **three functions** of carbon dioxide in a soft drink.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Effervescence (Fizz)**: Dissolved CO₂ escapes as bubbles on opening, giving the characteristic "tingling" mouthfeel.
2. **Mild Preservation**: Forms carbonic acid (lowers pH) and displaces dissolved O₂, retarding microbial and oxidative spoilage.
3. **Flavour Enhancement**: Mild carbonic acid stimulates the trigeminal nerve, enhancing the perception of tangy and fruity notes.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **complete manufacturing process of carbonated non-alcoholic beverages**, mentioning critical process parameters at each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain **Henry's Law** as applied to soft drink carbonation. A plant produces **50,000 L/day** of cola at **3.5 volumes** of CO₂. Calculate the **mass of CO₂ required per day** and the **minimum carbonator pressure** at **4 °C**, given $k_H = 1.45 \text{ g/(L·atm)}$.

**Model Solution (a):**

The manufacturing process has **five unit operations**:

1. **Water Treatment** (T = ambient; target hardness < 50 mg/L CaCO₃, residual Cl₂ < 0.1 ppm):
   - Sand filter → Activated carbon (dechlorination) → Ion-exchange softener → UV steriliser → 1 µm polishing filter.

2. **Sugar Syrup Preparation** (T = 70–80 °C; target 60 °Brix):
   - Sugar dissolved in treated water in a jacketed kettle.
   - Filtered through 50 µm screen, cooled to 4 °C.

3. **Brix Standardisation** (T = 4 °C; 11–13 °Brix finished):
   - Inline proportioner mixes syrup with treated water in calculated ratio (Pearson's Square).

4. **Carbonation** (T = 4 °C; P = 4.5–5.2 atm; 3.0–3.8 vol CO₂):
   - Chilled syrup–water blend passes through a **carbonator** where CO₂ is injected under pressure.
   - Inline CO₂ analyser verifies the carbonation level.

5. **Filling & Packaging** (T = 4 °C; counter-pressure):
   - Counter-pressure filler for glass bottles; rotary filler for PET/can.
   - Immediate crowning/capping; date coding; shrink-wrap packing; cold storage.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Mass of CO₂ per day:**
Density of CO₂ at STP = **1.977 g/L**.

$$
m_{CO_2} = 50{,}000 \text{ L} \times 3.5 \times 1.977 \text{ g/L} = 346{,}000 \text{ g} = 346 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Concentration of CO₂:**

$$
C = \frac{346{,}000 \text{ g}}{50{,}000 \text{ L}} = 6.92 \text{ g/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Carbonator pressure by Henry's Law ($C = k_H \cdot P$):**

$$
P = \frac{C}{k_H} = \frac{6.92 \text{ g/L}}{1.45 \text{ g/(L·atm)}} \approx 4.77 \text{ atm}
$$

**Practical operating pressure ≈ 5.2 atm (≈ 76 psi)** to account for friction losses, temperature fluctuations, and head-space CO₂.

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of each major ingredient** (water, sugar, CO₂, acidulants, preservatives) in a soft drink formulation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Pearson's Square method** with an example. A factory prepares a **55 °Brix** syrup. How much syrup and water (0 °Brix) are needed to make **5000 L** of finished drink at **11 °Brix**?

**Model Solution (a):**

| Ingredient | Function | Typical Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Water** | Universal solvent; provides body; > 90 % of drink | 88 – 92 % |
| **Sugar** | Sweetness, mouthfeel, body, energy | 8 – 12 % (10–13 °Brix) |
| **CO₂** | Carbonation, mild preservation, flavour lift | 3.0 – 4.5 volumes |
| **Acidulants** (phosphoric / citric) | Tartness, microbial inhibition, balance | 0.1 – 0.3 % |
| **Preservatives** (Na-benzoate / K-sorbate) | Inhibit yeast & mould | ≤ 0.012 % (120 ppm) |
| **Flavours** (natural / synthetic) | Characteristic taste | 0.05 – 0.2 % |
| **Colours** (Caramel, Sunset Yellow) | Visual appeal | ≤ 100 ppm |

**Model Solution (b):**

Pearson's Square is a graphical method for **diluting a concentrate to a desired intermediate concentration** when the diluent is pure (zero concentration). The formula:

$$
\text{Syrup parts} : \text{Water parts} = (R_{final} - R_{water}) : (R_{syrup} - R_{final})
$$

For our problem:

$$
\text{Syrup : Water} = (11 - 0) : (55 - 11) = 11 : 44
$$

Total parts = 55. Total volume required = 5000 L.

$$
\text{Syrup needed} = 5000 \times \frac{11}{55} = 1000 \text{ L}
$$

$$
\text{Water needed} = 5000 \times \frac{44}{55} = 4000 \text{ L}
$$

**Verification**: $1000 \times 55 \text{ °Bx} = 55{,}000$ °Brix-Litres ÷ 5000 L = **11 °Brix** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** — Where Students Lose Marks:
> 1. **Forgetting units** in Henry's Law (always specify g/L and atm).
> 2. **Using density 1.96 instead of 1.977 g/L** for CO₂ at STP — minor but examiners notice.
> 3. **Mixing up Pearson's Square parts** — students often write water:syrup ratio inverted.
> 4. **Omitting cooling step** before carbonation (Examiner's *favourite* deduct-mark trap).
> 5. **Not mentioning counter-pressure filling** — a major process control step.
> 6. **Failing to state FSSAI limits** in Part A 3-mark answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Soft drink** = CO₂ + water + sugar/acid + flavour + colour, with **< 0.5 % alcohol** (essentially 0 %).
- **Standard carbonation** for cola = **3.0 – 3.5 volumes**; standard **°Brix = 10–13**.
- **Henry's Law** governs dissolution: $C = k_H \cdot P$. **Lower T & higher P → more CO₂ retained**.
- **Five unit operations**: Water treatment → Syrup prep → Brix standardisation → Carbonation → Cold filling.
- **Counter-pressure filler** is mandatory to avoid CO₂ loss and foaming.
- **FSSAI minimum** = **3.0 volumes CO₂**; **Sodium benzoate limit** = **120 ppm**; **caffeine in cola** = **≤ 145 mg/L**.
- **PET vs Glass**: PET allows slow CO₂ permeation; shelf-life of PET drink is **shorter** than glass.
- **Hurdle technology** (low pH + CO₂ + benzoate + cold) gives the typical **6–9 month shelf life** without refrigeration (unopened).
- **Pearson's Square** for syrup:water = (R_f − R_w) : (R_s − R_f).
- **Always cool syrup to 4 °C before carbonation** — this is the single most repeated KTU 2-mark point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alcoholic Beverages - Processing of Beer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alcoholic Beverages - Processing of Beer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍺 Module 4: Alcoholic Beverages — Processing of Beer

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Beer)**
> Beer is a globally consumed, mildly alcoholic (typically **3–8% ABV**), carbonated beverage produced through the **controlled alcoholic fermentation of a starchy substrate (wort)** — derived primarily from **malted barley** — using a selected strain of yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae* for ales or *Saccharomyces pastorianus* for lagers), and **flavored/aromatized with hops** (*Humulus lupulus*). The resulting beverage is a complex colloidal system containing ethanol, CO₂, dextrins, proteins, polyphenols, hop resins, and minerals.

### The Three Biochemical Pillars of Beer Production
| Pillar | Biological Agent | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Enzymatic Conversion** | Amylases from malted barley | Starch → Fermentable sugars (maltose, glucose) |
| **Fermentation** | Yeast (*Saccharomyces* sp.) | Sugars → Ethanol + CO₂ |
| **Flavoring & Preservation** | Hops (lupulin glands) | Bitterness, aroma, antimicrobial stability |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Bread in Liquid Form"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine you are making **bread**. You mix flour (starch) + water + yeast. The yeast eats sugar and releases CO₂, which makes the bread rise. Now, imagine that **exact same process**, but:
> 1. You first **break the starch into sugar** using enzymes (because yeast cannot eat raw starch).
> 2. You keep the mixture **liquid in a sealed tank** (so CO₂ cannot escape and instead carbonates the liquid).
> 3. You add **hops** — a bitter flower — to balance the sweet malt and prevent bacterial spoilage.
>
> **That liquid, after fermentation and aging, is beer.** It is, in essence, "carbonated liquid bread" — an ancient technology (~5,000+ years old, attributed to Mesopotamian/Sumerian civilizations).

### 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context

> [!NOTE]
> - **Reinheitsgebot (1516, German Purity Law)**: Bavarian law restricting beer ingredients to **only water, barley, and hops** (yeast was added later once discovered by Pasteur in 1857).
> - **Standard Metrics**:
>   - **Original Gravity (OG)** = 1.040–1.055 (typical)
>   - **Final Gravity (FG)** = 1.005–1.015
>   - **ABV** = 3.0–8.0 % (mass/mass)
>   - **IBU (International Bitterness Units)** = 5–120
>   - **SRM Color** = 2 (pale lager) to 40+ (stout)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Beer — Theoretical Foundation

Beer production is governed by **three sequential biochemical reactions** plus several physical separation steps.

### A. Enzymatic Hydrolysis of Starch (Mashing)
Malted barley contains the natural enzymes **α-amylase** and **β-amylase**.

$$\text{Starch (amylose + amylopectin)} \xrightarrow{\text{α-amylase}} \text{Maltodextrins (long chains)}$$

$$\text{Maltodextrins} \xrightarrow{\text{β-amylase}} \text{Maltose (disaccharide)} \xrightarrow{\text{maltase}} \text{Glucose}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two temperatures?**
> - **α-amylase** optimum: **70–75 °C** (produces dextrins → body/mouthfeel of beer)
> - **β-amylase** optimum: **60–65 °C** (produces maltose → fermentable sugar)
> - Above 80 °C → enzymes denature (saccharification stops)

### B. Fermentation (The Core Reaction)
This is the **Gay-Lussac equation of alcoholic fermentation**:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (yeast)}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{Energy (ATP)}$$

**Molar mass accounting:**
$$180 \text{ g/mol (glucose)} \rightarrow 2 \times 46 + 2 \times 44 = 92 + 88 = 180 \text{ g/mol}$$

So theoretically, **100 g glucose** yields:
- **51.1 g ethanol**
- **48.9 g CO₂**

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, yeast also uses ~5% of sugar for **cell growth (biomass)** and produces minor by-products (esters, higher alcohols, diacetyl). Real yield ≈ **48% ethanol**, **46% CO₂**, ~6% biomass/by-products.

### C. Hop Chemistry (Bitterness & Aroma)
Hops contain **α-acids (humulone)** that are isomerized during boiling to **iso-α-acids**, which provide bitterness.

$$\text{Humulone} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat (boil)},\, \text{pH}>5} \text{Iso-humulone (bitter)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hops added early in boil (60+ min)** → bitterness.
> **Hops added late (5–15 min)** → aroma/flavor (volatile oils preserved).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Standard Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ABV (Simple)** = (OG − FG) × 131.25 | OG, FG = Specific Gravity | Dimensionless → % | Standard alcohol estimation |
| 2 | **ABV (Advanced)** = (OE − AE) × 0.819 / 0.794 | OE = Original Extract, AE = Apparent Extract (°Plato) | % v/v | More accurate lab method |
| 3 | **Theoretical Ethanol Yield** = m_glucose × 0.511 | m_glucose = mass of glucose | g | Mass balance |
| 4 | **Real Ethanol Yield** = m_glucose × 0.48 | accounts for biomass loss | g | Industrial reality |
| 5 | **Extract Yield (%)** = (Wort extract (kg) / Grist (kg)) × 100 | Wort extract, grain bill | % | Brewhouse efficiency |
| 6 | **Bitterness (IBU)** = (C_AA × V × 1000) / (V_w × 10) | C_AA = iso-α-acid conc., V = hop α-acid, V_w = wort volume | mg/L | Hops dosing |
| 7 | **Hop Utilization (U)** = f(t, gravity, pH) | t = boil time, often 25–30% | % | Empirical: U ≈ 0.000125 × t(min) × (1 + e^(0.04×(t−60))) |
| 8 | **Degree of Fermentation (RDF)** = ((OE − RE) / OE) × 100 | OE = original extract, RE = real extract | % | Fermentation completeness |
| 9 | **Plato (°P) to SG** = SG = 1 + (°P / (258.6 − (°P/258.2 × 227.1))) | Plato degrees | Dimensionless | Density conversion |
| 10 | **CO₂ produced** = m_ethanol × (44/46) = m_ethanol × 0.9565 | m_ethanol = ethanol mass | g | Carbonation calculation |
| 11 | **Mashing Water-to-Grist Ratio** = 2.5 : 1 to 3.5 : 1 | L of water per kg of grain | L/kg | Lautering |
| 12 | **Diastatic Power (DP)** = °Lintner (°L) | Lintner value | °L | Malt enzyme strength |
| 13 | **Energy balance (boiling)** Q = m·c·ΔT + m·L_v | m=mass, c=specific heat, L_v=latent heat | kJ | Kettle design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions (always memorize):**
> - Mashing: **45–78 °C**
> - Boiling: **>95 °C for 60–90 min**
> - Fermentation: **Ale 18–22 °C / Lager 8–13 °C**
> - Maturation: **0–4 °C for lagers**, 15–20 °C for ales
> - pH of wort: **5.2–5.6** (acidified with CaSO₄ or lactic acid bacteria)

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does a Chemical Engineer study this?**
> 1. **Mass & Energy Balances**: Designing brewhouse heat exchangers, kettles, fermenters.
> 2. **Reaction Engineering**: Modeling yeast kinetics (Monod equation), enzyme kinetics (Michaelis-Menten).
> 3. **Process Control**: Maintaining temperature profiles, pH, dissolved O₂, gravity.
> 4. **Unit Operations**: Milling, lautering (filtration), centrifugation, pasteurization, aseptic packaging.
> 5. **Scale-up**: Lab-scale (5 L) → Pilot (500 L) → Industrial (500,000 L).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Process Flow & Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 The Eight Sequential Stages of Beer Processing

> [!NOTE]
> Beer manufacturing is conventionally divided into **two broad phases**:
> - **BREWHOUSE (cold side + hot side)**: Steps 1–5
> - **FERMENTATION & FINISHING (cold side)**: Steps 6–8

### **Stage 1 — Raw Material Reception & Storage**
**Inputs:** Malted barley (malt), adjuncts (maize, rice, sorghum), hops (pellets/extract), yeast, water, CO₂.

**Step 1.1:** Malt is received at 4–8% moisture, screened for foreign material, and stored in silos at <20 °C, <60% RH to preserve enzymatic activity.

**Step 1.2:** Water is **treated** (decarbonation by lime, reverse osmosis, or ion exchange) because brewing water mineral profile critically affects beer style (e.g., **Pilsen water = very soft**; **Dublin water = high CaCO₃**).

---

### **Stage 2 — Milling (Crushing the Malt)**
The malt grains are passed through a **roller mill** to crack the husk and expose the starchy endosperm.

**Target particle size distribution:**
- Husk: intact (largest particles — used as filter bed during lautering)
- Grist (endosperm): fine powder (maximizes enzyme-substrate contact)
- Fines <5%

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not grind too fine?** Fine flour clogs the lauter tun, slows filtration, and extracts **excess tannins** from the husk (causing astringency). Coarse grind → poor sugar extraction. This is a critical **mass-transfer** optimization problem.

---

### **Stage 3 — Mashing (Starch → Sugar Conversion)**

Mashing is a **solid–liquid extraction + enzymatic hydrolysis** performed in a **Mash Tun** (insulated, steam-jacketed vessel).

**Step 3.1 — Dough-in:** Milled grist is mixed with hot water at **~50 °C** (water-to-grist = 2.5–3.5 L/kg). This is the **"Acid Rest"** that lowers pH to 5.4 via phytase enzyme activity.

**Step 3.2 — Temperature Rests (Time–Temperature Program):**

| Rest | Temperature | Time | Enzyme Active | Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acid Rest | 35–45 °C | 15–30 min | Phytase | Lowers pH (5.5→5.2) |
| Protein Rest | 45–55 °C | 15–30 min | Protease | Free amino acids (FAN) for yeast |
| β-Amylase Rest | 60–65 °C | 45–60 min | β-amylase | Maltose (fermentable) |
| α-Amylase Rest | 70–75 °C | 20–30 min | α-amylase | Dextrins (body) |
| Mash-off | 78 °C | 5 min | Enzyme deactivation | Stops reaction |

**Step 3.3 — Iodine Test:** A drop of iodine is added to cooled mash. **No blue color** = starch fully converted = **saccharification complete**.

---

### **Stage 4 — Lautering (Wort Separation)**
The sweet liquid (wort) must be **separated from the spent grains**.

**Step 4.1:** Mash is transferred to the **Lauter Tun** (false-bottom vessel).
**Step 4.2 — Mash-out:** Temperature raised to **78 °C** to stop enzyme activity and reduce wort viscosity.
**Step 4.3 — Recirculation (Vorlauf):** First cloudy wort is recirculated until **clear** (particles act as filter aid).
**Step 4.4 — Sparging:** Hot water (**76–78 °C**) is sprayed over the grain bed to extract residual sugars.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lautering is a deep-bed filtration** governed by **Darcy's Law**:
> $$Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$
> where k = permeability of grain bed, A = filter area, ΔP = pressure drop, μ = wort viscosity, L = bed depth.

**Step 4.5 — Boiling Stage Decision:** Wort is collected in the kettle until the **run-off gravity drops below 1.008** (1.5–2.5 L/kg sparge water used).

---

### **Stage 5 — Boiling with Hops (Wort Boil)**

Wort is boiled vigorously in the **Brew Kettle** for **60–90 minutes**.

**Purposes (5-fold):**
1. **Hop isomerization:** α-acids → iso-α-acids (bitterness)
2. **Sterilization:** kill wild microorganisms
3. **Coagulation of proteins** (hot break / trub formation)
4. **Evaporation of undesirable volatiles** (DMS — dimethyl sulfide)
5. **Concentration** of wort (~5–10% volume loss)

**Hop Addition Schedule (typical 60-min boil):**
- **Bittering hops:** 60 min before flame-out (full utilization)
- **Flavor hops:** 15 min before flame-out
- **Aroma hops:** 5 min or **whirlpool/dry-hop** (preserves volatile oils)

---

### **Stage 6 — Cooling & Aeration**

After boiling, wort must be rapidly cooled from **100 °C → fermentation temperature (8–22 °C)**.

**Equipment:** **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** — counter-current flow of wort and chilled water/glycol.

**Step 6.1:** Cooling: 100 °C → 20 °C in **<30 min** (rapid = less DMS, less oxidation).
**Step 6.2:** **Aeration / Oxygenation:** Pure O₂ injected to **8–12 mg/L** (yeast requires O₂ for membrane lipid synthesis before anaerobic fermentation).
**Step 6.3 — Cold Break Removal:** Whirlpool or centrifuge removes protein-polyphenol precipitates.

---

### **Stage 7 — Fermentation**

Wort is pitched with yeast (1 × 10⁶ cells/mL per °Plato) in a **Fermentation Vessel (FV)** — either:
- **Cylindro-conical tank (CCT)** — modern standard, height:diameter = 3:1
- **Open square fermenter** — traditional ales

**Yeast Metabolism Phases:**
| Phase | Time | Substrate | Products |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lag | 0–8 h | O₂ uptake | Biomass synthesis |
| Growth | 8–24 h | Sugars + O₂ | Cells, CO₂ |
| Fermentation (exponential) | 1–5 days | Glucose, maltose | Ethanol, CO₂ |
| Stationary | 5–7 days | Maltotriose | Slow ethanol |
| Flocculation | 7–10 days | — | Yeast settles to cone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ale fermentation:** 18–22 °C, 5–7 days, *S. cerevisiae* (top-cropping).
> **Lager fermentation:** 8–13 °C, 7–14 days, *S. pastorianus* (bottom-cropping).

---

### **Stage 8 — Maturation (Conditioning) & Finishing**

**A. Maturation (Lagering):**
- Temperature: **0–4 °C** (lager) for **2–8 weeks**
- Purpose: chill haze precipitation, diacetyl reduction, flavor mellowing
- Diacetyl (butterscotch off-flavor) is re-absorbed by yeast:
$$\text{Diacetyl (2,3-butanedione)} \xrightarrow{\text{yeast reductase}} \text{2,3-Butanediol (tasteless)}$$

**B. Filtration:**
- **Kieselguhr (diatomaceous earth) filter** or **cross-flow membrane (PVDF)**
- Removes yeast cells, proteins → produces "bright" beer

**C. Carbonation:**
- Forced CO₂ injection to **2.5–3.0 volumes CO₂** (one volume = 1 L CO₂ per L beer at STP)

**D. Packaging:**
- **Bottling (350/650 mL)**, **Canning (330/440 mL)**, or **Kegging (50 L)**
- **Pasteurization**: Tunnel pasteurizer (PU = 15–20) for bottles/cans; flash pasteurizer for kegs.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A brewery produces **10,000 L of beer/day** at OG = 1.050, FG = 1.010, extract = 12.5 °P. Find (a) ABV, (b) ethanol mass produced, (c) CO₂ mass released.

### Solution:

**(a) ABV Calculation:**
$$\text{ABV} = (\text{OG} - \text{FG}) \times 131.25$$
$$\text{ABV} = (1.050 - 1.010) \times 131.25 = 0.040 \times 131.25$$
$$\boxed{\text{ABV} = 5.25\% \, \text{v/v}}$$

**(b) Ethanol Mass:**
Volume of beer = 10,000 L
Volume of ethanol = 10,000 × 0.0525 = **525 L**

Density of ethanol = **0.789 kg/L**

$$\text{Mass of ethanol} = 525 \times 0.789 = 414.2 \text{ kg}$$
$$\boxed{m_{\text{ethanol}} \approx 414.2 \text{ kg/day}}$$

**(c) CO₂ Mass Released:**
By stoichiometry: 46 g ethanol ↔ 44 g CO₂
$$\text{Mass of CO}_2 = 414.2 \times \left(\frac{44}{46}\right) = 414.2 \times 0.9565$$
$$\boxed{m_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 396.2 \text{ kg/day}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The ~**396 kg/day CO₂** is not lost — it is **captured, scrubbed, and re-used** for carbonation in Stage 8 (closed-loop economy).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Beer Production Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords, no markdown formatting inside labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Material Receipt: Malt Hops Yeast Water] --> B1[Milling Roller Mill]
    B1 --> C1[Mashing Mash Tun 45 to 75 C]
    C1 --> D1[Lautering Lauter Tun Filtration]
    D1 --> E1[Wort Boiling Brew Kettle 60 to 90 min with Hops]
    E1 --> F1[Whirlpool Hot Break Removal]
    F1 --> G1[Plate Heat Exchanger Cooling to 8 to 22 C]
    G1 --> H1[Aeration O2 Injection 8 to 12 mg per L]
    H1 --> I1[Fermentation Cylindro Conical Tank 5 to 14 days]
    I1 --> J1[Maturation Lagering 0 to 4 C for 2 to 8 weeks]
    J1 --> K1[Filtration Kieselguhr or Membrane]
    K1 --> L1[Carbonation CO2 to 2.5 to 3.0 Volumes]
    L1 --> M1[Packaging Bottle Can or Keg]
    M1 --> N1[Pasteurization Tunnel or Flash]
    N1 --> O1[Finished Beer Storage and Dispatch]
    I1 --> I2[CO2 Capture Scrubbing and Reuse]
    I2 --> L1
```

## 4.2 The Mashing Temperature Program (Infographic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start 35 C Acid Rest] --> P2[45 C Protein Rest]
    P2 --> P3[62 C Beta Amylase Maltose]
    P3 --> P4[72 C Alpha Amylase Dextrins]
    P4 --> P5[78 C Mash Off Enzyme Stop]
    P5 --> P6[Saccharification Complete Iodine Test Negative]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Mass & Energy Flow in Brewhouse

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Grain Solids 1000 kg] -->|Heat 75 C| M1[Mash Tun Reactor]
    IN2[Water 3000 L at 75 C] --> M1
    M1 -->|Enzymatic Hydrolysis| OUT1[Liquid Wort 2800 L]
    M1 -->|Solid Liquid Separation| OUT2[Spent Grains 1100 kg at 80 percent moisture]
    OUT1 -->|Heat Input 100 C| M2[Boil Kettle]
    M2 -->|Hop Addition| M3[Hop Utilization 25 to 30 percent]
    M3 -->|Evaporation 8 percent| M4[Water Vapor and DMS]
    M3 -->|Output| OUT3[Hot Wort 2576 L at 12.5 Plato]
    OUT2 -->|Sale as Cattle Feed| EXT[By product Stream]
    OUT3 -->|Heat Recovery 30 percent| M5[Plate Heat Exchanger]
    M5 --> COLD[Cooled Wort at 20 C]
```

## 4.4 Quality Control Checkpoints (QC Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QC1[Incoming Malt QC1 Moisture DP Extract] --> QC2[Milling QC2 Particle Size Distribution]
    QC2 --> QC3[Mashing QC3 pH 5.2 to 5.6 Iodine Test]
    QC3 --> QC4[Boil QC4 Hop Utilization and Trub]
    QC4 --> QC5[Fermentation QC5 Gravity and Temperature and pH]
    QC5 --> QC6[Maturation QC6 Diacetyl and Chill Haze]
    QC6 --> QC7[Final QC7 ABV IBU Color Foam Stability]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "draw a flow diagram" in KTU exams, a Mermaid-rendered block diagram with clearly labeled unit operations, temperatures, and mass streams will fetch **full marks** under the structured answer key.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define beer. List any four major raw materials used in its production and state the function of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Beer** is an alcoholic beverage produced by the fermentation of a starchy substrate (malt-derived wort) using yeast, flavored with hops.
>
> | Raw Material | Function |
> |---|---|
> | Malted Barley | Provides amylases & fermentable sugars (maltose) |
> | Hops (*Humulus lupulus*) | Provides bitterness, aroma, and antimicrobial stability |
> | Yeast (*Saccharomyces* sp.) | Converts sugars to ethanol + CO₂ |
> | Water | Solvent, ~90% of final beer; mineral profile defines style |
> | Adjuncts (rice/maize) | Source of additional fermentable starch (lighter body) |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Ale and Lager fermentation in 4–5 points.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Ale | Lager |
> |---|---|---|
> | Yeast Strain | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | *Saccharomyces pastorianus* |
> | Fermentation Temp | 18–22 °C (warm) | 8–13 °C (cold) |
> | Fermentation Time | 5–7 days | 7–14 days |
> | Yeast Position | Top-cropping (floats) | Bottom-cropping (settles) |
> | Maturation | Short (1–2 weeks) at 15–20 °C | Long (2–8 weeks) at 0–4 °C (lagering) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **🟦 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **unit operations involved in the mashing and lautering stages** of beer production. Include a **labeled flow diagram** of the temperature rests. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A wort has Original Extract (OE) = 12.5 °P and Real Extract (RE) = 4.2 °P. Calculate (i) **Apparent Degree of Fermentation (ADF)**, (ii) **Real Degree of Fermentation (RDF)**, and (iii) **Real ABV (in % v/v)**. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 marks

**1. Mashing (3.5 marks):**
Mashing is the **enzymatic conversion of starch to fermentable sugars** in a mash tun.

- **Dough-in:** Crushed malt (grist) mixed with water at 35–50 °C, ratio 2.5:1 to 3.5:1 (L water : kg grist). Phytase lowers pH to 5.2.
- **Acid Rest (35–45 °C):** Phytase releases phosphate → pH drops.
- **Protein Rest (45–55 °C):** Proteases break proteins → Free Amino Nitrogen (FAN) for yeast nutrition.
- **Saccharification Rests:**
  - **β-amylase rest (60–65 °C):** Produces **maltose** (fermentable).
  - **α-amylase rest (70–75 °C):** Produces **dextrins** (non-fermentable, give body).
- **Mash-off (78 °C):** Enzyme denaturation → stops reaction.
- **Iodine test** confirms starch conversion (no blue color = complete).

**2. Lautering (3.5 marks):**
Lautering is the **solid–liquid separation** of sweet wort from spent grains.

- Mash transferred to **Lauter Tun** (false-bottom vessel).
- **Mash-out** at 78 °C to reduce viscosity and stop enzymes.
- **Vorlauf (recirculation):** First cloudy wort recirculated until bright (forms filter bed).
- **Sparging:** Hot water (76–78 °C) sprayed over grain bed to extract residual sugars.
- Governed by **Darcy's Law**: $Q = \frac{kA\Delta P}{\mu L}$.
- Run-off continues until wort gravity falls below 1.008.
- **Output:** Bright sweet wort (~12.5 °P) + Spent grains (sold as cattle feed).

> *Include the Mashing Temperature Program diagram (from Section 4.2) for full marks.*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Original Extract (OE) = 12.5 °P
- Real Extract (RE) = 4.2 °P
- Apparent Extract (AE) = OE − (real gravity drop approx 1.25 × apparent)
- Assume: Apparent Extract (AE) = 12.5 − (12.5 − 4.2) × 0.819 ≈ 12.5 − 6.80 = 5.70 °P (standard estimate)

> **Step (i) — ADF:**
> $$\text{ADF} = \frac{(\text{OE} - \text{AE})}{\text{OE}} \times 100 = \frac{(12.5 - 5.70)}{12.5} \times 100$$
> $$\boxed{\text{ADF} \approx 54.4\%}$$

> **Step (ii) — RDF:**
> $$\text{RDF} = \frac{(\text{OE} - \text{RE})}{\text{OE}} \times 100 = \frac{(12.5 - 4.2)}{12.5} \times 100$$
> $$\boxed{\text{RDF} = 66.4\%}$$

> **Step (iii) — Real ABV:**
> $$\text{ABV (real)} = \frac{(\text{OE} - \text{RE}) \times 0.819}{0.794}$$
> $$\text{ABV} = \frac{(12.5 - 4.2) \times 0.819}{0.794} = \frac{8.3 \times 0.819}{0.794} = \frac{6.798}{0.794}$$
> $$\boxed{\text{ABV (real)} \approx 8.56\% \, \text{v/v}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students confuse **apparent** and **real** extract. The factor **0.819** converts °P to SG; **0.794** is alcohol density. Writing RDF and ADF interchangeably will cost **2 marks**.
> - Always state units explicitly: °P, % v/v.
> - Drawing the mashing diagram (even a simple one) gains an **extra 1 mark** in part (a).

---

### **🟩 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** With a **neat flow diagram**, explain the **complete brewing process** from raw material to packaged beer, highlighting the **functions of each unit operation**. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Calculate the **theoretical and actual ethanol yield** from **1 ton of malt** containing **65% starch**, assuming **85% mashing efficiency** and **95% fermentation efficiency**. Molecular mass of glucose = 180 g/mol, ethanol = 46 g/mol. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 marks

> Refer to Section 3.1 (Eight Sequential Stages). The student must reproduce a flow diagram (Malt → Mill → Mash → Lauter → Boil → Cool → Ferment → Mature → Filter → Package) and label the **function of each step**:
>
> 1. **Milling** — Increases enzyme-substrate contact area.
> 2. **Mashing** — Starch → Maltose (saccharification).
> 3. **Lautering** — Separates wort from spent grains (filtration).
> 4. **Boiling + Hops** — Sterilization, bitterness, aroma, DMS removal, protein coagulation.
> 5. **Cooling (PHE)** — Brings wort to fermentation temperature; cold break removal.
> 6. **Fermentation** — Sugar → Ethanol + CO₂ (yeast metabolism).
> 7. **Maturation** — Flavor mellowing, diacetyl reduction, chill haze precipitation.
> 8. **Filtration + Carbonation + Packaging** — Clarification, CO₂ adjustment, sterile packaging.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Malt mass = 1,000 kg
- Starch in malt = 65% → 650 kg starch
- Mashing efficiency = 85% → Glucose produced from starch
- Fermentation efficiency = 95%

**Step 1 — Convert starch to glucose mass equivalent (hydrolysis):**
Hydrolysis reaction: $162 \text{ g starch} + 18 \text{ g H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 180 \text{ g glucose}$

$$\text{Glucose from 650 kg starch} = 650 \times \frac{180}{162} = 650 \times 1.111 = 722.2 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply mashing efficiency:**
$$\text{Glucose in wort} = 722.2 \times 0.85 = 613.9 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical ethanol yield (51.1% by Gay-Lussac):**
$$\text{Theoretical ethanol} = 613.9 \times 0.511 = 313.7 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Actual ethanol yield (95% fermentation efficiency):**
$$\text{Actual ethanol} = 313.7 \times 0.95 = 298.0 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Theoretical ethanol} \approx 313.7 \text{ kg, Actual} \approx 298.0 \text{ kg}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the 18 g water addition in starch hydrolysis** is the most common mistake. Use 180/162 conversion, not 1:1.
> - The factor **0.511** is theoretical; many students incorrectly use 0.48 (real yield) and skip the fermentation efficiency step.
> - Always present the final answer with **units (kg)** and mention assumptions (no yeast biomass loss accounted separately).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🍺 Beer Processing — Final 1-Page Revision**

- ✅ **Beer** = fermented cereal-based beverage, 3–8% ABV, flavored with hops.
- ✅ **8 Sequential Stages**: Milling → Mashing → Lautering → Boiling → Cooling → Fermentation → Maturation → Packaging.
- ✅ **Mash Rests**: Acid (35 °C) → Protein (50 °C) → β-amylase (65 °C) → α-amylase (72 °C) → Mash-off (78 °C).
- ✅ **Core Reaction**: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂ (Gay-Lussac, 51.1% theoretical ethanol yield).
- ✅ **Two fermentations**: Ale (warm, *S. cerevisiae*) vs Lager (cold, *S. pastorianus*).
- ✅ **Hop function**: Bittering (early boil) + Aroma (late boil/dry hop) + Antimicrobial.
- ✅ **Diacetyl** (butterscotch off-flavor) must be reduced by yeast reductase during maturation.
- ✅ **Lautering** = deep-bed filtration (Darcy's Law).
- ✅ **Cooling** uses Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) — 100 °C → 20 °C in <30 min.
- ✅ **CO₂ captured** from fermentation is re-used for carbonation (sustainability).
- ✅ **ABV formula**: (OG − FG) × 131.25; **Plato → SG**; **RDF = (OE−RE)/OE × 100**.
- ✅ **Diastatic Power (Lintner °L)** = measure of malt enzyme strength.
- ✅ **Reinheitsgebot (1516)**: Only water, malt, hops (and yeast, added later) — German purity law.
- ✅ **Adjuncts** (rice, maize, sorghum) lighten body and reduce cost.
- ✅ **Mashing water:grist ratio** = 2.5–3.5 L/kg.
- ✅ **Final CO₂ content** = 2.5–3.0 volumes (one volume = 1 L CO₂/L beer at STP).
- ✅ **Pasteurization units (PU)**: 15–20 for tunnel pasteurizer (in-bottle), 30+ for flash.

> **End of Module 4 — Alcoholic Beverages: Processing of Beer** 🍻
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
