# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the term "Arrack". Mention its raw material of origin in the Kerala context and the standard alcohol strength prescribed by the Kerala State Beverages Corporation (KSBC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Arrack is a traditional distilled alcoholic beverage of South and Southeast Asia, produced by fermenting the sugar-rich sap of plants followed by pot-still distillation. In the Kerala context, the raw material is **neera**, the unfermented sap collected from the unopened inflorescence (spathe) of the coconut palm (*Cocos nucifera*). The sap is naturally fermented to toddy (4–8% v/v alcohol) and then distilled. The standard ABV prescribed by the KSBC (Kerala State Beverages Corporation) for commercial arrack is **42.8% v/v**. (3 marks)

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Write the Gay-Lussac equation for alcoholic fermentation and state the theoretical yield of ethanol from 1 kg of glucose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Gay-Lussac equation for alcoholic fermentation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (Yeast)}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

The theoretical (stoichiometric) yield of ethanol is **0.5111 g of ethanol per gram of glucose** fermented, i.e., from 1 kg of glucose, **511.1 g of pure ethanol** can be theoretically obtained. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **traditional method of production of arrack in Kerala** starting from coconut palm tapping. Indicate the typical alcohol strength at each major stage.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **biochemistry of alcoholic fermentation** with emphasis on the role of yeast enzymes, by-products formed, and the factors affecting the fermentation efficiency of neera toddy.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The traditional production of arrack in Kerala is a **three-stage process** — sap collection, fermentation, and pot-still distillation.

**Stage 1 — Sap Collection (Tapping):**
- Mature coconut palms aged 15–60 years are selected.
- The unopened inflorescence (spathe) is gently tapped daily for 2–3 weeks to induce sap flow — a process called *panaikkal* in Malayalam.
- A clay pot or plastic vessel is hung below the cut spathe; **fresh neera** (~1.5–3.0 L per palm per day) is collected twice daily.

**Stage 2 — Fermentation:**
- The neera is strained through muslin and transferred to wooden vats (*manna*).
- **Spontaneous fermentation** by wild yeasts (*S. cerevisiae*, *Schizosaccharomyces pombe*, *Candida* spp.) proceeds for **24–48 hours** at ambient temperature (28–35 °C).
- The product, called **toddy (kallu)**, has a **4–8% v/v** alcohol content and a sour, effervescent character.

**Stage 3 — Distillation:**
- Toddy is charged into a **copper pot still (bhatti)** and heated over a wood or LPG furnace.
- Ethanol-rich vapour (boiling at 78.4 °C) rises, passes through a copper coil condenser, and is collected.
- The first distillate (**25–35% ABV**) undergoes a **second pot-still pass** for rectification.
- Heads (5% discard — methanol, aldehydes), Hearts (80% — arrack core, ~80% ABV), and Tails (15% — fusel oils, recycled) are separated.
- Hearts are diluted with demineralized water to **42.8% v/v** and bottled under KSBC excise marking.

**Typical ABV at each stage:**

| Stage | Product | ABV (% v/v) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fresh sap | Neera | 0 (no alcohol) |
| After 24–48 h | Toddy | 4 – 8 |
| First distillation | Crude spirit | 25 – 35 |
| Second distillation | Arrack hearts | 70 – 85 |
| Diluted, bottled | Commercial arrack | **42.8** |

(7 marks — 3 marks for stages, 2 marks for flow logic, 2 marks for ABV data)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Biochemistry of Alcoholic Fermentation:**

The conversion of glucose in neera toddy to ethanol is mediated by the enzyme system of yeast, collectively called **zymase**, which operates through the **Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) glycolytic pathway**.

**Step 1 — Glycolysis:** Glucose is phosphorylated and split into two molecules of pyruvate, generating 2 ATP and 2 NADH.

**Step 2 — Decarboxylation:** Pyruvate decarboxylase removes CO₂ from pyruvate, yielding **acetaldehyde**.

**Step 3 — Reduction:** Alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) transfers hydride from NADH to acetaldehyde, producing **ethanol** and regenerating NAD⁺.

**Net Reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2\,ADP + 2\,Pi \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + 2\,ATP
\end{aligned}
$$

**By-products formed:**

| By-product | Pathway | Sensory Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Glycerol** | Alternative to acetaldehyde reduction | Sweetness, body |
| **Higher alcohols (fusel oils)** — isoamyl, isobutyl, n-propyl | Ehrlich pathway from amino acids | Heady aroma |
| **Esters** — ethyl acetate, isoamyl acetate | Acetyl-CoA + alcohol | Fruity notes |
| **Aldehydes** — acetaldehyde | Pyruvate decarboxylation | Pungent, grassy |
| **Acetic acid** | Bacterial contamination (Acetobacter) | Sour off-note |
| **Diacetyl** | Spontaneous decarboxylation of α-acetolactate | Buttery off-note |

**Factors affecting fermentation efficiency of neera toddy:**

1. **Sugar concentration:** 12–18 °Brix is optimum; lower = poor alcohol yield, higher = osmotic stress on yeast.
2. **Temperature:** 28–35 °C optimum for *S. cerevisiae*; > 40 °C kills yeast.
3. **pH:** Must drop to 3.8–4.2; too high → bacterial contamination.
4. **Yeast strain and inoculum:** Wild vs inoculated cultures — inoculated gives 10–15% higher yield.
5. **Nutrient availability:** Nitrogen (amino acids), vitamins (biotin, thiamine) affect yeast growth.
6. **Oxygen:** Brief aeration at start helps biomass build-up; anaerobic thereafter.
7. **Antimicrobials:** Contamination by *Acetobacter* and wild moulds is the single largest cause of batch failure.

(7 marks — 2 for EMP pathway, 2 for by-products, 3 for factors)

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **principle of pot-still distillation** as applied to arrack production. Explain the cut points (heads, hearts, tails) and their significance with respect to product quality and safety.

**(b) [7 marks]** A small arrack unit ferments **3000 L of neera** at **15 °Brix** with **92% sugar conversion** and distils with **78% recovery efficiency**. Calculate (i) the total pure ethanol produced (in kg), and (ii) the volume of **42.8% v/v commercial arrack** that can be bottled.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle of Pot-Still Distillation:**

A pot still is a **batch distillation unit** consisting of three parts: a heated kettle (boiler), a vapour riser (swan neck), and a condenser (worm tub). The principle rests on the **differential volatility** of ethanol (boiling point 78.37 °C) and water (100 °C). When fermented toddy is heated, ethanol evaporates preferentially, rises as vapour, and is condensed back to liquid — yielding a higher-ABV spirit.

**The Cut Points:**

The distillation profile of fermented toddy is divided into three fractions:

**1. Heads / Foreshots (5% of distillate):**
- First fraction to come over; rich in **low-boiling, toxic compounds**: methanol, acetone, ethyl acetate, acetaldehyde.
- Must be **discarded** to ensure consumer safety.
- Recognized by pungent, "nail-polish" aroma.

**2. Hearts (80% of distillate):**
- The middle, clean fraction — the consumable arrack.
- Rich in **ethanol** with balanced esters and higher alcohols (which give arrack its characteristic flavour).
- This fraction is diluted to 42.8% v/v for bottling.

**3. Tails / Feints (15% of distillate):**
- Last fraction; rich in **fusel oils** (isoamyl alcohol, n-propanol), water, and organic acids.
- Higher boiling, hence comes over last.
- **Recycled** into the next batch to recover residual ethanol.

**Significance:**
- **Quality:** Cut ratios determine the smoothness, aroma, and mouthfeel of the final arrack. Over-cutting hearts (allowing heads to remain) gives harsh, headache-inducing spirit.
- **Safety:** Methanol content in the hearts must be < **50 g/hL of pure alcohol** as per FSSAI regulations; careful head-cut achieves this.
- **Economy:** Optimizing heart yield (maximizing the 80% fraction) directly improves plant profitability.

(7 marks — 2 for principle, 3 for cut-point logic, 2 for quality/safety significance)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given data:**
- $V_{neera} = 3000 \text{ L}$
- Brix = 15 °Bx → sugar mass fraction = 0.15
- Density of neera $\rho_{neera} \approx 1.06 \text{ g/mL} = 1060 \text{ g/L}$
- Sugar conversion efficiency $X = 0.92$
- Distillation recovery efficiency $\eta_{dist} = 0.78$
- $\rho_{EtOH} = 0.789 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 789 \text{ g/L}$
- Theoretical yield of ethanol from glucose = 0.5111 g/g

**Step 1: Sugar mass in neera**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{sugar} &= 0.15 \times 1060 = 159.0 \text{ g/L} \\
m_{sugar} &= 159.0 \times 3000 = 4.77 \times 10^5 \text{ g} = 477.0 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Pure ethanol after fermentation (theoretical × conversion)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{EtOH,fermented} &= 0.5111 \times 0.92 \times 477.0 \\
m_{EtOH,fermented} &= 224.30 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Pure ethanol after distillation recovery**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{EtOH,recovered} &= 0.78 \times 224.30 \\
m_{EtOH,recovered} &= 174.95 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **(i) Final Answer:** The total pure ethanol produced is **≈ 174.95 kg (≈ 175 kg).**

**Step 4: Volume of pure ethanol**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{EtOH,pure} &= \dfrac{174.95 \text{ kg}}{0.789 \text{ kg/L}} = 221.74 \text{ L of pure ethanol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Volume of 42.8% v/v arrack**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{arrack} &= \dfrac{221.74}{0.428} = 518.1 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **(ii) Final Answer:** The unit can bottle **≈ 518.1 litres of 42.8% v/v commercial arrack** from a 3000 L neera batch.

(7 marks — 2 for sugar/ethanol mass balance, 2 for distillation recovery, 2 for dilution calculation, 1 for unit consistency)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the CO₂ term** in the Gay-Lussac equation → −0.5 mark
> 2. **Not specifying Brix-to-mass conversion** when given in °Brix → −1 mark
> 3. **Confusing % w/w and % v/v** in alcohol strength calculations → −1 mark
> 4. **Skipping the dilution step** to commercial 42.8% v/v in production yield problems → −1 mark
> 5. **Writing "yeast" instead of specific species** (e.g., *S. cerevisiae*) → −0.5 mark
> 6. **Ignoring the heads-cut safety reasoning** in distillation questions → −1 mark
> 7. **Not using Antoine/Raoult's Law** when asked about distillation principle → −0.5 mark
> 8. **Missing wild vs inoculated culture distinction** — KTU 2024 specifically tests this → −1 mark

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Arrack (Module 4)**

- ✅ Arrack = **distilled spirit** from fermented **plant sap** (mainly coconut toddy in Kerala).
- ✅ Kerala Arrack's raw material = **Coconut inflorescence sap (neera)** from *Cocos nucifera*.
- ✅ Commercial strength = **42.8% v/v** (KSBC / Indian Excise standard).
- ✅ Fermentation is **spontaneous (wild yeast)**; species: *S. cerevisiae*, *S. pombe*, *Candida* spp.
- ✅ Gay-Lussac equation: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$; theoretical yield = **0.5111 g/g**.
- ✅ Distillation is **pot-still based**; exploits **ethanol's lower boiling point (78.4 °C)**.
- ✅ Three distillation cuts: **Heads (5%, discard), Hearts (80%, arrack), Tails (15%, recycle)**.
- ✅ Ethanol-water azeotrope at **95.6% v/v, 78.2 °C** is the upper limit of simple distillation.
- ✅ By-products: **glycerol, fusel oils, esters, aldehydes, organic acids**.
- ✅ Critical factors: Brix (12–18), temperature (28–35 °C), pH (3.8–4.2), yeast strain, hygiene.
- ✅ FSSAI methanol limit: **< 50 g/hL of pure alcohol** in finished arrack.
- ✅ KTU 2024 emphasizes: **(i) Kerala-specific context, (ii) mass-balance numerics, (iii) biochemistry of fermentation, (iv) cut-point safety logic.**
- ✅ Arrack differs from rum/whisky in: **no aging**, **no grain substrate**, **single pot-still tradition**.
- ✅ Modern industrial trend: **mixed-culture inoculation + continuous column distillation + heat integration.**
- ✅ Energy demand dominated by $\Delta H_{vap}^{EtOH} = 38.56 \text{ kJ/mol}$ — key driver of distillation economics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vodka
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vodka

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Vodka** is a clear, colorless, **neutral spirit** (also termed *rectified spirit* or *potable alcohol*) produced by the **fermentation** of starch- or sugar-based agricultural raw materials (cereals, potatoes, molasses, fruits, or other agricultural produce) followed by **multiple distillations** and **filtration** (commonly through **activated charcoal**) to yield a high-purity ethanol-water azeotrope. Commercially, vodka is bottled at an **alcoholic strength by volume (ABV) of 37.5 % to 50 %** (most commonly **40 % ABV**), with the balance being primarily demineralized water and trace *congener* compounds.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, vodka falls under the broad category of **high-purity distilled beverages** (Module 4 — *Beverages: Distilled Spirits*) where the central technical goal is the **maximization of ethanol purity** and the **minimization of congener content** (fusel alcohols, aldehydes, esters, methanol, and acids) that impart flavor to aged spirits like whisky, rum, or brandy.

### 1.2 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition / Significance |
|---|---|
| **ABV (Alcohol By Volume)** | Volume of pure ethanol per 100 mL of beverage at 20 °C |
| **Proof** | 2 × ABV (US system); e.g., 80 proof = 40 % ABV |
| **Rectified Spirit** | Spirit purified to ≥ 95 % ABV through fractional distillation |
| **Ethanol-Water Azeotrope** | Constant-boiling mixture at 95.6 % ABV, 78.2 °C |
| **Congeners** | Minor flavor compounds: fusel oils, aldehydes, esters |
| **Potable Water** | Water fit for human consumption, demineralized for vodka cut |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of vodka as the "white T-shirt of the spirits world."**
> Just as a plain white T-shirt is the *neutral base* onto which any design can be printed, vodka is a *clean, near-flavourless* ethanol-water canvas. Where whisky, rum, and brandy proudly *showcase* their raw material (grain, sugarcane, grape) by retaining congeners, vodka deliberately **strips** them away. The genius of vodka production is therefore *not* flavour creation, but **purification engineering** — repeated distillation, charcoal polishing, and ultra-pure water blending.

### 1.4 Historical & Geographical Context (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Origin:** Documented in **Poland (1405)** and **Russia (1430s)** as *gorzałka* and *khlebnoe vino* respectively.
> - **Etymology:** From the Slavic *voda* meaning **"little water"** — reflecting its high water content and clean character.
> - **UNESCO Recognition:** Polish and Russian vodka traditions inscribed on the **UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage** list (2023).
> - **Largest Producers (2024):** Russia, Poland, USA, Sweden, France.

### 1.5 Classification of Vodka

| Class | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Grain Vodka** | From wheat, rye, corn, barley; smooth, neutral | Smirnoff, Absolut |
| **Potato Vodka** | From potatoes; fuller body, slight earthiness | Chopin, Luksusowa |
| **Flavored Vodka** | Infused with fruits, herbs, spices | Stolichnaya Ohranj, Absolut Citron |
| **Premium / Craft Vodka** | Multiple distillations, single-origin, filtered through precious metals | Belvedere, Grey Goose, Beluga |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Production Process — Structured Logical Flow

Vodka manufacture proceeds in **five distinct unit operations**, each governed by rigorous mass- and energy-balance principles.

#### **Step 1 — Raw Material Preparation & Mashing**

- Cereal grains (wheat, rye, maize) are **milled** to expose starch granules.
- The flour is mixed with water at **65–70 °C** in a *mash tun*, activating native enzymes (amylases) that hydrolyse starch to fermentable sugars via:
  - **Liquefaction:** Starch → Maltodextrins
  - **Saccharification:** Maltodextrins → Maltose + Glucose
- **Potato-based** vodkas require **cooking under pressure** (1.5–3.0 bar, 120–150 °C) to gelatinize the highly resistant potato starch.
- The resulting *wort* is cooled to **20–25 °C** before yeast pitching.

#### **Step 2 — Fermentation**

- Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) is added at **0.5–1.0 % w/v**.
- Fermentation is typically **batch** over **48–96 hours**, producing a *beer* or *wash* of **7–12 % ABV**.
- The governing biochemical equation:

$$
\ce{C6H12O6 ->[Yeast] 2 C2H5OH + 2 CO2 ^}
$$

- Real industrial yield ≈ **90–95 %** of the **theoretical** 51.1 % w/w (see derivation in Section 3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not ferment to higher ABV?** Yeast is inhibited and ultimately killed at **>15 % ABV** due to membrane disruption and protein denaturation. Hence the 7–12 % ceiling in a *single* fermentation.

#### **Step 3 — Distillation (Multi-Stage, Continuous)**

- The fermented wash passes through **multi-column continuous distillation** (typically a **3- or 4-column system**):
  1. **Beer Column / Stripper** — strips alcohol from the wash (produces *spent wash* with < 0.05 % alcohol).
  2. **Rectifying Column** — concentrates ethanol toward the **95.6 % ABV azeotrope**.
  3. **Finishing / Hydro-Selection Column** — further refines to ≥ **96.0–96.5 % ABV** (super-azeotropic "neutral grain spirit").
- Many premium brands use **pot-still batch distillation** in copper (e.g., Belvedere uses 4 pot distillations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Copper's role:** Copper acts as a *catalyst* that binds sulfur compounds (dimethyl sulfide, hydrogen sulfide), removing off-aromas — a phenomenon known as **"copper catalyzation."**

#### **Step 4 — Filtration & Polishing**

- The rectified spirit is filtered through:
  - **Activated charcoal** (most common): adsorbs residual fusel oils, aldehydes, and esters.
  - **Quartz sand / membrane filters**: removes particulates.
  - **Precious metals** (silver, platinum — premium brands): further chemical polishing.
- **Russian "GOST" standard** (historically) required filtration through **birch/alder charcoal** and **quartz sand** — a *national* filtration protocol.

#### **Step 5 — Dilution (Cutting) & Bottling**

- The high-proof spirit is diluted to **40 % ABV** with **demineralized, deionized, and UV-sterilized water**.
- The water must have very low mineral content (< 50 ppm TDS) to preserve the spirit's *clean* mouthfeel.
- After dilution, the vodka is **resting-stabilized** for 24–72 hours to allow molecular equilibrium, then **cold-filtered (0–4 °C)** to remove any fatty-acid cloudiness, and bottled.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory equations — memorize these for ESE and module tests.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ | $C$ = concentration (ABV), $V$ = volume | L, % | Dilution (cutting) of high-proof spirit to bottling strength |
| 2 | $m_{\text{EtOH,theor}} = 0.511 \cdot m_{\text{glucose}}$ | Theoretical ethanol from glucose | kg, kg | Maximum possible ethanol from sugar |
| 3 | $Y_{\text{EtOH}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{EtOH,actual}}}{m_{\text{glucose}}} \times 100 \,\%$ | Fermentation yield | % | Industrial efficiency (~90–95 %) |
| 4 | $V_{\text{EtOH}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{EtOH}}}{\rho_{\text{EtOH}}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{EtOH}}}{0.789}$ | Ethanol volume from mass | L | Bottle-strength calculations |
| 5 | $m_{\text{CO2}} = 0.489 \cdot m_{\text{glucose}}$ | CO2 produced during fermentation | kg | Carbon-dioxide balance & greenhouse accounting |
| 6 | $n_{\text{EtOH,ferment}} \le \dfrac{m_{\text{sugar}} \cdot Y \cdot 1000}{180 \cdot 0.789}$ | Moles of ethanol from sugar | mol | Stoichiometric control |
| 7 | $\text{ABV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EtOH}}}{V_{\text{solution}}} \times 100$ | Alcohol by volume | % | Final bottling specification |
| 8 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat to warm mash / wort | kJ | Energy balance for mashing |
| 9 | $\rho_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{EtOH}} \cdot V_{\text{EtOH}} + \rho_{\text{H2O}} \cdot V_{\text{H2O}}}{V_{\text{total}}}$ | Density of ethanol-water mix | g/mL | Used in hydrometry and ABV determination |
| 10 | $\text{Strecker yield} = \dfrac{\text{ABV}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{ABV}_{\text{original wash}}} \times 100$ | Concentration ratio in distillation | % | Distillation column performance |

> **Syllabus Tip:** The ethanol-water **azeotrope at 95.6 % w/w (89.4 mol %)** boiling at **78.2 °C** is the *thermodynamic ceiling* of simple distillation — students must know this for any KTU question on rectification.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Beyond the beverage industry, the vodka production line is a **miniature model** of:
> - **Pharmaceutical ethanol purification** (USP-grade ethanol).
> - **Bioethanol / E-100 fuel** manufacture (same fermentation + distillation train).
> - **Hand-sanitizer production** (where the *post-COVID surge* in vodka-distillery conversion is a famous case study).
> - **CO2 capture** from fermentation (pure food-grade CO2 byproduct).

The same unit operations — mashing, fermentation, rectification, adsorption filtration — are taught in chemical engineering mass-transfer courses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

Starting from the fermentation reaction:

$$
\ce{C6H12O6 -> 2 C2H5OH + 2 CO2 ^}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute molar masses.**

| Species | Molecular formula | Molar mass (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| Glucose | C6H12O6 | 180.16 |
| Ethanol | C2H5OH | 46.07 |
| Carbon dioxide | CO2 | 44.01 |

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric mass ratio (per 180 g glucose).**

$$
m_{\text{EtOH, theor}} = 2 \times 46.07 \; \text{g} = 92.14 \; \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Yield factor (mass of ethanol per mass of glucose).**

$$
Y_{\text{theor}} = \dfrac{92.14}{180.16} = 0.5114
$$

Therefore, **theoretically, 1 kg of glucose yields 0.5114 kg of pure ethanol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **51.1 % rule** is the KTU-favorite number. Commit it to memory.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Industrial (Actual) Ethanol Yield

**Step 1 — Real industrial fermentation** is constrained by:
- Yeast biomass synthesis (≈ 2 % of sugar consumed).
- Glycerol & organic acid by-products (≈ 2–3 %).
- Incomplete sugar conversion (residual sugar).
- CO2 entrainment losses.

The combined industrial efficiency is **η ≈ 0.90 to 0.95**.

**Step 2 — Actual yield calculation:**

$$
Y_{\text{actual}} = \eta \times Y_{\text{theor}} = 0.92 \times 0.5114 = 0.4705
$$

**Step 3 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{EtOH, actual}} = 0.4705 \times m_{\text{glucose}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** A 10,000 L wheat mash containing **150 g/L of fermentable sugars** (after mashing) is fermented.

- Total sugar = $10{,}000 \times 150 = 1{,}500{,}000 \; \text{g} = 1500 \; \text{kg}$.
- Theoretical ethanol = $1500 \times 0.5114 = 767.1 \; \text{kg}$.
- Actual ethanol (η = 0.92) = $767.1 \times 0.92 = 705.7 \; \text{kg}$.

Volume of pure ethanol at ρ = 0.789 g/mL:

$$
V_{\text{EtOH}} = \dfrac{705{,}700 \; \text{g}}{0.789 \; \text{g/mL}} = 894{,}424 \; \text{mL} = 894.4 \; \text{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Fermentation wash ABV:**

Wash volume ≈ 10,000 L (negligible volume change). Assuming post-distillation wash density ≈ 1.00 g/mL:

$$
\text{ABV}_{\text{wash}} = \dfrac{894.4}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 8.94 \,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **8–10 % ABV wash** is the typical industry output — a key data point for KTU problems.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dilution (Cutting) Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A distillery produces **1500 L of 96.0 % ABV** rectified spirit. How much **demineralized water** must be added to bring the final vodka to **40.0 % ABV**?

**Step 1 — Use the conservation equation:**

$$
C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute knowns:**

$$
96.0 \times 1500 = 40.0 \times V_2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for final volume:**

$$
V_2 = \dfrac{96.0 \times 1500}{40.0} = 3600 \; \text{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute water added:**

$$
V_{\text{water}} = V_2 - V_1 = 3600 - 1500 = 2100 \; \text{L}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Students forget that when water is mixed with ethanol, there is a small **volume contraction** (≈ 4 % for the 40 % mixture). In strict *mass* balance, we use **mass fractions**, not volume fractions. For board-exam precision, apply the **mass-balance** form:

**Step 5 — Rigorous mass-balance check:**

- Mass of pure ethanol = $1500 \times 0.96 \times 0.96^* \approx 1380 \; \text{kg}$ (using ρ_96% ≈ 0.96 g/mL at 20 °C).
- For exactness at 40 % ABV, ρ_40% ≈ 0.9481 g/mL.

$$
V_{\text{final}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{EtOH}}}{\rho_{\text{EtOH}} \times \text{ABV}/100} = \dfrac{1380}{0.789 \times 0.40} = 4372.5 \; \text{L}
$$

This discrepancy (3600 vs. 4372) highlights the importance of mass-based methods in *precise* beverage engineering.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Required for Mashing

**Step 1 — Wheat mash heating:**

Given:
- $m_{\text{water}} = 8000 \; \text{kg}$
- $m_{\text{grain}} = 2000 \; \text{kg}$ (specific heat $c_{p,\text{grain}} = 1.50 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$)
- $T_{\text{initial}} = 20 \; \text{°C}$, $T_{\text{final}} = 65 \; \text{°C}$
- $\Delta T = 45 \; \text{K}$
- $c_{p,\text{water}} = 4.18 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$

**Step 2 — Total heat:**

$$
Q = m_{\text{water}} c_{p,\text{water}} \Delta T + m_{\text{grain}} c_{p,\text{grain}} \Delta T
$$

$$
Q = (8000 \times 4.18 \times 45) + (2000 \times 1.50 \times 45)
$$

$$
Q = 1{,}504{,}800 + 135{,}000 = 1{,}639{,}800 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh (for utility billing):**

$$
Q = \dfrac{1{,}639{,}800}{3600} = 455.5 \; \text{kWh}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Distillation Performance

| Parameter | Pot Still (1×) | Pot Still (4×) | 3-Column Continuous | 4-Column Continuous |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABV after distillation (%) | 60–70 | 85–90 | 95.0–95.6 | 96.0–96.5 |
| Congener content (ppm) | 1500 | 300 | 80 | 20 |
| Energy (kJ/L alcohol) | 8500 | 12,000 | 5200 | 5800 |
| Capital cost (relative) | 1× | 1.2× | 3× | 4× |
| Vodka grade produced | Mid-range | Premium | Premium/Super | Super-premium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love *comparative tables* — the column-still **energy** is *lower* than pot-still because the **reboiler heat is recycled** between columns.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vodka Production — Master Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material: Grain / Potato / Molasses] --> B[Cleaning and Milling]
    B --> C[Cooking and Mashing at 65 to 70 °C]
    C --> D[Saccharification by Amylase Enzymes]
    D --> E[Cooling of Wort to 20 to 25 °C]
    E --> F[Yeast Pitching: Saccharomyces cerevisiae]
    F --> G[Fermentation: 48 to 96 hours]
    G --> H[Fermented Wash: 8 to 10 % ABV]
    H --> I[Beer Column Stripping]
    I --> J[Rectifying Column 1]
    J --> K[Rectifying Column 2]
    K --> L[Hydro Selection Column]
    L --> M[Rectified Spirit: 96 % ABV]
    M --> N[Activated Charcoal Filtration]
    N --> O[Quartz Sand and Membrane Polishing]
    O --> P[Dilution with Demineralized Water]
    P --> Q[Resting and Cold Stabilization: 0 to 4 °C]
    Q --> R[Final Quality Control: ABV, GC, Organoleptic]
    R --> S[Bottling and Labelling]
    S --> T[Finished Vodka: 40 % ABV]

    style A fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    style M fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style T fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

    subgraph FINISH_ZONE [FINISHING AND PACKAGING ZONE]

```mermaid

    AIR[Sterile Air 0.2 vvm] --> MIX

    MIX --> OUT1[Beer Output: 8 to 10 percent ABV]
    MIX --> OUT2[CO2 Vent: Food Grade]
    MIX --> OUT3[Yeast Cream Recycle: 5 percent]

    style MIX fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    style OUT1 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    style OUT2 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level interpretation:** This diagram abstracts the *physical* geometry of fermentation/distillation into a **functional process map** (a common KTU 2024 exam requirement when physical schematics are not feasible on a written answer sheet).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define vodka. What is the typical ABV range for commercial vodka, and what is the significance of the ethanol-water azeotrope in vodka distillation?

> **Model Answer:**
> *Vodka is a clear, colorless, neutral spirit distilled from fermented grain, potato, or other agricultural substrates. Commercial vodka is bottled at 37.5–50 % ABV (most commonly 40 %). The ethanol-water azeotrope at 95.6 % ABV (78.2 °C) is the thermodynamic ceiling of simple distillation — beyond this purity, simple distillation cannot concentrate ethanol. Multi-column continuous distillation is used to approach or marginally exceed this ceiling via hydro-selection. The azeotrope's existence is why vodka achieves its characteristic high-purity, low-congener profile.* **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** List any three raw materials used for vodka production. Why is activated charcoal filtration a critical step in vodka manufacture?

> **Model Answer:**
> *Common raw materials: (1) Wheat (smooth, neutral), (2) Rye (spicy undertone), (3) Potato (full-bodied, slightly earthy), (4) Molasses, (5) Corn, (6) Fruits/apples (less common).*
> *Activated charcoal filtration adsorbs residual **fusel alcohols** (propanol, isobutanol, amyl alcohols), **aldehydes** (acetaldehyde), **esters**, and trace sulfur compounds that survived distillation. This is critical because vodka's signature is *flavour neutrality* — any residual congener becomes an off-note. Premium brands may use 5–10 successive charcoal passes.* **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester, May 2024]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either A or B.*

**(a)** Describe in detail the **raw material preparation, mashing, and fermentation** stages of vodka manufacture from wheat. Include the key biochemical reaction, typical process conditions, and the practical yield. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (a):**
> 1. **Raw Material Selection (1 mark):** Hard winter wheat with 12–14 % protein and 65–70 % starch is selected. Wheat provides smoother, more neutral spirit than rye.
> 2. **Milling (1 mark):** Grains are hammer-milled to a fine grist to rupture the endosperm and expose starch granules to enzymatic action.
> 3. **Mashing (2 marks):** The grist is mixed with water at a 1:3 to 1:4 ratio and heated in a mash tun to **65–70 °C** for 60–90 min. Native *β-amylase* and added exogenous *α-amylase* hydrolyse starch → maltose + dextrins (saccharification). For adjuncts, **jet-cooking at 120–150 °C** may be used.
> 4. **Wort Cooling (0.5 mark):** The sweet wort is cooled via plate heat exchanger to **20–25 °C** to be yeast-friendly.
> 5. **Fermentation Reaction (1 mark):**
>    $$\ce{C6H12O6 ->[S. cerevisiae] 2 C2H5OH + 2 CO2 ^}$$
> 6. **Process Conditions (1 mark):** Batch fermentation for 48–96 h with *S. cerevisiae* pitched at 0.5–1 % w/v, pH 4.5–5.0, temperature controlled at 25–30 °C. Wash reaches 8–10 % ABV.
> 7. **Practical Yield (0.5 mark):** Industrial yield = 0.92 × 0.5114 = **0.47 kg ethanol per kg glucose** (~92 % of theoretical).

**(b)** Explain the **multi-column continuous distillation** system used in modern vodka production. Discuss the role of **copper** and **activated charcoal** in achieving vodka's characteristic purity. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (b):**
> 1. **Beer/Separator Column (1.5 marks):** Fermented wash (8–10 % ABV) is fed at the top. Steam at the base strips alcohol. The bottoms (spent wash) exit with < 0.05 % ABV and are sent to effluent treatment or sold as liquid fertilizer.
> 2. **Rectifying Column (1.5 marks):** Vapours from the beer column enter. Through repeated condensation-revaporization, the **ethanol-water azeotrope (95.6 %)** is approached at the top.
> 3. **Hydro-Selection / Finishing Column (1 mark):** Water is injected at a specific tray to *reject* water molecules back, taking the spirit to 96.0–96.5 % ABV — the "neutral grain spirit" (NGS) sold to many vodka brands.
> 4. **Role of Copper (1.5 marks):** Copper in pot stills and column packing catalytically binds **sulfur compounds** (H2S, DMS) and forms insoluble copper-sulfides that are removed. It also reacts with fatty acids to form **copper soaps**, removing *soapy* off-notes. This is the basis for "copper-catalysed maturation."
> 5. **Activated Charcoal Filtration (1 mark):** Polish-stage filtration through food-grade activated carbon (often from coconut shell or hardwood) adsorbs residual congeners — fusel oils, aldehydes, esters, methanol — driving them to single-digit ppm levels.
> 6. **Premium Examples (0.5 mark):** Belvedere (Poland) uses four pot distillations through copper; Ketel One uses charcoal from Northern European birch; Grey Goose uses single-column copper pot + limestone filtration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** When asked about distillation, students often confuse **simple distillation** (used for essential oils) with **fractional/rectifying distillation** (used for vodka). Vodka *requires* fractional distillation to overcome the **95.6 % azeotrope**. Similarly, do not write "potato vodka" when the question is specifically about *grain* vodka.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice.*

**(a)** A distillery ferments **5000 L of wort** containing **160 g/L of fermentable sugar**. Calculate: (i) the **mass of ethanol** produced theoretically, (ii) the **actual mass** at 90 % fermentation efficiency, and (iii) the **ABV of the resulting wash**. Given: density of ethanol = 0.789 g/mL. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (a):**
> **Step 1 — Total sugar mass (1 mark):**
> $$m_{\text{sugar}} = 5000 \; \text{L} \times 160 \; \text{g/L} = 800{,}000 \; \text{g} = 800 \; \text{kg}$$
> **Step 2 — Theoretical ethanol mass (2 marks):**
> $$m_{\text{EtOH, theor}} = 0.5114 \times 800 = 409.12 \; \text{kg}$$
> **Step 3 — Actual ethanol mass (2 marks):**
> $$m_{\text{EtOH, actual}} = 0.90 \times 409.12 = 368.21 \; \text{kg}$$
> **Step 4 — Ethanol volume (1 mark):**
> $$V_{\text{EtOH}} = \dfrac{368{,}210}{0.789} = 466{,}679 \; \text{mL} = 466.68 \; \text{L}$$
> **Step 5 — Wash ABV (1 mark):**
> $$\text{ABV} = \dfrac{466.68}{5000} \times 100 = 9.33 \,\%$$
> **Final answer: 409.12 kg theoretical, 368.21 kg actual, 9.33 % ABV wash.** ✓

**(b)** After distillation, the rectified spirit is at **95.5 % ABV**. If the bottling target is **42.5 % ABV** and **3000 L of 95.5 % spirit** is available, calculate: (i) the **total final volume** of vodka, and (ii) the **volume of demineralized water** to add, **ignoring volume contraction**. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (b):**
> **Step 1 — Pure ethanol volume (1.5 marks):**
> $$V_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.955 \times 3000 = 2865 \; \text{L}$$
> **Step 2 — Apply C₁V₁ = C₂V₂ (2.5 marks):**
> $$95.5 \times 3000 = 42.5 \times V_2$$
> $$V_2 = \dfrac{286{,}500}{42.5} = 6741.18 \; \text{L}$$
> **Step 3 — Water added (1.5 marks):**
> $$V_{\text{water}} = 6741.18 - 3000 = 3741.18 \; \text{L}$$
> **Step 4 — Verification (1.5 marks):**
> Check: 6741.18 × 0.425 = 2865 L of ethanol ✓
> **Final answer: V₂ = 6741.18 L, V_water = 3741.18 L** (excluding volume contraction of ~4 %).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall #1:** Mark loss if you write `V₂ = 2865 / 0.425` without the prior `C₁V₁` form. Always state the *conservation principle* before solving.
> **Examiner Pitfall #2:** In a *strict* mass balance, water must be added by **mass**, not volume, because ethanol-water mixtures exhibit **negative volume deviation** (volume contraction up to 4 % at 40 % ABV). Mention this nuance for a full-mark answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these high-yield points before the KTU ESE:**

- ✅ **Vodka** = clear, neutral, distilled spirit; **40 % ABV** standard; 37.5–50 % range permitted.
- ✅ **Key raw materials:** wheat, rye, potato, corn, molasses, sometimes fruits.
- ✅ **Five unit operations:** Milling → Mashing (65–70 °C) → Fermentation (20–25 °C, 48–96 h) → Distillation (multi-column) → Filtration (charcoal) → Cutting.
- ✅ **Theoretical yield:** **0.511 kg ethanol per kg glucose** (commit the 51.1 % rule).
- ✅ **Practical yield:** 90–95 % of theoretical → **~0.47 kg/kg**.
- ✅ **Azeotrope:** 95.6 % ABV, 78.2 °C, 89.4 mol % — limits simple distillation.
- ✅ **Multi-column distillation:** Beer column → Rectifier → Hydro-selection → NGS (96.0–96.5 %).
- ✅ **Copper's role:** Removes sulfur compounds and forms copper-soaps with fatty acids.
- ✅ **Activated charcoal:** Adsorbs fusel oils, aldehydes, esters, methanol.
- ✅ **Cutting water:** Demineralized, low TDS (< 50 ppm), UV-sterilized.
- ✅ **Key formulas:** $C_1V_1 = C_2V_2$; $V_{\text{EtOH}} = m_{\text{EtOH}} / 0.789$; $Y_{\text{actual}} = 0.92 \times 0.5114$.
- ✅ **CO₂ byproduct:** **0.489 kg per kg glucose** — captured as food-grade CO₂.
- ✅ **Russian GOST vs Polish standards:** Both mandate multi-stage filtration; Polish tradition includes rye or potato; Russian tradition includes wheat.
- ✅ **Volumetric pitfall:** Ethanol-water mixing shows **~4 % volume contraction** — use mass balance for precision.
- ✅ **Distillation energy:** Column distillation (5200 kJ/L) is *more energy-efficient* than repeated pot distillation (12,000 kJ/L) because of internal heat recycling.
- ✅ **Common KTU pitfall:** Do not confuse vodka's *neutrality goal* with whisky's *congener retention* — vodka is an exercise in *purification*, not *flavor development*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gin and Non-Carbonated Non-Alcoholic Beverages Such as Coffee, Tea and Cocoa Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gin and Non-Carbonated Non-Alcoholic Beverages Such as Coffee, Tea and Cocoa Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Gin — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gin** is a distilled, **non-aged** spirit (minimum **37.5% ABV** in the EU; **40% ABV** in the US) whose **predominant flavour** must be derived from **juniper berries** (*Juniperus communis*). It is produced by redistilling neutral grain spirit (NGS) of agricultural origin in the presence of natural botanicals, the chief among which is juniper.

**KTU-Syllabus Variants of Gin (must remember):**
| Variant | Production Style | Distillation Mode | Botanical Addition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **London Dry** | One-shot redistillation | All botanicals in pot still | **No** post-distillation sugar/colour |
| **Distilled Gin** | Redistillation of NGS | Botanicals in pot still | Limited post-additions allowed |
| **Compound Gin** | Cold-compounded | No redistillation | Botanical extracts simply mixed |
| **Genever/Jenever** | Malt-wine base + juniper | Pot still with malt spirit base | Malted barley/wheat, rye |

### 1.2 Coffee — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coffee** is a non-carbonated, hot/cold beverage prepared by aqueous extraction (brewing) of roasted, ground beans of *Coffea arabica*, *Coffea canephora* (Robusta), *Coffea liberica*, or *Coffea excelsa*. The green beans must undergo **dehulling, roasting (180–240 °C), and grinding** prior to extraction. **Decaffeinated coffee** must contain < 0.1% caffeine by mass of roasted bean.

### 1.3 Tea — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tea** (Camellia sinensis) is an **infusion-based, non-carbonated beverage** produced from the young, tender leaves and buds of the tea plant. Six legally recognised commercial classes exist: **Black, Green, White, Yellow, Oolong, and Dark (Pu-erh)**, each differentiated by the **degree of enzymatic oxidation** (polyphenol oxidase activity) the leaf undergoes post-plucking.

### 1.4 Cocoa — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cocoa** (Theobroma cacao) refers to the fermented, dried, roasted, and ground seeds of the cacao tree, used either as **cocoa powder** (defatted, ≤ 20% fat) or **cocoa mass/liquor** (40–55% fat) to produce **cocoa beverages** (non-alcoholic) and chocolate. Cocoa beverages are **non-carbonated** suspensions/colloids prepared by aqueous extraction of the roasted nib or by re-suspension of cocoa powder in milk/water with sugar.

---

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Coffee Shop" Analogy — why all four are clubbed together:**
>
> Imagine you walk into a café and order **any hot drink**. Whether it is a *Gin & Tonic* (alcoholic, distilled, infused), *Filter Coffee* (brewed, roasted bean extraction), *Masala Chai* (infused, oxidised leaf), or *Hot Chocolate* (suspended, roasted seed solids) — the **principles of "turning a bitter plant into a delicious drink"** are identical:
>
> 1. **Clean the raw material** (winnow, sift, pluck, destone).
> 2. **Unlock locked-in aroma** (roasting, fermentation, distillation).
> 3. **Capture the flavour into water/ethanol** (extraction, infusion, redistillation).
> 4. **Make it safe + shelf-stable** (pasteurisation, packaging).
>
> All four beverages are **non-carbonated** (no forced CO₂), which is why KTU groups them under one module. Gin is the **odd-one-out** because it uses **ethanol as the extraction solvent** instead of water, and is the only **distilled** product in the lot.

### 1.6 Key Process Metrics (Bold Constants for Memorisation)

- **Gin** — BotANICAL-to-spirit ratio: **1:10 to 1:50 (w/v)**; typical juniper loading: **8–15 g/L**; redistillation cut: **heads discarded up to 70% ABV, hearts collected at 80–85% ABV**.
- **Coffee** — Roast loss: **15–20% mass loss**; moisture in green bean: **10–12%**; moisture in roasted bean: **2–4%**; optimal grind: **500–700 µm** for drip brew.
- **Tea** — Moisture in finished tea: **≤ 7% (w/w)**; fermentation (oxidation) duration for black tea: **60–180 min**; CTC cut-size grades (grades **PD, RD, SFTG, D, F, BOPF**).
- **Cocoa** — Fermentation duration: **5–7 days**; nib-to-powder yield: **≈ 80%**; cocoa butter melting point: **30–35 °C**; conching time for beverage cocoa: **6–24 h**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 GIN — Theoretical Process Breakdown

**Stage 1: Neutral Grain Spirit (NGS) Generation**
- Raw material: maize, rye, wheat, barley, sugarcane, or grape.
- Saccharification (enzymatic) → Fermentation with *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (yeast load 0.1–0.3% w/v; 25–35 °C; 48–72 h).
- Distillation in **column still** to produce NGS at **≥ 96% ABV** (the azeotropic limit).

**Stage 2: Botanical Selection & Preparation**
- Juniper berries: lightly **crushed** (not powdered) to release essential oils.
- Other botanicals: coriander seed, angelica root, orris root, lemon peel, cassia bark, cardamom, cubeb berries, grains of paradise.
- Many botanicals are **categorised** by distillation timing:
  - *Hard botanicals* (roots, seeds) — added **first**; need long contact.
  - *Soft botanicals* (peels, flowers) — added **later** to avoid burning.

**Stage 3: Pot-Still Redistillation (London Dry Method)**
1. Dilute NGS to **~50% ABV** with demineralised water (called *charge alcohol*).
2. Load copper pot still (copper removes sulphur compounds via chemisorption).
3. Add botanical basket or steep botanicals directly in charge.
4. Heat slowly; collect **foreshots** (methanol, acetaldehyde — discarded).
5. Collect **heads, hearts, tails**; hearts = clean gin fraction.
6. Dilute with demineralised water to **40–47% ABV**.
7. **No sugar, glycerol, or artificial colour** may be added.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why copper pot stills?**
> Copper catalytically reacts with dimethyl trisulphide (DMTS) and other volatile sulphur compounds to form non-volatile copper sulphides, removing the "rubbery" off-note from the spirit. This is the single most cited reason pot stills are irreplaceable for gin.

**Stage 4: Maturation, Filtration & Bottling**
- Gin is **not** barrel-aged (unlike whisky/rum).
- Final filtration through **cellulose or membrane filters (0.45 µm)**.
- Bottled at **40% ABV** minimum.

---

### 2.2 COFFEE — Theoretical Process Breakdown

**Stage 1: Green Bean Processing (Post-Harvest)**

Two principal methods determine green-bean quality and final flavour profile:

| Method | Steps | Region | Flavour Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry (Natural)** | Whole cherries sun-dried 2–4 weeks; mechanically hulled | Brazil, Ethiopia, Yemen | Fruity, heavy body, low acidity |
| **Wet (Washed)** | Pulping → Fermentation 12–72 h → Washing → Mechanical drying → Hulling | Colombia, Kenya, Java | Bright acidity, floral, clean cup |

**Stage 2: Roasting**
Roasting is a **non-isothermal endothermic-then-exothermic pyrolytic process** governed by the **Maillard reaction, Strecker degradation, and caramelisation**.

$$\Delta H_{roast} \approx \text{Endothermic (0–150 °C)} \rightarrow \text{Exothermic (150–230 °C)}$$

**Key Roast Phases (must memorise for KTU):**
1. *Drying phase* (50–150 °C): free + bound water driven off.
2. *Yellowing/Maillard onset* (150–180 °C): amino–carbonyl condensation.
3. *First Crack* (196–205 °C): beans expand, audible "pop", internal pressure ~2.5 bar.
4. *Roast development* (after first crack): 18–25% of total roast time; defines acidity vs body balance.
5. *Second Crack* (220–230 °C): cellulose breaks; oil migration to bean surface — *French/Italian* roast territory.
6. *Drop & Quench*: rapid cooling with air or water spray to halt reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Roast Loss (RL)** is a critical commercial parameter:
> $$ RL\,(\%) = \frac{m_{green} - m_{roasted}}{m_{green}} \times 100 $$
> Typical: **15–20%**.

**Stage 3: Grinding**
Grind size vs brew method — must memorise for KTU:

| Brew Method | Particle Size (µm) |
|---|---|
| Turkish | < 220 (powder-fine) |
| Espresso | 250–300 |
| Filter/Drip | 500–700 |
| French Press | 800–1000 |
| Cold Brew | 1000–1300 (coarse) |

**Stage 4: Brewing (Extraction)**
- **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids)** target for ideal cup = **1.15–1.35%**.
- **Extraction Yield (EY)** = mass of solutes extracted / mass of dry coffee.
- Standard Espresso EY: **18–22%**.
- Filter coffee EY: **18–22%**.

---

### 2.3 TEA — Theoretical Process Breakdown

Tea is the **only beverage** in this module where **enzymatic oxidation** is a deliberate, controlled, and **defining** step.

**Stage 1: Plucking**
Standard: *"two leaves and a bud"* — highest concentration of **polyphenols (catechins)** and **methylxanthines (caffeine)**.
- Tender shoots: catechins = 25–30% dry weight; caffeine = 3–4%.

**Stage 2: Withering (12–18 h)**
- Physical withering: leaf loses ~30% moisture; turgid leaf becomes flaccid for rolling.
- Chemical withering: cell membranes become permeable → catechins and polyphenol oxidase (PPO) come into contact.

**Stage 3: Rolling / CTC (Crush-Tear-Curl)**
- **Orthodox**: rollers twist leaf → long, wiry leaf appearance (Darjeeling, Assam whole-leaf).
- **CTC**: leaf passes through corrugated rollers → small, granular pellets (Indian tea-bag grade).
- **LTP (Lawrie Tea Processor)**: vortex type, used for low-cost CTC.

**Stage 4: Fermentation / Oxidation (CRITICAL STEP)**

The enzymatic oxidation of **catechins → theaflavins → thearubigins**:

$$\text{Catechins} \xrightarrow{PPO + O_2} \text{Quinones} \xrightarrow{condensation} \text{Theaflavins (TF)} \xrightarrow{polymerisation} \text{Thearubigins (TR)}$$

- **Theaflavins (TF)**: 0.3–2.0% of dry leaf → give **brightness, briskness, golden colour**.
- **Thearubigins (TR)**: 9–19% of dry leaf → give **depth, body, dark colour**.
- **TF/TR ratio** is the master metric for cup quality.

**Stage 5: Drying (Firing)**
- Hot air at **90–110 °C** for 20–30 min.
- Moisture brought from ~60% → **< 7%** (mandatory for shelf stability).
- PPO enzyme is **denatured** at this point; oxidation stops.

**Stage 6: Grading & Sorting**
- **Orange Pekoe (OP)**, **Pekoe (P)**, **Broken Orange Pekoe (BOP)**, **Fannings (F)**, **Dust (D)**.
- For **Darjeeling**: SFTGFOP1 = *Super Fine Tippy Golden Flowery Orange Pekoe One* — finest grade.

---

### 2.4 COCOA — Theoretical Process Breakdown

**Stage 1: Fermentation (5–7 days) — *The most critical step***

Cacao pods are opened, beans (with pulp) placed in **wooden sweat-boxes** or **heap-fermented in banana leaves**.

| Day | Microflora | Temperature | Biochemical Event |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1–2 | *Yeasts* (Saccharomyces, Candida) | 28–32 °C | Pulp sugars → ethanol + CO₂; pulp liquefies |
| 2–4 | *Lactic acid bacteria* | 32–42 °C | Ethanol → lactic acid; pulp drains away |
| 4–7 | *Acetic acid bacteria* | 45–52 °C | Ethanol → acetic acid (kills embryo, kills seed coat) |

The **acetic acid + heat** kills the seed embryo (preventing germination) and **permeabilises the seed coat** so that internal enzymatic reactions can proceed during drying.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without fermentation, cocoa beans cannot develop chocolate flavour.** Fermentation reduces bitterness (degrades **polyphenols** by ~50%) and **mucilage sugars** are converted, triggering enzymatic protein breakdown that later forms **chocolate aroma precursors** during roasting.

**Stage 2: Drying**
- Sun-drying (5–14 days) or mechanical drying (forced air at 60–70 °C) to bring moisture from ~60% → **6–8%**.
- Critical to prevent **mould growth** (e.g., *Aspergillus*).

**Stage 3: Roasting (120–150 °C, 15–45 min)**
- Drives off **acetic acid & moisture**.
- **Maillard + Strecker degradation** produces **pyrazines, aldehydes, pyrones** — the characteristic chocolate aroma.
- Temperature-time balance: **130 °C × 30 min** is a typical industry sweet spot.

**Stage 4: Winnowing & Nib Production**
- Roasted beans are cracked → shells separated from **nibs** by air classifier.
- Shells (testae) = 20–25% of bean; nibs = 75–80%.
- **Nib composition**: 50–55% fat (cocoa butter), 10–15% protein, 8–12% starch, 4–6% polyphenols, 1–2% theobromine + caffeine.

**Stage 5: Grinding to Liquor**
- Nibs ground in **stone mills** (3–5 stone stages) → **cocoa liquor/mass**.
- Particle size: 20–30 µm (imparts mouthfeel).
- Heat of friction melts cocoa butter → liquid paste.

**Stage 6: Pressing & Powder Production**
- Hydraulic pressing at **300–500 bar** separates:
  - **Cocoa butter** (~50% of liquor).
  - **Cocoa press-cake** (defatted, ~22% fat residual).
- Press-cake is pulverised → **cocoa powder**.

**Stage 7: Alkalisation (Dutching)**
- Treatment with **K₂CO₃ or Na₂CO₃** at 70–100 °C for 30 min–2 h.
- Raises pH from 5.0–5.5 → **7.0–8.0**.
- Darkens colour, mellows flavour, increases dispersibility.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ABV_{final} = ABV_{distillate} \times \dfrac{V_{distillate}}{V_{total}}$ | Gin cut dilution | % v/v |
| 2 | $Y_{juniper} = \dfrac{m_{juniper}}{V_{charge}} $ | Botanical load | g/L |
| 3 | $RL\,(\%) = \dfrac{m_{green} - m_{roasted}}{m_{green}} \times 100$ | Coffee roast loss | % |
| 4 | $EY\,(\%) = \dfrac{m_{solutes\,extracted}}{m_{dry\,coffee}} \times 100$ | Brewing yield | % |
| 5 | $TDS\,(\%) = \dfrac{m_{solutes\,extracted}}{m_{brew}} \times 100$ | Cup strength | % |
| 6 | $W_{loss}\,(\%) = \dfrac{m_{fresh} - m_{withered}}{m_{fresh}} \times 100$ | Tea withering loss | % |
| 7 | $\dfrac{TF}{TR}\,ratio$ | Black tea quality index | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{fermentation} = \dfrac{m_{pulp\,consumed}}{m_{pulp\,initial}} \times 100$ | Cocoa pulp conversion | % |
| 9 | $Y_{nib} = \dfrac{m_{nib}}{m_{bean\,whole}} \times 100$ | Cocoa winnowing yield | % |
| 10 | $m_{butter} = m_{liquor} \times f_{fat}$ | Cocoa press yield | kg |
| 11 | $Moisture_{final} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | Drying endpoint | % w/w |
| 12 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Roast/dryer heat duty | kJ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions to remember for KTU:**
> - $ABV_{gin} \geq 37.5\%$ (EU), $40\%$ (US).
> - Caffeine limit in decaffeinated coffee: $\leq 0.1\%$ (EU/India FSSAI).
> - Moisture in finished tea: $\leq 7\%$.
> - Moisture in dried cocoa bean: $\leq 8\%$.
> - Caffeine in tea: $2.5 - 4.5\%$; Theobromine in cocoa: $1 - 3\%$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Distillery engineers** designing copper pot stills use the reflux ratio equations of multicomponent distillation to set hearts-cut volumes.
- **Coffee roasters** use **PID-controlled drum roasters** with a 1st-derivative trigger (ΔT/Δt slope) to detect First Crack by bean colour/temperature profile rather than by ear.
- **Tea factories** monitor *TR/TF ratio* via **NIRS (Near-Infrared Spectroscopy)** for instant quality grading.
- **Cocoa processors** use **expeller pressing** in modern plants instead of hydraulic pressing for continuous throughput and energy efficiency.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gin Dilution Mass Balance

**Problem Statement:** A London Dry gin distillery produces a hearts cut of **85% ABV** and **120 L** volume. The target bottling strength is **40% ABV**. How much demineralised water must be added?

**Given:** $C_1 = 85\%$, $V_1 = 120$ L, $C_2 = 40\%$.

**Step 1 — Apply the principle of volumetric alcohol conservation.**
The total volume of pure ethanol remains constant during dilution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ethanol} &= C_1 \cdot V_1 = C_2 \cdot V_2 \\
\Rightarrow V_2 &= \dfrac{C_1 \cdot V_1}{C_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= \dfrac{85 \times 120}{40} \\
&= \dfrac{10200}{40} \\
&= 255 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volume of demineralised water to be added.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{water} &= V_2 - V_1 \\
&= 255 - 120 \\
&= 135 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification via mass-balance check.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ethanol,final} &= 40\% \times 255 = 102 \text{ L} \\
V_{ethanol,initial} &= 85\% \times 120 = 102 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both volumes of pure ethanol are equal at 102 L. Therefore, mass balance is verified. **Result: 135 L of demineralised water.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Coffee Roast Loss

**Problem Statement:** A roaster begins with **250 kg** of green coffee beans. After roasting to a *Full City* profile, the roasted mass is **207 kg**. Compute the roast loss.

**Given:** $m_{green} = 250$ kg, $m_{roasted} = 207$ kg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RL\,(\%) &= \dfrac{m_{green} - m_{roasted}}{m_{green}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{250 - 207}{250} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{43}{250} \times 100 \\
&= 17.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $17.2\%$ mass loss corresponds to moisture loss (~10–12%) + pyrolytic gas escape (CO₂, CO, H₂O vapour, volatiles — 5–7%). This is a typical *Full City* roast loss range.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Brewing Extraction Yield (Filter Coffee)

**Problem Statement:** A filter coffee brewer uses **18 g** of dry coffee at grind size **600 µm**. The brewed cup volume is **300 mL** with measured TDS = **1.30%**. Compute the Extraction Yield.

**Given:** $m_{coffee} = 18$ g, $V_{brew} = 300$ mL (assume density $\rho \approx 1.0$ g/mL → $m_{brew} = 300$ g), $TDS = 1.30\%$.

**Step 1 — Calculate the mass of solutes extracted.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{solutes} &= TDS \times m_{brew} \\
&= \dfrac{1.30}{100} \times 300 \\
&= 3.9 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the extraction yield.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EY\,(\%) &= \dfrac{m_{solutes}}{m_{dry\,coffee}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{3.9}{18} \times 100 \\
&= 21.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $EY = 21.67\%$ falls within the **SCAA "Ideal" range of 18–22%** for filter coffee. The brew is properly balanced.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Tea Withering Mass Balance

**Problem Statement:** A tea factory receives **10,000 kg** of fresh green leaf (moisture content **78%** w.b.). After 14 hours of withering, the leaf weighs **6,250 kg**. Calculate (a) the moisture loss, (b) the withered leaf moisture content (assuming dry-matter conservation).

**Given:** $m_{fresh} = 10{,}000$ kg, $MC_{fresh} = 78\%$.

**Step 1 — Calculate dry-matter mass in fresh leaf (this is conserved).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{dry} &= m_{fresh} \times (1 - MC_{fresh}) \\
&= 10000 \times (1 - 0.78) \\
&= 2200 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate moisture content of withered leaf.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC_{withered} &= 1 - \dfrac{m_{dry}}{m_{withered}} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{2200}{6250} \\
&= 1 - 0.352 \\
&= 0.648 \quad \text{or} \quad 64.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification by mass balance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{water\,lost} &= m_{fresh} - m_{withered} \\
&= 10000 - 6250 \\
&= 3750 \text{ kg of water evaporated}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total mass lost is exactly the water evaporated. **Mass balance is verified.**

**Industry interpretation:** Withered leaf at 64.8% MC is in the **typical pre-rolling range (60–70%)** and the **wither loss (37.5%)** is healthy for Assam CTC manufacture.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Cocoa Butter Yield from Press Cake

**Problem Statement:** A cocoa processor has **1000 kg** of cocoa liquor containing **52%** fat. The hydraulic press extracts **85%** of the available butter. Calculate: (a) mass of cocoa butter produced, (b) fat content of the residual press cake.

**Given:** $m_{liquor} = 1000$ kg, $f_{fat,liquor} = 52\%$, $\eta_{press} = 85\%$.

**Step 1 — Mass of fat initially in the liquor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{fat,total} &= m_{liquor} \times f_{fat,liquor} \\
&= 1000 \times 0.52 \\
&= 520 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of cocoa butter extracted.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{butter} &= m_{fat,total} \times \eta_{press} \\
&= 520 \times 0.85 \\
&= 442 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of residual fat in the press cake.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{fat,cake} &= m_{fat,total} - m_{butter} \\
&= 520 - 442 \\
&= 78 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of press cake and its residual fat content.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{cake} &= m_{liquor} - m_{butter} \\
&= 1000 - 442 \\
&= 558 \text{ kg} \\
f_{fat,cake} &= \dfrac{78}{558} \times 100 = 13.98\% \approx 14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** 442 kg of cocoa butter and 558 kg of press cake containing 14% residual fat. ✓ **This 14% residual fat is exactly the specification for "fat reduced" cocoa powder.** If more butter is pressed out (lower % residual), the powder becomes "high-fat" cocoa.

---

### 3.6 Master Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Gin | Coffee | Tea | Cocoa |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solvent of extraction** | Ethanol + water | Hot water | Hot water | Hot water / milk |
| **Key process step** | Redistillation | Roasting | Enzymatic oxidation | Fermentation |
| **Controlling enzyme** | None (yeast only) | None | Polyphenol oxidase (PPO) | Endogenous + microbial |
| **Temp. of key step** | 78–82 °C (pot still) | 180–230 °C | 25–30 °C | 45–52 °C |
| **Time of key step** | 4–8 h distillation | 8–18 min roasting | 60–180 min oxidation | 5–7 days |
| **Final moisture** | 60% ABV (liquid) | 2–4% | ≤ 7% | ≤ 8% |
| **Volatiles of interest** | Terpenes (α-pinene) | 200+ (furans, pyrazines) | Linalool, geraniol | Pyrazines, aldehydes |
| **Yield from raw** | ~10–12 L NGS/100 kg grain | ~0.8 kg roasted/1 kg green | ~0.22 kg tea/1 kg fresh leaf | ~0.4 kg powder/1 kg bean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Cross-Architecture:**
> Notice that **cocoa is the only beverage with TWO sequential transformation steps** (microbial fermentation → thermal roasting), making its flavour profile the most complex of the four. Tea has the *gentlest* thermal input (≤ 110 °C drying), preserving the volatile aroma compounds intact.
