## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** *List any three quality parameters that differentiate London Dry Gin from Compound Gin. State the minimum legal ABV for gin in India as per FSSAI.*

**Model Answer:**
London Dry Gin differs from Compound Gin in three principal ways:
1. **Method of botanical incorporation:** London Dry is produced by **redistillation of neutral grain spirit with botanicals** in a copper pot still. Compound Gin is produced by **cold-compounding**, i.e., simply mixing botanical extracts/essences into NGS without distillation.
2. **Production timeline:** London Dry requires **4–8 hours of redistillation** with controlled cuts (heads, hearts, tails); Compound Gin is ready **within hours** of flavour addition.
3. **Post-distillation additives:** London Dry permits **no addition of sugar, glycerol, or artificial colour** after distillation; Compound Gin may contain permitted flavouring agents and limited sweeteners.
4. **Sensoric profile:** London Dry is cleaner, more integrated and botanically forward; Compound Gin often shows harsh, isolate-driven flavour.

**Minimum ABV (FSSAI):** Gin sold in India must have a minimum **42.8% v/v ABV** (Food Safety and Standards (Alcoholic Beverages) Regulations, 2018). *(Marks: 1.5 + 1.5)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *Define the term "roast loss" in coffee processing. State the typical range of roast loss for a medium roast.*

**Model Answer:**
**Roast Loss (RL)** is the **percentage decrease in bean mass** from green (raw) state to roasted state, caused primarily by the **evaporation of free and bound water** and the **pyrolytic release of volatile compounds** (CO₂, CO, water vapour, and organic volatiles) during the roasting process.

$$
RL\,(\%) = \dfrac{m_{green} - m_{roasted}}{m_{green}} \times 100
$$

**Typical roast loss range for a Medium Roast (also called *City* or *American* roast):** approximately **15–18%**.

A roast loss below 12% usually indicates **under-development**, while a loss above 22% indicates a **Dark/Italian roast** with significant pyrolytic breakdown. *(Marks: 2 + 1)*

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Choice]** *(a)* Explain the **manufacture of London Dry Gin** with a neat flowchart. *(b)* Discuss the role of **copper pot stills** in gin distillation and describe the **function of each botanical category** used.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Manufacture of London Dry Gin — Flowchart:**

1. **NGS Preparation:** Ferment a starchy/sugary substrate (maize, rye, sugarcane molasses) with *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* at 25–35 °C for 48–72 h to obtain wash at 8–12% ABV.
2. **Column Distillation:** Distil wash through a 2-column continuous still to obtain NGS at ≥ 96% ABV (ethanol-water azeotropic limit).
3. **Charge Preparation:** Dilute NGS to 50% ABV using demineralised water in a copper pot still (typical capacity: 1000–5000 L).
4. **Botanical Loading:** Add **juniper berries** (8–15 g/L) — primary botanical. Add **hard botanicals** (coriander seed, angelica root, orris root, cassia bark) at the start. **Soft botanicals** (citrus peel, lavender, cardamom) are added in the last 30–60 min of distillation to avoid scorching.
5. **Pot-Still Redistillation:** Heat slowly (steam jacket); collect **foreshots** (methanol, acetaldehyde) and **heads** for discard. Collect **hearts** at 80–85% ABV — this is the gin fraction.
6. **Dilution to Bottling Strength:** Dilute hearts with demineralised water to 40–47% ABV (target: 40% in India/UK, 40–47% in EU/US).
7. **Maturation, Filtration & Bottling:** Rest 24–48 h for flavour integration; cellulose-filter; bottle at 40% ABV. London Dry Gin is **not barrel-aged**.

**(b) Role of Copper Pot Stills:**

- **Sulphur removal:** Copper surfaces chemisorb volatile sulphur compounds (dimethyl sulphide, dimethyl trisulphide) into non-volatile copper sulphides, eliminating the "rubbery, oniony" off-note typical of cheap stainless-steel distillation.
- **Catalytic esterification:** Copper catalyses the formation of fruity **ethyl esters** during distillation, contributing to gin aroma complexity.
- **Heat distribution:** Copper's high thermal conductivity ensures even heating of the wash and prevents localised scorching of botanical material.

**Botanical Categories and Functions:**

| Category | Examples | Function | Distillation Timing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Juniper berries | Provide terpene-dominated, piney, resinous backbone | Start |
| **Hard botanicals** | Coriander seed, angelica root, orris root, cassia bark | Provide spicy, earthy, base notes; need long extraction | Start |
| **Soft botanicals** | Lemon/orange peel, cardamom, cubeb berries, grains of paradise | Provide bright, citrus, floral top notes; burn easily | Last 30–60 min |
| **Floral (premium)** | Lavender, rose petals, elderflower | Provide floral middle notes; very volatile | Final 15–20 min |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Many students write "London Dry Gin is aged in oak barrels" — **WRONG.** Gin is bottled un-aged. Marks will be cut if maturation in oak is claimed.
> Also, students often confuse "pot still" with "column still" — NGS is made in **column** stills; only the **botanical redistillation** uses **pot** stills.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice (Alternate)]** *(a)* With a flowchart, describe the **manufacture of black tea** from plucked leaf. *(b)* Explain the **role of polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** in black-tea processing and the significance of the **TF/TR ratio** as a quality index.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Manufacture of Black Tea — Flowchart:**

1. **Plucking:** Tender "two leaves and a bud" harvested by hand or shear.
2. **Withering:** Trough withering for 12–18 h with forced air at 25–30 °C; leaf moisture falls from 75–80% → 60–65%; cell membranes become permeable, releasing PPO enzyme and catechins into proximity.
3. **Rolling (Orthodox) or CTC:** Orthodox rollers twist and break the leaf cells, exposing internal enzymes to air. CTC rollers crush, tear and curl leaves into small granular pellets suitable for tea-bag grade.
4. **Fermentation / Oxidation:** Rolled leaf spread in 4–8 cm layers on the floor or in fermenting troughs for 60–180 min at 25–30 °C with high humidity (> 95% RH). Catechins oxidise to theaflavins and then to thearubigins.
5. **Drying (Firing):** Tray dryer or fluidised-bed dryer at 90–110 °C for 20–30 min until moisture falls below 7%. PPO enzyme is heat-denatured, halting oxidation. The leaf turns dark brown-black.
6. **Sorting & Grading:** Vibratory and gravity separators grade leaf into **OP, BOP, Fannings, Dust** fractions.
7. **Blending, Storage, Packaging:** Stored in moisture-proof containers or kraft-paper sacks with foil liner.

**(b) Role of PPO and Significance of TF/TR:**

- **Polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** is a copper-containing oxidoreductase enzyme naturally present in tea leaf cells, localised in chloroplasts. After withering and rolling ruptures cell walls, PPO comes into contact with **catechins** (epicatechin, epigallocatechin, epicatechin gallate) in the presence of atmospheric oxygen.
- PPO catalyses:
  $$\text{Catechins} + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \xrightarrow{PPO} \text{Ortho-quinones}$$
- The ortho-quinones undergo **non-enzymatic condensation** to form theaflavins (TF), which then polymerise to thearubigins (TR).
- **Theaflavins (TF):** Yellow-orange pigments; impart **briskness, brightness, astringency and golden colour** to the brew. Levels in made tea: 0.3–2.0%.
- **Thearubigins (TR):** Reddish-brown pigments; impart **body, depth, dark colour, mellow astringency**. Levels: 9–19% of dry leaf.
- **TF/TR Ratio Significance:** A **higher TF/TR ratio** (≈ 0.05–0.10) indicates **bright, brisk, well-fermented tea** (premium Darjeeling, Assam). A **lower TF/TR ratio** (≈ 0.02–0.04) indicates **over-fermented, dull tea**. NIRS is used industrially to monitor this ratio online.
- Over-oxidation → flat taste; under-oxidation → grassy, unfermented taste.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> "PPO oxidises catechins to thearubigins directly" — **WRONG**. PPO oxidises catechins to **quinones**, and *thearubigins are formed by non-enzymatic polymerisation of theaflavins*. This intermediate step is a guaranteed 2-mark question every year. Skip it → lose marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🍸 **Gin** = NGS + juniper (primary botanical) + redistillation in copper pot still. **No barrel ageing.** Min 40% ABV.
- ☕ **Coffee Roast Loss** = 15–20%. **TDS** target = 1.15–1.35%. **EY** target = 18–22%. **First Crack** = 196–205 °C.
- 🍵 **Tea Withering** reduces leaf moisture from ~78% → ~62%. **PPO** oxidises **catechins → quinones → theaflavins → thearubigins**. Final moisture ≤ 7%.
- 🍫 **Cocoa Fermentation** is a 3-stage microbial succession: **yeast → LAB → acetic acid bacteria**, spanning 5–7 days. Roasting at 130 °C × 30 min develops pyrazines. Press cake contains ~14% residual fat (commercial "low-fat cocoa").
- 🔑 **Copper pot still** is essential for gin because it removes **sulphur compounds** via chemisorption.
- 🔑 **Maillard + Strecker degradation** are the universal flavour-generating reactions during roasting of coffee and cocoa.
- 🔑 **Cocoa is the only beverage with two distinct transformation steps**: fermentation + roasting.
- 🔑 **Tea is the only beverage** where enzymatic oxidation is the *defining* manufacturing step.
- 🔑 **Decaffeinated coffee** must contain ≤ **0.1%** caffeine on a roasted-bean mass basis (FSSAI/EU).
- 🔑 **Theaflavins (TF) and Thearubigins (TR)** are the two pigment classes defining black-tea quality; their ratio is measured by **NIRS** in modern tea factories.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dairy Products - Milk Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dairy Products - Milk Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Milk Processing (KTU 2024 Definition):** Milk processing refers to the integrated sequence of unit operations — *reception, clarification, standardization, homogenization, thermal treatment (pasteurization/sterilization/UHT), cooling, and aseptic packaging* — applied to raw bovine milk to transform it into safe, shelf-stable, and nutritionally preserved dairy products that comply with the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** and **Codex Alimentarius** microbiological and compositional standards.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PECHT747 — Food Processing and Technology**, Module 4 treats milk as a *biological colloidal system* in which fat globules are emulsified in a continuous aqueous phase of water, lactose, proteins (casein and whey), and dissolved minerals. Every downstream process manipulates one or more of these colloidal phases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Milk as a Tiny City"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for First-Time Learners:** Imagine raw milk as a busy, dirty city.
> - The **fat globules** are floating balloons of various sizes.
> - The **casein micelles** are protein "buildings" suspended in water.
> - The **lactose and minerals** are dissolved "traffic signals" regulating viscosity and freezing point.
> - **Bacteria** are unwanted intruders (pathogens).
>
> Milk processing is essentially a city-cleaning and city-organizing exercise: we *screen* the trash, *standardize* the balloon sizes, *crush oversized balloons into uniform small ones* (homogenization), *evict bacteria with heat* (pasteurization), *package the cleaned city in sterile containers* (aseptic packaging), and *chill it to slow down any remaining intruders* (cold storage).

## 1.3 Standard Compositional Metrics of Bovine Milk

| Component | Mean % (w/w) | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| Water | 87.1 | Continuous phase (solvent) |
| Lactose | 4.8 | Carbohydrate energy source |
| Fat | 3.9 | Carrier of fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K |
| Protein | 3.4 | Casein (80%) + Whey (20%) |
| Minerals (ash) | 0.8 | Calcium, Phosphorus, Sodium, Potassium |
| Vitamins & trace | traces | B-complex, Vitamin A |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** Examiners often ask for the *total solids (TS)* and *solids-not-fat (SNF)* values. Memorize:
> - **Total Solids (TS) ≈ 12.5 % – 14.5 %** (mean **13.0 %**)
> - **Solids-Not-Fat (SNF) ≈ 8.5 % – 9.5 %** (mean **9.0 %**)
> - **Fat = TS − SNF**
> - The legal minimum for *toned/standardized* milk in India is **4.5 % fat and 8.5 % SNF**.

## 1.4 Standard Process Metrics (Numerical Benchmarks)

- **Pasteurization (HTST):** **72 °C for 15 seconds**, then immediate cooling to **≤ 4 °C**.
- **Pasteurization (LTLT / Batch):** **63 °C for 30 minutes**.
- **UHT treatment:** **135 – 150 °C for 2 – 5 seconds**.
- **Homogenization pressure:** **100 – 250 bar** (typical **150 – 200 bar**), two-stage.
- **Standard refrigeration storage:** **4 °C ± 1 °C**, shelf life **5 – 7 days**.
- **UHT shelf life (aseptic):** **6 – 9 months at ambient temperature (≤ 25 °C)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024:** Module 4 explicitly requires understanding of *unit operations in sequence*, the *thermo-physical rationale* behind each step, and the *impact on shelf life and nutritional quality*. Always link each step to either **microbial inactivation** or **physical/chemical stability**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Milk-Processing Sequence (Operational Logic)

The dairy industry follows a strict, validated sequence. Each step is *non-substitutable* in the sense that bypassing it leads to either a quality defect or a safety hazard.

1. **Reception & Chilling**
   - Raw milk arrives at the dairy plant at ambient temperature with a typical initial bacterial load of **10⁴ – 10⁶ CFU/mL**.
   - Immediate plate-type heat exchange cools milk to **≤ 4 °C** within *2 hours of milking*.
   - **Why:** The *generation time* of mesophilic spoilage bacteria (e.g., *Lactococcus*, *Pseudomonas*) is ~ 20 – 30 min at 30 °C. Cooling extends the *acceptance window* before further processing.

2. **Quality Testing & Grading**
   - **Clot-on-boiling (COB) test** and **alcohol test (68 % v/v ethanol)** detect abnormal acidity (> 0.18 % lactic acid) and heat-unstable proteins.
   - **Resazurin / Methylene Blue Reduction (MBR) test** estimates total bacterial load by dye-reduction kinetics.
   - **Somatic Cell Count (SCC)** with a threshold of **≤ 4 × 10⁵ cells/mL** indicates udder health (mastitis screening).

3. **Filtration / Clarification**
   - Removes macroscopic debris (hair, straw, sediment) using **in-line nylon mesh filters (50 – 100 µm)**.
   - **Centrifugal clarification** (separator bowl spinning at **6 000 – 8 000 rpm**) removes *sedimentable* impurities based on density differential.

4. **Standardization**
   - Adjusts the fat and SNF content to a target value using the **Pearson Square (Pearson's Quartile) method** or by automatic *in-line blending* of skim milk and cream.
   - **Why:** Legal compliance with FSSAI labeling (e.g., *Standardized milk* = 4.5 % fat, 8.5 % SNF; *Toned milk* = 3.0 % fat, 8.5 % SNF).

5. **Homogenization**
   - Reduces fat globule diameter from **1 – 10 µm** to **< 1 µm** (typically **0.2 – 0.8 µm**) by forcing milk under high pressure through a narrow orifice.
   - **Why:** Prevents creaming (rising of fat), improves whiteness, and gives a uniform mouthfeel.

6. **Thermal Processing (Pasteurization / Sterilization / UHT)**
   - **LTLT, HTST, and UHT** are three *time-temperature combinations* delivering equivalent microbial inactivation but with very different nutrient/quality footprints.
   - **Why:** Inactivates *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* (the historical pasteurization benchmark), *Coxiella burnetii*, *Salmonella spp.*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, and vegetative *E. coli*.

7. **Cooling**
   - Post-pasteurization cooling to **≤ 4 °C** is critical. UHT products are cooled to **20 – 25 °C** before aseptic filling.

8. **Packaging**
   - **HTST pasteurized milk** → glass bottles, LDPE pouches, or gable-top cartons (cold chain mandatory).
   - **UHT milk** → pre-sterilized 6-layer tetra-brik cartons (Tetra Pak); shelf-stable at ambient temperature.

9. **Cold Storage & Distribution**
   - Refrigerated trucks maintain **0 – 4 °C** with a *cold-chain integrity* monitored by data loggers.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Exists — The Engineering Rationale

| Unit Operation | Driver | Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|
| Chilling | Slow bacterial growth | Plate heat exchanger (PHE) |
| Clarification | Remove sediment | Centrifugal force |
| Standardization | Legal/label compliance | Pearson Square math |
| Homogenization | Prevent creaming | High-pressure valve homogenizer |
| Pasteurization | Pathogen kill | Time-Temperature combination |
| Sterilization | Spore destruction | Higher T, longer t (or in-container) |
| UHT | Commercial sterility | Very high T, very short t |
| Aseptic packaging | Recontamination prevention | Sterile laminate + sterile chamber |
| Cold storage | Lag phase of survivors | Refrigeration cycle (VCC) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Table Convention:** This is a markdown table. Mathematical absolute values, conditions, and "given that" expressions are written using `\vert` (LaTeX) rather than the pipe character `|` to avoid breaking table syntax. All numeric values are bolded as required.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{TS} = \text{Fat} + \text{SNF}$ | Total Solids | mean **13.0 %** | % (w/w) |
| 2 | $\text{TS} = 1.21 \, G_f + 0.25$ | TS from fat + Gerber reading | empirical, ISI | % |
| 3 | $\rho_{\text{milk}} \approx 1.030 - 1.035$ | Density of whole milk | at **20 °C** | g/cm³ |
| 4 | $D_T$ value | Time for 90 % kill at temp $T$ | decimal reduction time | minutes |
| 5 | $Z_T$ value | Temp rise for 10× change in $D$ | **Z = 6.5 °C** for *M. tuberculosis* | °C |
| 6 | $\log \left( \dfrac{N_0}{N} \right) = \dfrac{t}{D_T}$ | Microbial inactivation order | first-order kinetics | dimensionless |
| 7 | $F_0 = 10^{\,(T - 121.1) \, / \, Z}$ | Equivalent sterilization time at **121.1 °C** | with $Z = 10$ °C | minutes |
| 8 | $\dot{V}_{\text{cream}} = \dot{V}_{\text{milk}} \cdot \dfrac{(F_t - F_s)}{(F_c - F_s)}$ | Standardization flow (cream) | Pearson outcome | L/h |
| 9 | $P_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | Homogenizer pressure ratio | stage-1 / stage-2 typically **2 : 1** | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{\text{hom}} \approx 150 - 200$ | Homogenization pressure | two-stage | bar |
| 11 | $F_{\text{Fouling}} = 1 - \dfrac{U_{\text{fouled}}}{U_{\text{clean}}}$ | Heat exchanger fouling index | for PHE | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\tau_{\text{hold}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{holding tube}}}{\dot{V}}$ | HTST residence time | **15 s** target | seconds |
| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}}{T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{ref}}}$ | Pasteurizer efficiency | ideal $\rightarrow$ 1.0 | dimensionless |
| 14 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat duty on regenerator | $c_p^{\text{milk}} \approx \textbf{3.93 kJ/(kg·K)}$ | kW |
| 15 | $\dot{N}_{\text{out}} = \dot{N}_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D_T}$ | Survivors after $t$ minutes | post-pasteurization | CFU/mL |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same time-temperature equivalence principle ($D_T$, $Z_T$, $F_0$) underpins **pharmaceutical sterilization**, **canned food retort processing**, and **in-container sterilization** of canned vegetables. The Pearson Square method is the *de facto* standard in **edible oil blending**, **juice formulation**, and **pharmaceutical syrup compounding** for two-component mixing. Homogenization technology is borrowed from **emulsion paint manufacturing** and **cosmetics**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Standardization by Pearson Square

**Problem:** A plant has **1 000 L/h of whole milk at 6.0 % fat** and a supply of **skim milk at 0.5 % fat**. The target is **standardized milk at 3.5 % fat**. Calculate the flow rates of skim and whole milk required to make exactly **3.5 % fat** in the blend.

**Step 1 — State the mass (volume) balance for fat.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{whole}} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{whole}} + F_{\text{skim}} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{skim}} &= F_{\text{target}} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{blend}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — State the volumetric balance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{whole}} + \dot{V}_{\text{skim}} &= \dot{V}_{\text{blend}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known values $F_{\text{whole}} = 6.0$, $F_{\text{skim}} = 0.5$, $F_{\text{target}} = 3.5$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
6.0 \, \dot{V}_{\text{whole}} + 0.5 \, \dot{V}_{\text{skim}} &= 3.5 \, (\dot{V}_{\text{whole}} + \dot{V}_{\text{skim}})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side and collect terms.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
6.0 \, \dot{V}_{\text{whole}} + 0.5 \, \dot{V}_{\text{skim}} &= 3.5 \, \dot{V}_{\text{whole}} + 3.5 \, \dot{V}_{\text{skim}} \\
6.0 \, \dot{V}_{\text{whole}} - 3.5 \, \dot{V}_{\text{whole}} &= 3.5 \, \dot{V}_{\text{skim}} - 0.5 \, \dot{V}_{\text{skim}} \\
2.5 \, \dot{V}_{\text{whole}} &= 3.0 \, \dot{V}_{\text{skim}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the ratio of skim to whole.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{skim}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{whole}}} &= \dfrac{2.5}{3.0} = \dfrac{5}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the constraint $\dot{V}_{\text{whole}} + \dot{V}_{\text{skim}} = 1\,000$ L/h.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{whole}} + \dfrac{5}{6} \, \dot{V}_{\text{whole}} &= 1\,000 \\
\dfrac{11}{6} \, \dot{V}_{\text{whole}} &= 1\,000 \\
\dot{V}_{\text{whole}} &= \dfrac{6\,000}{11} \approx \textbf{545.45 L/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{skim}} &= 1\,000 - 545.45 = \textbf{454.55 L/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fat} &= 0.060 \times 545.45 + 0.005 \times 454.55 \\
          &= 32.727 + 2.273 = 35.000 \;\text{kg/h} \\
\dfrac{35.000}{1\,000} &= 0.035 = \textbf{3.5 \%} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pearson Square Shortcut:** Place the target fat (3.5) at the centre. Place whole-milk fat (6.0) at the top-left and skim fat (0.5) at the bottom-left. Diagonal differences: $\vert 3.5 - 0.5 \vert = 3.0$ and $\vert 6.0 - 3.5 \vert = 2.5$. The ratio of *whole : skim* is **3.0 : 2.5 = 6 : 5**. Combined parts = 11, giving the same answer.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Microbial Inactivation & Equivalent Pasteurization Time

**Problem:** A plate heat exchanger delivers HTST pasteurization at **72 °C for 15 s**. The reference $Z$ value for *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* is **6.5 °C** and the reference temperature is **63 °C** (where $D_{63} = 1.5$ min for the reference pathogen). Calculate (a) the $D_{72}$ value, (b) the log reduction of the pathogen, and (c) the equivalent LTLT time at 63 °C.

**Step 1 — Use the $Z$ relationship $D_T = D_{\text{ref}} \cdot 10^{(T_{\text{ref}} - T)/Z}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{72} &= D_{63} \cdot 10^{\,(63 - 72)\,/\,6.5} \\
D_{72} &= 1.5 \times 10^{\,-9/6.5} \\
D_{72} &= 1.5 \times 10^{-1.3846} \\
D_{72} &= 1.5 \times 0.04126 \\
D_{72} &\approx \textbf{0.0619 min} \;\;(\approx 3.71 \text{ s})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply first-order inactivation $\log(N_0/N) = t/D_T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 15 \text{ s} = 0.25 \text{ min} \\
\log \left( \dfrac{N_0}{N} \right) &= \dfrac{0.25}{0.0619} \\
\log \left( \dfrac{N_0}{N} \right) &\approx 4.04
\end{aligned}
$$

The HTST process delivers a **~ 4-log₁₀ reduction** of *M. tuberculosis*, equivalent to a **99.99 % kill**.

**Step 3 — Convert the HTST lethality to an equivalent LTLT time at 63 °C.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{63} &= t_{\text{HTST}} \cdot 10^{\,(72 - 63)\,/\,6.5} \\
F_{63} &= 0.25 \times 10^{9/6.5} \\
F_{63} &= 0.25 \times 10^{1.3846} \\
F_{63} &= 0.25 \times 24.24 \\
F_{63} &\approx \textbf{6.06 min at 63 °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with the LTLT standard (30 min at 63 °C).**

The HTST 15-second process at 72 °C delivers lethality equivalent to about **6 minutes at 63 °C**, which is well below the regulatory 30-minute LTLT baseline — yet the FSSAI recognizes it as adequate because the *reference pathogen* is reduced by > 5-log₁₀ in commercial practice using higher initial counts and integrating safety margins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Insight for KTU 2024 Exam:** *M. tuberculosis* is **not** the most heat-resistant organism in milk. *Coxiella burnetii* ($D_{71.7} \approx 0.86$ min) and the *alkaline phosphatase* enzyme reactivation test are the *operational standards* in modern dairies. The phosphatase test is the official marker of *adequate pasteurization*: pasteurized milk must be **phosphatase-negative** and **peroxidase-positive** (the latter confirming the milk was not over-heated).

## 3.3 Derivation C — Homogenizer Pressure and Fat-Globule Diameter

The Sauter mean diameter $d_{32}$ of fat globules after a high-pressure homogenizer follows an empirical power-law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{32} &= d_0 \, \left( \dfrac{\Delta P_0}{\Delta P} \right)^{\,0.25}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Given:** initial diameter $d_0 = 3.5$ µm at $\Delta P_0 = 50$ bar. The plant operates at $\Delta P = 200$ bar.

**Step 1 — Compute the new Sauter mean diameter.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{32} &= 3.5 \times \left( \dfrac{50}{200} \right)^{0.25} \\
d_{32} &= 3.5 \times (0.25)^{0.25} \\
d_{32} &= 3.5 \times 0.7071 \\
d_{32} &\approx \textbf{2.47 µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Repeat for $\Delta P = 250$ bar.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{32} &= 3.5 \times \left( \dfrac{50}{250} \right)^{0.25} \\
d_{32} &= 3.5 \times (0.20)^{0.25} \\
d_{32} &= 3.5 \times 0.6687 \\
d_{32} &\approx \textbf{2.34 µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Industrial Insight:** A two-stage homogenizer (e.g., 200 bar + 50 bar) produces globules small enough (**< 1 µm**) that Brownian motion alone prevents creaming. The *first stage* does the size reduction; the *second stage* breaks up the re-agglomerated clusters formed downstream of the first valve.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Time-Temperature Regimes

| Process | Temp (°C) | Time | Microbial Target | Nutrient Loss | Shelf Life | Package |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thermization | 63 | 15 s | Psychrotrophs | Minimal | 3 d (cold) | Bulk tank |
| LTLT (Batch) | 63 | 30 min | Pathogens, *M. tb.* | Mild | 7 – 14 d | Bottles / pouches |
| HTST | 72 | 15 s | Pathogens, *C. burnetii* | Mild | 14 – 21 d | Cartons / pouches |
| HHST | 89 | 1.0 s | + Spores partly | Moderate | 30 – 45 d | Cartons |
| UHT (direct) | 140 – 150 | 2 – 5 s | All vegetative + many spores | Slight Maillard | 6 – 9 mo | Aseptic tetra-brik |
| In-container sterilization | 115 – 121 | 12 – 30 min | All incl. spores | Significant (caramel) | 6 – 12 mo | Cans / glass |

## 3.5 Mass-Balance Check on a Pasteurization Line

**Problem:** A dairy processes **20 000 L/h** of standardized milk at **4 °C** through an HTST plant. The regenerator recovers **85 %** of the heat. The steam section heats milk from **65 °C** to **72 °C** (then the holding tube). Boiler steam enters the heat exchanger at **1.5 bar (saturated, ~ 127 °C)**. Compute (a) the heat duty, (b) the steam flow, and (c) the cooling water flow in the regenerator's cold side.

**Step 1 — Heating load from 65 °C to 72 °C.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T \\
Q_1 &= \dfrac{20\,000}{3600} \times 3.93 \times (72 - 65) \\
Q_1 &= 5.556 \times 3.93 \times 7 \\
Q_1 &\approx \textbf{152.8 kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial heating from 4 °C to 65 °C (15 % of total since regenerator does 85 %).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_2 &= 5.556 \times 3.93 \times (65 - 4) \times (1 - 0.85) \\
Q_2 &= 5.556 \times 3.93 \times 61 \times 0.15 \\
Q_2 &\approx \textbf{199.8 kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total steam-side duty.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{steam}} &= Q_1 + Q_2 \approx \textbf{352.6 kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Steam consumption with latent heat $h_{fg} \approx 2\,194$ kJ/kg at 1.5 bar.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} &= \dfrac{Q_{\text{steam}}}{h_{fg}} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} &= \dfrac{352.6}{2\,194} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} &\approx \textbf{0.161 kg/s} \;\;(\approx 578 \text{ kg/h})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Regenerator heat recovery (from hot to cold side).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{regen}} &= 5.556 \times 3.93 \times 61 \times 0.85 \\
Q_{\text{regen}} &\approx \textbf{1\,132.3 kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cooling water flow in the *final cooler* (72 °C → 4 °C).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{cool}} &= 5.556 \times 3.93 \times (72 - 4) \\
Q_{\text{cool}} &\approx \textbf{1\,485 kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

For cooling water with $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\Delta T = 5$ °C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{cw}} &= \dfrac{1\,485}{4.18 \times 5} \approx \textbf{71.0 kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — HTST Pasteurization Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[RawMilkReception4C] --> A2[ChillingPlateHEX]
    A2 --> A3[QualityTestsCOBMBR]
    A3 --> A4[CentrifugalClarifier6000rpm]
    A4 --> A5[StandardizationPearsonSquare]
    A5 --> A6[Homogenizer200bar2stage]
    A6 --> B1[RegeneratorSectionColdSide4Cto65C]
    B1 --> B2[HeaterSectionSteam65Cto72C]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define pasteurization. List any two time-temperature combinations used commercially for milk pasteurization.**

**Model Answer:**

> Pasteurization is a *mild thermal process* applied to milk to destroy all pathogenic vegetative microorganisms and to inactivate most spoilage enzymes, while preserving the sensory, nutritional, and rheological qualities of milk. The official KTU definition aligns with the **FSSAI (2011) regulation**: *"pasteurization means the process of heating every particle of milk to at least 63 °C for 30 minutes (LTLT) or 72 °C for 15 seconds (HTST) or any equivalent time-temperature combination that achieves an equivalent bactericidal effect."*

Two commercial time-temperature combinations:

1. **HTST (High-Temperature Short-Time):** 72 °C for 15 seconds — used in continuous plate heat exchangers, the industry default.
2. **LTLT (Low-Temperature Long-Time / Batch):** 63 °C for 30 minutes — used in small dairies and traditional plants.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **sterilization** with **pasteurization**. Sterilization destroys *all* viable microorganisms including spores, while pasteurization destroys only *vegetative pathogens*. Writing that pasteurization "kills all bacteria" will cost you **1 mark minimum**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is homogenization? Why is two-stage homogenization preferred over single-stage in commercial milk processing?**

**Model Answer:**

> Homogenization is the mechanical process of *reducing the diameter of fat globules* in milk to less than 1 µm (typically 0.2 – 0.8 µm) by forcing the milk under high pressure (150 – 250 bar) through a narrow orifice. The fat globules are thus converted from a *coarse unstable emulsion* into a *fine permanent emulsion* that resists creaming.

Two-stage homogenization is preferred because:

1. **First stage (high pressure, e.g., 200 bar):** Performs the actual size reduction via combined mechanisms of *turbulent flow, cavitation, and shear*. It produces very fine globules but also generates re-agglomerated clusters downstream of the valve.
2. **Second stage (low pressure, e.g., 50 bar):** Breaks up the *clustered globules* formed by the first stage, producing a uniform dispersion with a narrow size distribution. This prevents the formation of a *visible fat plug* at the neck of milk bottles.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing "second stage reduces the size further" is *technically wrong*. The second stage primarily *de-agglomerates*. Always phrase it as **"break-up of clusters formed after stage 1"** to score full marks.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Either Q-A or Q-B must be answered.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Set-A]`

**Q-A (a)** Describe the complete sequence of unit operations involved in the processing of market milk, with a neat flow diagram. Highlight the role of each operation. **(7 marks)**

**Q-A (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **HTST pasteurization system**. Discuss the function of the **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** and the **regenerator section** in detail. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q-A (a)

The processing of market milk follows a **standardized nine-step sequence**:

1. **Reception and Chilling** — Milk is received in insulated tankers and cooled to ≤ 4 °C within 2 hours via a plate heat exchanger to inhibit mesophilic bacterial growth.
2. **Quality Testing** — Clot-on-boiling, alcohol (68 %), and resazurin tests are conducted; any sample failing the alcohol test is rejected.
3. **Filtration and Clarification** — In-line mesh filtration followed by centrifugal clarification at 6 000 – 8 000 rpm removes sediment and dirt.
4. **Standardization** — Fat content is adjusted (typically to 4.5 % for standardized milk) by blending whole milk and skim milk using the Pearson Square method.
5. **Homogenization** — Two-stage homogenization at 200 + 50 bar reduces fat globules to < 1 µm and prevents creaming.
6. **Pasteurization** — HTST at 72 °C for 15 s destroys pathogens.
7. **Cooling** — Immediate cooling to ≤ 4 °C prevents post-process contamination growth.
8. **Packaging** — Aseptic filling into pouches, bottles, or cartons.
9. **Cold Storage and Distribution** — Cold chain at 0 – 4 °C maintains shelf life.

(Include the flow diagram from SECTION 4.1 in the answer script.)

#### Model Solution — Q-A (b)

**HTST Pasteurization System:**

The HTST plant consists of five functional sections (refer SECTION 4.1):

| Section | Function |
|---|---|
| Cold milk balance tank + raw pump | Suction head; positive displacement pump |
| Regenerator (cold side) | Heats incoming milk from 4 °C to ~ 65 °C using the *heat of the outgoing pasteurized milk*; **recovers ~ 80 – 90 % of thermal energy** |
| Heater (steam side) | Uses boiler steam to raise milk from 65 °C to exactly 72 °C |
| Holding tube | Provides the legally required **15-second residence time** |
| Flow Diversion Valve (FDV) | Automatically diverts sub-standard milk (T < 72 °C) back to the balance tank; permits forward flow only when T ≥ 72 °C |

**Function of the FDV:** The FDV is a *pneumatically actuated three-way valve* with a temperature sensor (RTD/thermocouple) at its inlet. If the milk exiting the holding tube is below 72 °C, the FDV diverts it to the *reprocess loop*. This is the *single most critical safety device* in any pasteurizer.

**Function of the Regenerator:** A counter-flow plate heat exchanger that transfers heat from the *hot pasteurized milk* (72 °C) to the *cold incoming milk* (4 °C). The outgoing pasteurized milk leaves at ~ 18 °C and the incoming milk reaches ~ 65 °C. Heat recovery of ~ 85 % significantly reduces the plant's steam and refrigeration energy bills.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Cost: up to 2 marks):**
> 1. Forgetting to mention **"two-stage"** in the regenerator description. The milk stream is split and *re-mixed* for optimal heat transfer.
> 2. Calling the regenerator a *heater*. It is a *heat exchanger*, not a heater. The heating is done by the steam section.
> 3. Missing the **"FDV diversion logic"** — students often describe the FDV as merely a "valve" without explaining its *automatic temperature-triggered diversion* behaviour.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Set-B]`

**Q-B (a)** Explain the **Pearson Square method** for milk standardization. A dairy has **whole milk with 5.5 % fat** and **skim milk with 0.3 % fat**. Calculate the volumes of each required to obtain **800 L of standardized milk at 3.0 % fat**. **(7 marks)**

**Q-B (b)** Differentiate between **HTST, UHT, and sterilization** of milk with respect to time-temperature regime, target organisms, nutrient impact, packaging, and shelf life. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q-B (a)

**Pearson Square Method (Theory):** It is a graphical-arithmetic method used to determine the **proportions** of two components of known concentration to be mixed to obtain a product of desired intermediate concentration. For milk standardization:

- Place the *desired fat %* in the centre of a square.
- Place the *fat %* of whole milk at the top-left and *fat %* of skim milk at the bottom-left.
- Compute the diagonal *absolute differences*: $\vert F_{\text{whole}} - F_{\text{target}} \vert$ and $\vert F_{\text{skim}} - F_{\text{target}} \vert$.
- The right-hand numbers give the *parts* of the *opposite* component to be used.

**Numerical Solution:**

| | Whole (5.5 %) | Skim (0.3 %) |
|---|---|---|
| Target 3.0 % | $\vert 5.5 - 3.0 \vert = 2.5$ parts (skim) | $\vert 0.3 - 3.0 \vert = 2.7$ parts (whole) |

**Step 1 — Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Whole : Skim} = 2.7 : 2.5 = 27 : 25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of parts.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
27 + 25 = 52 \text{ parts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume per part.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{800 \text{ L}}{52} = 15.385 \text{ L per part}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Component volumes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{whole}} &= 27 \times 15.385 = \textbf{415.4 L} \\
\dot{V}_{\text{skim}} &= 25 \times 15.385 = \textbf{384.6 L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fat} &= 0.055 \times 415.4 + 0.003 \times 384.6 \\
          &= 22.85 + 1.15 = 24.00 \text{ kg} \\
\dfrac{24.00}{800} &= 0.030 = \textbf{3.0 \%} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution — Q-B (b)

| Parameter | HTST | UHT | In-Container Sterilization |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 72 °C | 135 – 150 °C | 115 – 121 °C |
| Time | 15 s | 2 – 5 s | 12 – 30 min |
| Microbial target | Vegetative pathogens (*M. tb.*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*) | All vegetative + most spores (*Bacillus*, *Clostridium* partially) | All incl. *Clostridium botulinum* spores |
| Nutrient impact | Mild (5 – 10 % loss of vit. C, B₁) | Moderate; Maillard browning possible | Significant; cooked/caramelized flavour |
| Packaging | Cold-chain-dependent pouches/cartons | Pre-sterilized 6-layer tetra-briks (aseptic) | Hermetically sealed cans/glass bottles |
| Shelf life | 14 – 21 d (4 °C) | 6 – 9 mo (ambient ≤ 25 °C) | 6 – 12 mo (ambient) |
| Regulatory | Phosphatase-negative, Peroxidase-positive | Commercial sterility, 9-log spore reduction | Commercial sterility (12-log *C. bot.* reduction) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Cost: up to 2 marks):**
> 1. Calling UHT milk "sterilized milk" — technically *correct* in industry, but for academic KTU answers, *prefer the term "commercially sterile"*.
> 2. Confusing *phosphatase* and *peroxidase* tests. Phosphatase must be **negative** (under-pasteurized = positive) and peroxidase should be **positive** (over-pasteurized = negative, which is *also undesirable*).
> 3. Missing the *Maillard reaction* note for UHT — prolonged storage at ambient temperature can cause non-enzymatic browning via lysine-lactose interaction, slightly reducing *available lysine* content.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Milk is a colloidal emulsion** of fat in water, with **mean composition 87 % water, 4.8 % lactose, 3.9 % fat, 3.4 % protein, 0.8 % ash**.
- [x] **Total Solids (TS)** ≈ 13 %; **SNF** ≈ 9 %; **Fat = TS − SNF**.
- [x] The canonical sequence is **Reception → Chilling → Testing → Clarification → Standardization → Homogenization → Pasteurization → Cooling → Packaging → Storage**.
- [x] **HTST (72 °C / 15 s)** is the industrial default. **LTLT (63 °C / 30 min)** is the legacy. **UHT (140 °C / 4 s)** gives ambient shelf life.
- [x] **Standardization** uses the **Pearson Square** with absolute differences; remember the diagonal rule.
- [x] **Homogenization** at 150 – 200 bar two-stage reduces fat globules to **< 1 µm**, preventing creaming and improving mouthfeel.
- [x] The **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** is the *single most important safety device* — automatically diverts under-heated milk.
- [x] The **regenerator** recovers **~ 85 % of thermal energy** in HTST plants, drastically reducing steam and refrigeration costs.
- [x] **Microbial inactivation** is first-order: $\log(N_0/N) = t/D_T$; **$D_T$** changes tenfold for every **$Z$** °C change.
- [x] **Phosphatase-negative** + **Peroxidase-positive** is the operational indicator of *adequate but not excessive* pasteurization.
- [x] **UHT milk** uses **aseptic Tetra Brik packaging**; the aseptic chamber is sterilized with **35 % H₂O₂** at **70 – 80 °C** and hot sterile air.
- [x] **Alkaline phosphatase** is the marker enzyme: it is *inactivated* only at temperatures *above legal pasteurization*; if it is detected in the finished product, *under-pasteurization* has occurred.
- [x] **Homogenization** is *mandatory* for *standardized* and *recombined* milk in India under FSSAI rules.
- [x] **Indian FSSAI standards for fluid milk:** Toned = 3.0 % fat / 8.5 % SNF; Standardized = 4.5 % fat / 8.5 % SNF; Full cream = 6.0 % fat / 9.0 % SNF; Double toned = 1.5 % fat / 9.0 % SNF.
- [x] **Creaming** in unhomogenized milk is the *physical* manifestation of the **Stokes' law settling** of large fat globules (ρ_fat = 0.91 g/cm³ < ρ_skim = 1.035 g/cm³).
- [x] **Cold chain integrity** of **≤ 4 °C** is *non-negotiable* for HTST pasteurized milk throughout distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always draw a **neat labelled flow diagram** with the **FDV, holding tube, regenerator, and cooler** clearly marked. The diagram is worth **2 – 3 marks** by itself. Then narrate the process step-by-step with **time-temperature data** in bold. This combination of *visual + numeric* answers consistently fetches **full 14 marks** in Module 4 ESE questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Butter Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Butter Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Butter (FSSAI / Codex Alimentarius Standard)

**Butter** is a **fatty dairy product** derived exclusively from milk, cream, or both, with a **minimum milk fat content of 80 % by weight**, a **maximum moisture content of 16 %**, and a **maximum milk solids-not-fat (SNF) content of 2 %**. It is produced by the **phase inversion of an oil-in-water emulsion (cream) into a water-in-oil emulsion (butter)** through the mechanical process of **churning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI Standard for Table Butter (FSSAI, 2011, revised 2020):**
> - Milk Fat: **Not less than 80 % by weight**
> - Moisture: **Not more than 16 % by weight**
> - Milk solids-not-fat (SNF): **Not more than 2 % by weight**
> - Food-grade salt (in salted butter): **Not more than 3 % by weight**
> - Free fatty acids (as oleic acid): **Not more than 0.4 %**

**Table Butter** (per Codex) must contain **≥ 80 % fat, ≤ 16 % water, ≤ 2 % SNF, ≤ 0.2 % starch**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a glass of fresh milk and you let it stand overnight. The lighter fat globules slowly **float to the top**, forming a thick yellow layer — this layer is **cream**. Now, imagine putting that cream in a jar and shaking it vigorously. What happens?

Think of fat globules in cream as tiny **balloons floating in water** — they have a protective protein skin (the milk fat globule membrane, MFGM). When you shake the cream:
- The balloons **bump into each other**
- Their skins **break** and they **stick together**
- They form a **solid yellow lump (butter)**
- The water (buttermilk) is **squeezed out**

This is exactly what happens in industrial **churning**. The **oil-in-water (O/W) emulsion inverts to a water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion**. Butter is essentially a **W/O emulsion** where fat is the continuous phase and tiny water droplets are dispersed in it.

### 1.3 Classification of Butter

| Type | Key Characteristic | Typical Use |
|------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Table Butter** | Unsalted, mild flavor | Spreads, baking |
| **Salted Butter** | 1.5–2.0 % salt added | Preservation, flavor |
| **Sweet Cream Butter** | From pasteurized sweet cream (pH > 6.4) | Direct consumption |
| **Cultured (Ripened) Butter** | Fermented with lactic acid bacteria (pH 4.6–5.0) | Continental Europe, premium |
| **Whipped Butter** | Air/N₂ incorporated (≥ 50 % overrun) | Spreadable form |
| **Whey Butter** | From whey cream (lower fat, strong flavor) | Industrial, bakery |
| **Ghee (Clarified Butter)** | 99.5 % fat, moisture removed | Indian subcontinent, cooking |
| **Anhydrous Milk Fat (AMF)** | ≥ 99.8 % fat | Export, recombined dairy |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The module emphasizes the **manufacturing flow**, **phase inversion mechanism**, and **process control parameters** in butter manufacture. Cultured butter is a frequently asked topic.

### 1.4 Composition of Butter (Typical Reference Values)

| Constituent | Range (% w/w) | Role |
|-------------|---------------|------|
| Milk Fat | 80.0 – 82.0 | Continuous phase, flavor carrier |
| Water (Moisture) | 15.5 – 17.0 | Dispersed phase, texture |
| SNF (curd, lactose, ash) | 0.8 – 1.5 | Body, browning |
| Salt (optional) | 0 – 2.0 | Flavor, preservation |
| Air | 1.0 – 3.0 (vol) | Texture |

### 1.5 Why Butter is a W/O Emulsion

Milk is an **O/W emulsion** — fat droplets suspended in water. Cream (40 % fat) is still O/W. During churning, when fat droplets coalesce, the **fat becomes the continuous phase** and water becomes the **dispersed phase** as tiny droplets. This is the **critical inversion point** that defines butter-making.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry Behind Butter Formation

The milk fat globule in fresh milk is surrounded by a complex, multi-layered biological membrane — the **Milk Fat Globule Membrane (MFGM)**. This membrane is a **natural emulsifier** made of:
- **Phospholipids** (e.g., sphingomyelin, phosphatidylcholine)
- **Glycoproteins** (e.g., butyrophilin, xanthine oxidase)
- **Cholesterol** and **neutral lipids**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Churning Principle (The Three Stages):**
> 1. **Foam formation** — Air is beaten in; fat globules concentrate at the air–water interface.
> 2. **Clumping / Aggregation** — The MFGM gets mechanically disrupted; globules stick via **partial coalescence** (a solid fat crystal bridges two liquid droplets).
> 3. **Phase Inversion** — At ~30 % air, O/W flips to W/O; butter granules form.

**Partial Coalescence** is the rate-limiting step — fat must be **partially crystallized** (i.e., the cream must be **tempered at 7–10 °C** for 12–24 h) so that solid fat crystals pierce the membrane of colliding globules.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Industrial Process Logic

#### Stage 1: Cream Separation
- Milk (4.5 % fat) is fed to a **centrifugal cream separator** operating at **6,000–10,000 rpm**.
- **Skim milk** (≤ 0.05 % fat) and **cream** (35–45 % fat) are obtained.
- **Separation efficiency** is critical — fat losses in skim milk reduce butter yield.

#### Stage 2: Standardization of Cream
- Cream is standardized to a target fat % (typically **35–42 % for batch churns, 40–45 % for continuous churns**).
- This is the **Pearson Square** or algebraic standardization problem.

#### Stage 3: Pasteurization
- Cream is pasteurized at:
  - **Batch**: 63 °C × 30 min (LTLT)
  - **Continuous**: 72 °C × 15 s (HTST) or 85 °C × 15 s (vacuum pasteurization)
- This destroys pathogens, inactivates lipases (prevents rancidity), and standardizes microbial load.

#### Stage 4: Aging / Tempering of Cream (Vacuum Treatment Optional)
- Cream is cooled to **4–7 °C** and held for **12–24 hours** with intermittent gentle agitation.
- **Purpose**: Achieve proper **crystallization of triglycerides** → correct ratio of solid-to-liquid fat → best churning behavior.
- **Vacuum pasteurization** (80–95 °C under vacuum) deaerates the cream, eliminates "fishy" oxidized flavors, and improves body.

#### Stage 5: Churning
- **Batch churn**: Cream churned in rotary churns at 8–12 rpm; takes **30–45 min**.
- **Continuous churn** (e.g., **Westfalia, Alfa Laval, Fritz**): Uses high-speed beaters (Gusterson, Anderson) — completes in **1–3 seconds**.

**Churn temperature**: **8–13 °C** (sweet cream butter); lower for cultured cream (pH < 4.6 lowers fat melting point, so cooler temps needed).

#### Stage 6: Separation of Buttermilk
- Buttermilk = 0.4–0.7 % fat; rest is water, lactose, casein.
- For every **100 kg cream (40 % fat)**, ~**50 kg butter + 50 kg buttermilk** is obtained.

#### Stage 7: Washing
- Butter granules are washed with **potable chilled water (1–3 °C)** to:
  - Remove residual buttermilk sugars (prevent fermentation defects)
  - Adjust final moisture pH and salt distribution
- 1–2 washes are typical.

#### Stage 8: Salting (Optional)
- Salt added as **brine (saturated salt solution)** or **dry salt**.
- Typical dose: **1.5–2.0 %** in finished butter.
- Salt enhances flavor and extends shelf life by lowering water activity (a<sub>w</sub>).

#### Stage 9: Working / Kneading
- Butter is mechanically worked to:
  - **Disperse moisture** as fine droplets (< 10 µm ideal; > 20 µm causes microbial spoilage)
  - **Distribute salt** uniformly
  - Achieve **plastic, smooth, waxy texture**
- Working intensity: 30–50 revolutions in a batch churn; controlled in continuous churns (e.g., GEA Westfalia uses a **Buhler-Wenger** or **MDF – Multi-Dynamic Flow** working section).

#### Stage 10: Packaging
- Packed in **aluminum foil-lined parchment**, **PE tubs**, or **bulk containers**.
- Storage: **≤ –18 °C** for long-term (6–12 months); **4 °C** for short term (4–8 weeks).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Standardization of Cream** (algebraic) | $C_c \times V_c = C_m \times V_m + C_f \times V_f$ | Mass balance for fat |
| 2 | **Pearson Square (mass basis)** | $\dfrac{\text{Higher \%} - \text{Target \%}}{\text{Target \%} - \text{Lower \%}} = \dfrac{\text{parts of lower}}{\text{parts of higher}}$ | Quick mix-proportion method |
| 3 | **Cream Separator Efficiency** | $\eta_{sep} = \dfrac{F_{in} - F_{skim}}{F_{in}} \times 100$ | % of fat removed into cream |
| 4 | **Butter Yield (theoretical, 100 % fat recovery)** | $Y = M_{cream} \times f_{cream}$ | where $f_{cream}$ = fat fraction in cream (decimal) |
| 5 | **Butter Yield (practical, accounting for losses)** | $Y_{practical} = \dfrac{M_{cream} \times f_{cream} \times R}{0.80}$ | R = fat recovery factor (0.92–0.96) |
| 6 | **Fat Recovery (churning efficiency)** | $R = \dfrac{Fat_{butter}}{Fat_{cream}} \times 100$ | typically 92–96 % |
| 7 | **Overrun (continuous process)** | $OR = \dfrac{M_{butter} - M_{fat}}{M_{fat}} \times 100$ | Measures moisture + air |
| 8 | **Moisture content (Karl Fischer / oven)** | $M\% = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | % water in butter |
| 9 | **Salt in Moisture Phase (SMP)** | $SMP = \dfrac{\%\,salt}{\%\,moisture} \times 100$ | Critical for flavor & preservation |
| 10 | **Water Activity (approx.)** | $a_w = 0.96 - 0.0065 \times SMP$ | For salted butter |
| 11 | **Air Incorporation** | $\%\,air = \dfrac{V_{butter} - V_{fat+water}}{V_{butter}} \times 100$ | Should be ≤ 3 % by vol |
| 12 | **Emulsion Inversion Critical Point** | $\phi_{fat} \approx 0.74$ (random close packing) | Geometric inversion condition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight**: The 80 % fat target is **not arbitrary** — it is the **minimum continuous-phase concentration** needed for fat to fully envelop water droplets and prevent them from re-merging into skim milk. Below ~70 % fat, the system reverts to O/W.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Optimization**: Overrun directly determines **plant profitability** — +1 % overrun ≈ +1 % extra product from the same cream.
- **Shelf-life control**: SMP < 15 % is the most important hurdle against *Pseudomonas* and *lipolytic* spoilage.
- **Energy efficiency**: Vacuum pasteurization saves 25–30 % steam vs. HTST for cream.
- **Cold-chain design**: The 7–10 °C tempering window dictates chiller load and tank size.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Standardization of Cream Using Pearson Square

**Problem:** You have **1,000 kg** of cream at **45 % fat** and **skim milk at 0.05 % fat**. You want to standardize the cream to **40 % fat** for butter making. How much skim milk must be added?

**Pearson Square Setup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Top-left:} \quad & 45.00 \quad \text{(high)} \\
\text{Center:} \quad & 40.00 \quad \text{(target)} \\
\text{Bottom-left:} \quad & 0.05 \quad \text{(low)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute differences (cross-subtract on the diagonal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parts of low (skim milk)} &= 45.00 - 40.00 = 5.00 \\
\text{Parts of high (cream)} &= 40.00 - 0.05 = 39.95 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio:**

$$
\text{Skim milk} : \text{Cream} = 5.00 : 39.95 \approx 1 : 7.99
$$

**Step 3 — Total parts:**

$$
T_{parts} = 5.00 + 39.95 = 44.95
$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{skim} &= \dfrac{5.00}{44.95} \times 1000 = 111.24 \;\text{kg skim milk} \\
M_{cream} &= \dfrac{39.95}{44.95} \times 1000 = 888.76 \;\text{kg cream} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass balance verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fat in} &= (888.76 \times 0.45) + (111.24 \times 0.0005) \\
&= 399.94 + 0.056 \\
&= 400.00 \;\text{kg fat} \\
\text{Total mass} &= 888.76 + 111.24 = 1000 \;\text{kg} \\
\text{Target fat \%} &= \dfrac{400.00}{1000} \times 100 = 40.00 \,\% \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **[Result]: Add 111.24 kg of skim milk to 888.76 kg of cream to obtain 1,000 kg of standardized 40 % fat cream.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical and Practical Butter Yield

**Given:**
- Cream mass: $M_{cream} = 1{,}000\;\text{kg}$
- Cream fat content: $f_{cream} = 0.40$ (40 %)
- Target butter composition: 80 % fat, 16 % moisture, 2 % SNF, 2 % salt
- Fat recovery in churning: $R = 0.95$ (95 %)
- Loss in buttermilk: rest of the fat

**Step 1 — Total fat in cream:**

$$
F_{cream} = M_{cream} \times f_{cream} = 1000 \times 0.40 = 400\;\text{kg fat}
$$

**Step 2 — Fat recovered in butter (theoretical maximum at R = 1):**

$$
F_{butter, ideal} = F_{cream} \times R = 400 \times 0.95 = 380\;\text{kg fat}
$$

**Step 3 — Butter yield (mass) from fat content:**

Since finished butter is **80 % fat**, the total mass of butter is:

$$
Y_{practical} = \dfrac{F_{butter, ideal}}{0.80} = \dfrac{380}{0.80} = 475\;\text{kg butter}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass balance — what is the buttermilk?**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{buttermilk} &= M_{cream} - Y_{practical} \\
&= 1000 - 475 = 525\;\text{kg buttermilk}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify buttermilk fat content:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{buttermilk} &= F_{cream} - F_{butter, ideal} = 400 - 380 = 20\;\text{kg} \\
f_{buttermilk} &= \dfrac{20}{525} \times 100 = 3.81 \,\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this is unusually high. Real buttermilk has **< 0.7 % fat**; the 3.81 % above means we used an overly conservative recovery. Let's re-run with $R = 0.998$ (typical industrial):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{butter, real} &= 400 \times 0.998 = 399.2\;\text{kg} \\
F_{buttermilk} &= 0.8\;\text{kg} \\
f_{buttermilk} &= \dfrac{0.8}{600.8} \times 100 \approx 0.133\,\% \;\;\text{(reasonable)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Industrially, **R must be ≥ 0.995** to keep buttermilk fat < 0.5 % and butter yield close to the theoretical 500 kg.

**Step 6 — Calculate Overrun:**

Overrun is the extra mass in butter **attributable to non-fat constituents** (moisture + air + salt + SNF) relative to pure fat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{fat} &= 400\;\text{kg (input)} \\
M_{butter} &= 500\;\text{kg (output at 80\% fat)} \\
OR &= \dfrac{500 - 400}{400} \times 100 = 25\,\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Typical Value**: Overrun for sweet cream butter is **18–25 %**. A negative or low overrun signals **excess fat loss** in buttermilk (poor churn operation).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Salt-in-Moisture Phase (SMP) Analysis

**Given butter composition:**
- Salt: 1.6 % w/w
- Moisture: 16.0 % w/w

**Step 1 — Compute SMP (the actual salt concentration in the water phase, which is what microbes and taste buds "feel"):**

$$
SMP = \dfrac{\%\,salt}{\%\,moisture} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.6}{16.0} \times 100 = 10.0\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with threshold values:**

| SMP Range | Sensory / Microbial Effect |
|-----------|---------------------------|
| < 8 % | Bland taste |
| 10 – 14 % | **Optimal flavor & preservation** |
| > 18 % | Salty, inhibitory to desirable lactics |

**Step 3 — Calculate a<sub>w</sub> (approximate):**

$$
a_w = 0.96 - 0.0065 \times SMP = 0.96 - 0.0065 \times 10 = 0.895
$$

This $a_w$ is below the threshold for *Salmonella* and most spoilage moulds (which need $a_w > 0.94$).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Cream Separator Efficiency

**Given:**
- Whole milk inflow: $F_{in}$ = 5,000 kg/h
- Fat in milk: $C_{in}$ = 4.5 %
- Skim milk outflow: $F_{out}$ = 4,200 kg/h
- Fat in skim milk: $C_{out}$ = 0.05 %

**Step 1 — Fat balance check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{in} \times C_{in} &= F_{out} \times C_{out} + F_{cream} \times C_{cream} \\
5000 \times 0.045 &= 4200 \times 0.0005 + F_{cream} \times C_{cream} \\
225 &= 2.1 + F_{cream} \times C_{cream} \\
F_{cream} \times C_{cream} &= 222.9\;\text{kg fat/h} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Separation efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{sep} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{4.5 - 0.05}{4.5} \times 100 = 98.89\,\%
$$

This is a **very high-efficiency separator** — typical industrial separators achieve **95–99 %**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Variables vs. Butter Quality

| Variable | Low / Sub-optimal | Optimal | High / Sub-optimal |
|----------|-------------------|---------|---------------------|
| Churn temp (sweet cream) | < 6 °C → hard, oily butter | **8 – 13 °C** | > 15 °C → soft, leaky |
| Aging time | < 4 h → incomplete crystallization | **12 – 24 h** | > 36 h → lipolysis |
| Working intensity | Under-worked → free moisture | **30–50 rev** | Over-worked → greasy, soft |
| Moisture | < 15 % → crumbly, hard spread | **15.5 – 16 %** | > 16.5 % → microbiological risk |
| Salt | < 0.5 % → bland | **1.5 – 2.0 %** | > 3 % → regulatory breach |
| Fat in cream | < 30 % → low overrun | **38 – 45 %** | > 50 % → difficult churning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Butter Manufacturing Flow (Batch Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    milkA[Whole Milk Reception 4.5 percent Fat] --> milkB[Preheating to 50 to 55 deg C]
    milkB --> sepA[Centrifugal Cream Separator 6000 to 10000 rpm]
    sepA --> skimA[Skim Milk 0.05 percent Fat]
    sepA --> creamA[Cream 35 to 45 percent Fat]
    creamA --> stdA[Standardization to 40 percent Fat]
    stdA --> pasteA[HTST Pasteurization 72 deg C x 15 s]
    pasteA --> vacA[Optional Vacuum Pasteurization 80 to 95 deg C]
    vacA --> ageA[Aging and Tempering 4 to 7 deg C for 12 to 24 h]
    ageA --> churnA[Churning in Rotary Churn 8 to 12 rpm x 30 to 45 min]
    churnA --> butterA[Butter Granules Formed]
    churnA --> buttMilkA[Buttermilk Out 0.4 to 0.7 percent Fat]
    butterA --> washA[Washing with Chilled Water 1 to 3 deg C]
    washA --> saltA[Optional Salting Brine or Dry Salt 1.5 to 2.0 percent]
    saltA --> workA[Working and Kneading 30 to 50 revolutions]
    workA --> packA[Packaging Alu Foil or PE Tubs]
    packA --> coldA[Cold Storage minus 18 deg C long term or 4 deg C short term]
```

### 4.2 Continuous Butter Making Process (Alfa Laval / Westfalia Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1[Standardized Cream 40 to 45 percent] --> c2[Vacuum Pasteurizer 80 to 95 deg C]
    c2 --> c3[Deaeration Chamber]
    c3 --> c4[Aging Section 4 to 7 deg C]
    c4 --> c5[High Speed Beater Churn 1500 to 3000 rpm]
    c5 --> c6[Butter Grains plus Buttermilk Outlet]
    c6 --> c7[Separating Sieve]
    c7 --> c8[Buttermilk Exit]
    c7 --> c9[Working Section MDF or Buhler Wenger]
    c9 --> c10[Brine Injection]
    c10 --> c11[Final Working and Texture Control]
    c11 --> c12[Packaging Unit]
```

### 4.3 Phase Inversion Mechanism (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[Stage 1 O over W Emulsion Cream] --> a2[Stage 2 Air Beaten In 30 percent Air]
    a2 --> a3[Stage 3 Partial Coalescence Fat Crystals Pierce MFGM]
    a3 --> a4[Stage 4 Critical Inversion phi fat approx 0.74]
    a4 --> a5[Stage 5 W over O Emulsion Butter]
    a5 --> a6[Stage 6 Granule Aggregation and Free Buttermilk Release]
```

### 4.4 Batch Churn Internal Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    b1[Cream Inlet Port] --> b2[Internal Baffle System]
    b2 --> b3[Rotary Paddle Assembly 8 to 12 rpm]
    b3 --> b4[Air Vent with Check Valve]
    b3 --> b5[Chamber Wall Jacketed for Temperature Control]
    b5 --> b6[Chilled Water Circulation 1 to 5 deg C]
    b3 --> b7[Butter Granule Discharge Sieve]
    b7 --> b8[Buttermilk Drain Valve]
    b7 --> b9[Working Blades Post Churn]
    b9 --> b10[Butter Discharge Outlet]
    b10 --> b11[Manual Salt Addition Tray]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define butter as per FSSAI standards. List its four essential compositional requirements.**

**Model Answer:**
Butter is a fatty dairy product exclusively derived from milk and/or cream.
**FSSAI compositional requirements:**
- (i) Milk fat ≥ 80 % w/w
- (ii) Moisture ≤ 16 % w/w
- (iii) Milk SNF ≤ 2 % w/w
- (iv) Salt (salted) ≤ 3 % w/w; FFA as oleic acid ≤ 0.4 %

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is phase inversion? How does it occur during butter manufacture?**

**Model Answer:**
Phase inversion is the conversion of an **oil-in-water (O/W) emulsion** (cream) into a **water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion** (butter). It occurs during churning when:
- (i) Air is whipped into cream (≈ 30 % by volume).
- (ii) Fat globules are concentrated at air–water interface; their MFGM is disrupted.
- (iii) **Partial coalescence** — solid fat crystals bridge globules.
- (iv) At φ<sub>fat</sub> ≈ 0.74, fat becomes the continuous phase and water becomes the dispersed droplets.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step industrial manufacture of **cultured (ripened) butter**, with a clear flow diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **theoretical and practical yield of butter** from **5,000 kg of cream at 38 % fat**, given that churning recovers **94 % of the fat** and the finished butter contains **82 % fat, 16 % moisture, 2 % salt**. Also calculate the **% overrun** and the **fat content of the buttermilk** produced. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Cultured Butter Manufacture**

1. **Cream separation** → 38–42 % fat cream.
2. **Pasteurization** (HTST 72 °C × 15 s) — destroys competing flora.
3. **Cooling to inoculation temperature** (18–22 °C).
4. **Inoculation with starter culture** (*Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis*, *L. lactis* subsp. *cremoris*, *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* subsp. *cremoris*) at **1–2 %**.
5. **Ripening for 12–16 h** until pH falls to **4.6 – 4.8** → lactic acid & diacetyl produced.
6. **Cooling to churning temperature (8–12 °C)** — this also reduces fat melting point (lower for acidic cream).
7. **Churning** (gentler than for sweet cream; pH drops fat MFFT).
8. **Draining buttermilk** (now a high-acid buttermilk).
9. **Washing** (optional; washing removes acid → milder flavor).
10. **Salting** (1.5–2.0 %).
11. **Working** and **packaging**.

> Cultured butter has a **tangy aroma** from lactic acid and **nutty/butterscotch notes** from diacetyl — characteristic of European "beurre de baratte".

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Step 1 — Fat input:**

$$
F_{in} = 5000 \times 0.38 = 1900\;\text{kg fat}
$$

**Step 2 — Fat recovered in butter:**

$$
F_{butter} = 1900 \times 0.94 = 1786\;\text{kg fat}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical yield (100 % fat recovery, 80 % fat target):**

$$
Y_{theo} = \dfrac{1900}{0.80} = 2375\;\text{kg butter}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical yield (94 % recovery, 82 % fat target):**

$$
Y_{practical} = \dfrac{1786}{0.82} = 2178.05\;\text{kg butter}
$$

**Step 5 — Buttermilk fat content:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{buttermilk} &= 1900 - 1786 = 114\;\text{kg fat} \\
M_{buttermilk} &= 5000 - 2178.05 = 2821.95\;\text{kg} \\
f_{buttermilk} &= \dfrac{114}{2821.95} \times 100 = 4.04\,\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note**: The 4.04 % is high because we used a *deliberately low* recovery (94 %). In industry, recovery is 99.5 %, so buttermilk fat is < 0.5 %.

**Step 6 — Overrun:**

$$
OR = \dfrac{Y_{practical} - F_{in}}{F_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2178.05 - 1900}{1900} \times 100 = 14.6\,\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Yield Calculations:**
> - **Confusing 80 % and the actual fat %** of the target butter. Use the **actual finished fat %** (82 % here), not the standard 80 %.
> - **Ignoring practical recovery** — theoretical vs. practical differ by ~5–8 %.
> - **Not verifying buttermilk fat** — examiners award 1 mark just for a sanity check.
> - **Off-by-100 error** — fat content must be used as **decimal (0.38)** in mass-balance equations.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **role of milk fat globule membrane (MFGM)** in cream, and the mechanism of **partial coalescence** during churning. How does **cream aging/tempering** influence butter texture? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy plant standardizes **3,000 kg of cream at 45 % fat** to **40 % fat** using skim milk (0.05 % fat). Calculate: (i) mass of skim milk to add, (ii) mass of standardized cream, (iii) the **practical butter yield** assuming 96 % churning recovery and 80 % fat target. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MFGM, Partial Coalescence, and Cream Aging**

- **MFGM**: A tri-layer biological membrane (proteins, phospholipids, glycolipids, cholesterol) that **stabilizes the O/W emulsion** in fresh milk/cream by preventing fat globule coalescence.
- **Partial coalescence mechanism**:
  - During aging, triglycerides partially crystallize — solid fat crystals form within globules.
  - Churning introduces air and turbulence; globules collide.
  - A **solid crystal** from one globule **pierces** the MFGM of another → they share the membrane and stick.
  - This is **partial** (not full) coalescence — the original fat globule is not fully broken, just bonded.
  - Repeating → **fat aggregates (butter grains) → clumps → phase inversion → butter**.
- **Cream aging**:
  - Done at **4–7 °C for 12–24 h** with gentle agitation.
  - Establishes correct **solid:liquid fat ratio** (typically 30–35 % solid at churning temperature).
  - If aging is **insufficient** → too much liquid fat → butter is greasy and soft; if **over-aged** → lipolysis (rancidity) and crystallization defects.

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Step 1 — Pearson Square differences:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parts of skim milk} &= 45 - 40 = 5 \\
\text{Parts of cream} &= 40 - 0.05 = 39.95 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total parts:**

$$
T = 5 + 39.95 = 44.95
$$

**Step 3 — Skim milk required:**

$$
M_{skim} = \dfrac{5}{44.95} \times 3000 = 333.70\;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of standardized cream:**

$$
M_{std} = 3000 + 333.70 = 3333.70\;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Fat in standardized cream:**

$$
F = 3333.70 \times 0.40 = 1333.48\;\text{kg fat}
$$

**Step 6 — Fat recovered:**

$$
F_{recovered} = 1333.48 \times 0.96 = 1280.14\;\text{kg fat}
$$

**Step 7 — Practical butter yield (80 % fat target):**

$$
Y_{practical} = \dfrac{1280.14}{0.80} = 1600.18\;\text{kg butter}
$$

> **[Result]**: Add 333.70 kg skim milk → 3,333.70 kg standardized cream → **1,600 kg butter** at 96 % recovery.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Common Mark-Loss Areas in Butter Processing Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize the cream** before yield calculations — examiners explicitly test Pearson Square.
> 2. **Confusing O/W and W/O** — butter is **W/O**, cream is **O/W**. Wrong direction → 2 marks lost in (a)-part.
> 3. **Skipping the role of MFGM** — the **partial coalescence** mechanism is worth 2–3 marks by itself. Do NOT say "fat breaks" — say "MFGM is disrupted and partial coalescence occurs".
> 4. **Not mentioning the 80 % minimum fat rule** of FSSAI in definition questions.
> 5. **Ignoring the buttermilk fat balance** in numerical problems — it is a guaranteed 1-mark sub-question.
> 6. **Forgetting continuous churn process names** (e.g., Westfalia, Alfa Laval) in 14-mark descriptive answers — examiners expect at least **one** industrial process name.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: Butter = ≥ 80 % milk fat, ≤ 16 % moisture, ≤ 2 % SNF (FSSAI).
- **Butter is a W/O emulsion** formed by **phase inversion** of cream (O/W) during churning.
- **MFGM disruption + partial coalescence** is the molecular mechanism of butter formation.
- **Churning temperature**: 8–13 °C for sweet cream; cooler for cultured cream (pH effect).
- **Aging/Tempering** at 4–7 °C for 12–24 h → controls fat crystallization → controls body.
- **Working** disperses moisture to < 10 µm droplets and distributes salt uniformly.
- **Salt-in-Moisture Phase (SMP)** is the real concentration of salt in butter — keep at **10–14 %** for optimal flavor & safety.
- **Overrun** for sweet cream butter: **18–25 %**; for cultured butter: **lower (12–18 %)** due to higher fat retention.
- **Fat recovery** in churning should exceed **99 %** industrially.
- **Two manufacturing routes**:
  - **Batch** — traditional wooden/metal churn; 30–45 min cycle.
  - **Continuous** — Westfalia / Alfa Laval / Fritz; cycle 1–3 s; uses vacuum pasteurizer + deaerator + high-speed beater.
- **Cultured butter** uses lactic acid bacteria ripening to pH 4.6–4.8 — gives diacetyl and lactic acid flavor.
- **Defects** to know: **leaky butter** (over-warm churn), **crumbly butter** (over-aged, too cold), **greasy butter** (under-aged, high liquid fat), **rancid butter** (lipolysis), **fishy butter** (high copper in cream, oxidation).
- **Ghee** = clarified butter (≥ 99.5 % fat) — water removed by heating until 110–120 °C; "ready" when crackling stops.
- **Anhydrous Milk Fat (AMF)** = ≥ 99.8 % fat, made by emulsification–centrifugation (Amber Process) — major export product from India.
- **FSSAI standards** must be quoted in definition; **Codex** standards in international questions.
- **Pearson Square** is the **go-to** for standardization — practice it; it appears in **every** KTU numerical paper.
- **Phase inversion φ ≈ 0.74** is a useful conceptual benchmark — never quote it for cream (cream φ ≈ 0.40), only for the inversion point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacture of Ice Cream
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT747 - FOOD PROCESSING AND TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manufacture of Ice Cream

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Ice Cream** is a frozen dairy dessert manufactured by freezing a pasteurized mix of dairy ingredients (milk fat, milk solids-not-fat), sugar, stabilizers, emulsifiers, and water, with simultaneous agitation (scrape-surface freezing) that incorporates a controlled amount of air (overrun) to produce a smooth, semi-solid foam with a characteristic texture and overrun typically ranging from **70% to 100%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim-worthy):**
> Ice cream is a frozen emulsion–foam system consisting of:
> - A discontinuous fat phase (globules)
> - A continuous aqueous phase (serum with sugars, proteins, salts)
> - An ice phase (dispersed crystals)
> - An air phase (incorporated overrun)

### Standard Legal Composition (FSSR/FDA – India)
| Component | Range (wt %) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Milk Fat | **≥ 10.0%** |
| Milk Solids-Not-Fat (MSNF) | **≥ 3.5%** (FSSR min.) |
| Total Solids | **≥ 36.0%** |
| Sucrose / Sugar | **≥ 10.0%** |
| Stabilizer | ≤ 0.5% (gelatin, CMC, guar, carrageenan) |
| Emulsifier | ≤ 0.3% (mono-/diglycerides, lecithin) |

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Whipped Frozen Cake Frosting"**
> Imagine making a thick **cake frosting** by whipping cold cream with sugar until it's light and fluffy. Now imagine **freezing that fluffy frosting** while continuing to whip it so air bubbles stay trapped inside the ice crystals. That, in essence, is ice cream: a **frozen foam**, where:
> - The **cream** is your milk fat emulsion,
> - The **sugar** keeps it soft (sweet depression of freezing point),
> - The **whipping** introduces air (overrun),
> - The **freezing** locks everything into a smooth semi-solid structure.

### Why This Topic Matters in KTU
- A frequent **14-mark Module 4 question** in ESE (End Semester Examination).
- Combines concepts of **heat transfer, emulsions, foams, and crystallization** in a single product.
- Tests the student's ability to **draw process flow diagrams** and perform **mix formulation calculations** (Pearson's Square, Overrun).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Role of Each Ingredient

| Ingredient | Functional Role in Ice Cream | Typical Quantity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Milk Fat** | Texture, creaminess, flavor carrier, body, structure (partial coalescence) | **10 – 16 %** |
| **MSNF** (proteins, lactose, minerals) | Whey proteins stabilize the foam; caseins aid emulsification; lactose gives body | **9 – 12 %** |
| **Sugar** (sucrose / glucose / corn syrup) | Sweetness, depresses freezing point → smoother texture; influences hardness | **12 – 16 %** |
| **Stabilizers** (gelatin, CMC, guar gum, carrageenan, LBG) | Bind free water, retard ice crystal growth, prevent lactose crystallization, improve melt-down | **0.2 – 0.5 %** |
| **Emulsifiers** (mono-/diglycerides, lecithin, polysorbate-80) | Reduce interfacial tension, enable partial coalescence of fat, improve whipping, dryness | **0.1 – 0.3 %** |
| **Water** | Continuous phase; solvent for sugars, salts, stabilizers | **55 – 64 %** |
| **Air** (incorporated as overrun) | Lightness, body, palatability, thermal insulation during heat shock | **50 – 100 % overrun** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Students MUST memorize that *stabilizers* prevent ice crystal growth and *emulsifiers* control fat destabilization (partial coalescence). Examiner's commonly test this contrast.

### 2.2 Ice Cream as a Multi-Phase System

The product is **simultaneously**:
1. An **emulsion** (fat-in-water)
2. A **suspension** (ice crystals + fat globules in serum)
3. A **foam** (air cells in liquid matrix)
4. A **gel/solution** (stabilizer-bound water + sugars)

The fat globule surface is coated with **casein + absorbed emulsifier + stabilizer matrix**, which is critical for partial coalescence during freezing.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently asked in KTU ESE numericals** under Module 4.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Overrun (Volume basis)** | $\%OR_v = \dfrac{V_{ic} - V_{mix}}{V_{mix}} \times 100$ | % ; $V_{ic}$ = volume of ice cream, $V_{mix}$ = volume of mix |
| 2 | **Overrun (Weight basis)** | $\%OR_w = \dfrac{W_{mix} - W_{ic}}{W_{ic}} \times 100$ | % ; weight decreases because air adds volume without mass |
| 3 | **Density of Ice Cream** | $\rho_{ic} = \dfrac{W_{ic}}{V_{ic}}$ | g/mL or kg/L |
| 4 | **Mix to Ice Cream Conversion** | $W_{ic} = W_{mix} \left( \dfrac{100}{100 + \%OR} \right)$ | kg |
| 5 | **Pearson's Square (Standardization)** | Parts of lower $\div$ Parts of higher = desired ratio | Use for fat or MSNF |
| 6 | **Freezing Point Depression (sugar)** | $\Delta T_f \propto$ molality of sucrose | Used to calculate hardness |
| 7 | **Heat Load in Hardening** | $Q = m \cdot L_f$ where $L_f = 333$ kJ/kg | Latent heat of fusion of water |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ⚠️ **LaTeX Note:** In the formula table above, absolute-value bars are written as $\vert x \vert$ — never use the literal pipe `|` symbol inside markdown tables, as it breaks rendering.

### 2.4 Overrun — The Most-Tested Concept

**Definition (KTU-style):** Overrun is the *percentage increase in volume* of an ice cream mix due to the incorporation of air during the freezing process. It is the **key quality parameter** that distinguishes a dense "premium" ice cream from a light "low-overrun" or economy product.

**Engineering Significance:**
- **Too low overrun (< 50 %)** → heavy, icy, cold-sensation dominates.
- **Too high overrun (> 120 %)** → fluffy, "fluffy-melt," poor heat-shock resistance.
- **Optimum:** 70–100 % for most commercial ice creams.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Complete Manufacturing Process (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sequence below is a **KTU Board favorite**. In a 14-mark question, examiners expect each step with **its purpose + the standard operating conditions**.

#### Step 1 — Receipt & Selection of Raw Materials
- Fresh whole milk, cream, skim milk, sugar, stabilizer, emulsifier, flavor, color.
- Quality check: acidity (lactic % < 0.18 %), fat %, SNF %, bacterial count.

#### Step 2 — Standardization of Milk (Pearson's Square)

**Numerical Example:** Standardize milk to **10 % fat** using cream (25 % fat) and skim milk (0.5 % fat).

Using **Pearson's Square**, place desired fat at the centre, source fats on the corners:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Higher fat corner (Cream)} = 25 \% \\
&\text{Desired} = 10 \% \\
&\text{Lower fat corner (Skim)} = 0.5 \% \\
&\text{Difference (higher side)} = 25 - 10 = 15 \text{ parts of skim milk} \\
&\text{Difference (lower side)} = 10 - 0.5 = 9.5 \text{ parts of cream}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio** = Skim Milk : Cream = 15 : 9.5 → equivalently 1.579 : 1

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Fat} &= \dfrac{(15 \times 0.5) + (9.5 \times 25)}{15 + 9.5} \\
&= \dfrac{7.5 + 237.5}{24.5} \\
&= \dfrac{245}{24.5} \\
&= 10.0 \ \% \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$
